topics.title: Diversion Head Works: Layout and Functions of Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diversion Head Works: Layout and Functions of Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Diversion Head Works (DHW)** is a hydraulic structure constructed across a river or stream to raise the water level and divert the flow into a conveyance system (typically an irrigation canal or power channel) at the off-take point. It serves as the critical interface between the natural river regime and the engineered distribution network, regulating the abstraction of water while simultaneously managing the river's sediment load, flood discharges, and ecological flow requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A diversion headwork is defined as a small barrage or weir constructed across a perennial river to raise the water level upstream so that the diverted water can be fed into the off-taking canal by gravity flow. It is distinguished from a storage dam by the fact that it does not create a large reservoir; it merely causes a local afflux (raising) of the water level.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sink with a partially open drain**. The drain pipe is at a lower level, and you place a small plastic barrier (like a check dam) inside the sink. The water level behind the barrier rises slightly, allowing you to easily scoop water from the elevated side using a small bucket and channel it into a separate container. 

The **plastic barrier** = **Weir/Barrage** (raises water level).
The **raised water level** = **Pond/Pocket upstream**.
The **small bucket channel** = **Off-taking Canal**.
The **separate container** = **Irrigation field/distribution system**.

> [!NOTE]
> A diversion headwork is essentially the "kitchen sink dam" of river engineering ŌĆö it diverts, it doesn't store extensively. The afflux created is typically between **1.0 m and 3.0 m** for most small to medium headworks.

### 1.3 Classification of Diversion Headworks

Diversion head works are broadly classified into two fundamental types based on their hydraulic behavior and structural configuration:

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Weir (with shutters)** | A low-crest masonry/concrete barrier with adjustable gates/shutters raised on the crest. Afflux is limited; shutters can be added during floods. | Small to medium rivers with moderate discharge (< 3000 cumecs) |
| **Barrage (with gates)** | A gated structure with a depressed crest and a high downstream floor (apron). Gates are opened to pass floods. Afflux is small and controlled. | Large rivers with high discharge and high flood intensity |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**W**eir = **W**all" ŌĆö a weir is essentially a raised wall across the river, while a barrage is a **gated depression** with a robust floor.

### 1.4 Necessity and Purpose

A diversion headwork is constructed for the following engineered purposes:

1. **To raise the water level** at the off-take point so that the command area can be irrigated by gravity flow.
2. **To regulate the intake** of water into the canal through the head regulator.
3. **To control the entry of sediment** into the canal through silt excluders and ejectors.
4. **To ensure a minimum ecological flow** downstream during non-monsoon periods.
5. **To pass the design flood safely** without causing damage to the structure or surrounding floodplain.
6. **To control the river regime** (alignment, scour, and deposition) in the vicinity of the off-take.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Site Selection Criteria for Diversion Headworks

The selection of an appropriate site for constructing a diversion headwork is governed by both **hydrological** and **topographical** considerations. KTU examiners frequently test this aspect.

- **River Regime:** The site should preferably be on a straight reach of the river to avoid curved-flow-induced sediment entry.
- **Geology & Soil:** Firm and impervious foundation soil is essential to control seepage and prevent piping failure.
- **Adequate Command:** The site must be located at an elevation that can command the largest possible area by gravity.
- **Stable Banks:** Well-defined, stable, and high banks are preferred to reduce the length of marginal embankments.
- **Absence of Confluences:** The site should be away from the confluence of tributaries, which may cause erratic flow and sediment transport.
- **Construction Material:** Availability of stone, aggregate, and labor within economic distance.

### 2.2 Layout of a Diversion Headwork

The complete layout of a typical diversion headwork, extending from upstream to downstream, consists of the following sequenced components:

**A. Upstream Components:**
1. **Approach Channel** ŌĆö The natural river reach leading up to the headwork.
2. **Marginal Bunds (Left & Right)** ŌĆö Embankments constructed along the river banks upstream to confine the river flow and protect low-lying areas from submergence during floods.
3. **Groyne (Upstream)** ŌĆö A transverse structure projecting from the bank into the river to train the flow and protect the bank from erosion.
4. **Divide Wall (Groyne Wall)** ŌĆö A masonry/concrete wall constructed perpendicular to the weir axis to separate the weir proper from the under-sluices and to prevent cross-flow turbulence from adversely affecting the off-take.

**B. Main Structure Components:**
5. **Weir Proper / Main Weir** ŌĆö The main overflow section across the river that maintains the design pond level.
6. **Under-Sluices (Scouring Sluices)** ŌĆö A low-lying gated section adjacent to the canal off-take, used to:
    - Flush out the sediment deposited in front of the head regulator.
    - Maintain a deep channel (thalweg) in front of the off-take.
7. **Head Regulator** ŌĆö A gated structure at the off-take point that controls the discharge entering the canal. It is normally smaller in width than the canal.
8. **Fish Ladder** ŌĆö A stepped or baffled channel provided for the migration of fish upstream and downstream.

**C. Downstream Components:**
9. **Talus / Downstream Apron** ŌĆö An inclined or horizontal impervious floor downstream of the weir to resist the high-velocity flow and prevent scour.
10. **Launching Apron** ŌĆö A loosely packed stone apron at the downstream end of the talus, which "launches" or settles into a scour pit if it forms.
11. **Cut-Off Walls (Upstream and Downstream)** ŌĆö Vertical walls extending below the floor to intercept seepage flow and increase the creep length.

**D. Sediment Control Structures:**
12. **Silt Excluder** ŌĆö A structure located in the under-sluice pocket that excludes coarse sediment from entering the canal.
13. **Silt Ejector (Silt Extractor)** ŌĆö A structure located in the canal itself (a few hundred meters downstream of the head regulator) that ejects the sediment deposited in the canal bed.

### 2.3 Functions of Each Component ŌĆö Detailed Logical Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Step 1 ŌĆö Afflux Creation:** The weir crest is set at a level higher than the canal bed level, so the design pond level (HFL behind the weir) is always higher than the canal FSL. This is the *fundamental* reason for building a weir/barrage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Step 2 ŌĆö Flow Concentration:** Under-sluices are placed on the **same side as the canal off-take** so that during low-flow periods, all the dry-weather flow is concentrated in the under-sluice pocket, ensuring the deepest portion of the river (thalweg) remains adjacent to the head regulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Step 3 ŌĆö Sediment Exclusion:** Coarse bed load settles in the still pocket of the under-sluices during low flow and is periodically flushed downstream by opening the under-sluice gates. Fine sediment that escapes this barrier is tackled by the silt ejector downstream in the canal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Step 4 ŌĆö Energy Dissipation:** Water falling over the weir possesses high kinetic energy, which would scour the downstream riverbed. The talus apron (a depressed horizontal floor with a mild slope at the end) dissipates this energy by hydraulic jump formation, while the launching apron provides a self-healing mechanism against unforeseen scour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Step 5 ŌĆö Seepage Control:** Water seeping under the weir carries fine soil particles (piping). Cut-off walls force the water to take a longer path (increasing the creep length), reducing the hydraulic gradient and exit gradient to safe values (Bligh's creep theory / Khosla's theory).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **board-exam critical** for design problems in PECET755.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Afflux** | $h = H_1 - H_2$ | m | Difference between upstream and downstream water levels |
| 2 | **Discharge over Weir (Francis formula)** | $Q = C_d \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | cumecs | Discharge per metre length of weir |
| 3 | **Discharge over Weir (Rehbock formula)** | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | cumecs | More accurate for sharp-crested weirs |
| 4 | **Lacey's Normal Discharge** | $Q = A \cdot V_s$ | cumecs | $V_s$ = Lacey's silt-free mean velocity |
| 5 | **Lacey's Mean Velocity** | $V_s = 10.8 \cdot R^{1/2} \cdot S^{1/3}$ | m/s | Regime velocity for alluvial channels |
| 6 | **Lacey's Regime Width** | $P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q}$ | m | Wetted perimeter of regime channel |
| 7 | **Afflux (Barrage, simplified)** | $\left(\dfrac{Q}{Q_1}\right)^2 = \dfrac{h_2}{h_1} \left(\dfrac{h_2 + h_f}{h_1 + h_f}\right)$ | ŌĆö | Relation of upstream/downstream depths |
| 8 | **Bligh's Creep Length** | $L = C \cdot H$ | m | $C$ = Bligh's creep coefficient (2.0 to 18.0) |
| 9 | **Khosla's Exit Gradient** | $G_E = \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$ | ŌĆö | Where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}$, $\alpha = b/d$ |
| 10 | **Bligh's Hydraulic Gradient** | $\phi = \dfrac{H}{L}$ | ŌĆö | Total head loss divided by creep length |
| 11 | **Silt Charge** | $\rho = \dfrac{V^3}{g \cdot H \cdot w}$ | ŌĆö | Dimensionless sediment parameter |
| 12 | **Maximum Flood Discharge (Dickens)** | $Q_{max} = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}$ | cumecs | Empirical formula for design flood |
| 13 | **Maximum Flood Discharge (Ryves)** | $Q_{max} = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}$ | cumecs | Empirical formula for design flood |
| 14 | **Pond Level (above weir crest)** | $Pond\ Level = Crest\ Level + Design\ Head$ | m | Sets the upstream HFL for diversion |
| 15 | **Under-sluice Discharge Capacity** | $Q_{us} \geq 2 \times Dry\ Weather\ Flow$ | cumecs | Operational design rule for scouring |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students confuse **silt excluder** and **silt ejector**. Remember: **Excluder** = in the *under-sluice pocket* (excludes sediment from entering canal). **Ejector** = *in the canal itself* (ejects sediment that has already entered). Both are needed, but they operate at different locations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Diversion headworks are foundational to:
- **Irrigation Engineering** ŌĆö In India, the Lower Ganga Canal, Upper Ganga Canal, Sarda Canal, and Krishna-Godavari delta systems are all served by diversion headworks.
- **Hydropower Engineering** ŌĆö Run-of-river hydroelectric projects (e.g., small hydro projects in Kerala's Idukki and Pathanamthitta districts) use diversion headworks to channel water into power tunnels.
- **Water Supply** ŌĆö Municipal water intakes from rivers often use diversion structures.
- **Flood Management** ŌĆö The barrage serves as a controlled overflow, regulating floods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Discharge Over a Sharp-Crested Weir (Francis Equation)

The governing equation for flow over a sharp-crested weir is derived from the integration of the velocity distribution across the weir notch.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumptions:**
- The flow over the weir is two-dimensional.
- The velocity at any depth $h$ below the free surface is given by Torricelli's law: $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$.
- Atmospheric pressure acts on both sides of the nappe.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Consider a Horizontal Strip:**
Take an elemental horizontal strip at a depth $h$ below the water surface, with thickness $dh$, and length $L$ (length of the weir crest).

$$
\text{Elemental area: } dA = L \cdot dh
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Elemental Discharge:**
The velocity through this strip is $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$ by Torricelli's theorem. Therefore:

$$
dQ = V \cdot dA = \sqrt{2 g h} \cdot L \cdot dh
$$

$$
dQ = L \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Integration Across the Head H:**
Integrating the elemental discharge from $h = 0$ (water surface) to $h = H$ (weir crest level):

$$
Q = L \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh
$$

$$
Q = L \sqrt{2g} \left[ \dfrac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_0^H
$$

$$
Q = L \sqrt{2g} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} H^{3/2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Application of Discharge Coefficient:**
In real conditions, viscous effects, nappe contraction, and approach velocity reduce the theoretical discharge. This is accounted for by a discharge coefficient $C_d$ (typically **0.62 to 0.66** for a sharp-crested rectangular weir):

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \sqrt{2g} \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}} \quad \text{(for } C_d = 0.623 \text{)}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation:**
The discharge varies as the **3/2 power of the head**. This is the **fundamental weir equation** that KTU examiners expect you to derive and apply. The constant $1.84$ is widely used in Indian practice.

### 3.2 Derivation of Lacey's Regime Width (P = 4.75 ŌłÜQ)

Lacey's regime theory (1930) provides empirical relationships for stable alluvial channels carrying sediment-laden water. The derivation flow:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Lacey's Silt Factor:**
Lacey defined a non-dimensional silt factor $f$ as:

$$
f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m}
$$

where $d_m$ is the median grain size of the bed material in **millimetres**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Lacey's Mean Velocity:**
The regime (silt-transporting) velocity is given by:

$$
V_s = \left( \dfrac{Q \cdot f^2}{140} \right)^{1/6} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Regime Hydraulic Radius:**
Lacey proposed:

$$
R_s = 0.473 \left( \dfrac{Q}{f} \right)^{1/3} \text{ metres}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Wetted Perimeter:**
Since for a wide channel $P \approx R$ (a regime result), combining Steps 2 and 3:

$$
P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q} \text{ metres}
$$

This formula is extensively used to determine the **spacing of groynes** and the **regime width of a river** at the headworks site.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A weir is to be constructed across a river with the following data:
- Design discharge: $Q = 1500$ cumecs
- Designed head over the weir: $H = 1.5$ m
- Length of the weir available: $L = 100$ m
- River bed level: $\text{RL} = 100.00$ m
- Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = 0.62$

**Required:** (a) Compute the actual discharge capacity. (b) Check if it is sufficient. (c) If not, suggest the modification.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Discharge Capacity:**

$$
Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}
$$

$$
Q = 1.84 \times 100 \times (1.5)^{3/2}
$$

$$
(1.5)^{3/2} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 1.5 \times 1.2247 = 1.837
$$

$$
Q = 1.84 \times 100 \times 1.837 = 338.0 \text{ cumecs}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Comparison:**
The design discharge of **1500 cumecs** is far greater than the weir capacity of **338 cumecs** at $H = 1.5$ m. Therefore, the structure will be overtopped severely during the design flood.

**Part (c) ŌĆö Modification Options:**

Option 1: Increase the designed head by raising shutters/gates.

Option 2: Increase the effective length of the weir by adding more bays.

Option 3: Construct a **barrage** with gates that can be fully opened during floods, with a low depressed crest.

The correct KTU-expected answer is to redesign as a **barrage** with gates, since $Q$ is high.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Afflux, Scour, and Exit Gradient

| Parameter | Governing Equation | Typical KTU Value | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Afflux $h$ | $h = H_1 - H_2$ | 0.5 to 2.0 m | Causes submergence upstream |
| Lacey's Scour Depth | $D = 0.473 \left(\dfrac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | Variable | Sets the maximum depth of cut-off wall |
| Bligh's Creep Length | $L = C \cdot H$ (with $C = 2$ to 18) | For sandy soil, $C = 5$ to 7 | Determines total length of impervious floor |
| Khosla's Exit Gradient | $G_E = \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$ | Safe $G_E \le \dfrac{1}{5}$ to $\dfrac{1}{6}$ | Prevents piping failure |
| Hydraulic Gradient | $\phi = \dfrac{H}{L}$ | $\phi \le \dfrac{1}{15}$ to $\dfrac{1}{25}$ | Controls overall seepage safety |

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Weir vs Barrage

| Feature | Weir | Barrage |
|---------|------|---------|
| Crest level | At or near river bed | Depressed (well below bed) |
| Gates | Optional shutters | Integral gated bays |
| Afflux | Small (1ŌĆō2 m) | Small and controlled |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Use | Small/medium rivers | Large rivers |
| Energy dissipation | Talus may be sufficient | Full hydraulic jump apron required |
| Floor | Impervious floor + cut-offs | Long impervious floor with intermediate cut-offs |
| Scour handling | Limited | Robust launching apron |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plan View of a Diversion Headwork (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[River Approach] --> A2[Marginal Bund Left]
    A2 --> A3[Upstream Groyne]
    A3 --> A4[Upstream Cutoff Wall]
    A4 --> A5[Weir Proper with Crest]
    A5 --> A6[Undersluice Pocket]
    A6 --> A7[Divide Wall]
    A7 --> A8[Head Regulator]
    A8 --> A9[Canal Offtake FSL]
    A5 --> A10[Downstream Apron Talus]
    A10 --> A11[Launching Apron]
    A11 --> A12[Downstream Cutoff]
    A12 --> A13[Downstream River]
    A6 --> A14[Silt Excluder]
    A9 --> A15[Silt Ejector in Canal]
    A6 --> A16[Fish Ladder]
```

### 4.2 Sectional View (L Section) of Weir

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UPSTREAM
        U1[Upland Flood] --> U2[U/S Cutoff Wall]
        U2 --> U3[Impervious Floor]
        U3 --> U4[Weir Crest with Shutter]
    end
    U4 --> D1[Downstream Apron]
    D1 --> D2[Hydraulic Jump Zone]
    D2 --> D3[Talus Slope]
    D3 --> D4[Launching Apron Loose Stone]
    D4 --> D5[D/S Cutoff Wall]
    D5 --> D6[Original River Bed]
    D6 --> D7[Downstream River]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Diversion Headwork

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Natural River Flow] --> B{Diversion Headwork Complex}
    B --> C1[Afflux Section Weir]
    B --> C2[Scour Section Undersluices]
    B --> C3[Regulation Section Head Regulator]
    C1 --> D1[Pond Level Rises]
    D1 --> D2[Water Diverted to Canal]
    C2 --> D3[Sediment Flushed to D/S]
    C3 --> D4[Controlled Discharge into Canal]
    D2 --> E1[Irrigation Command Area]
    D4 --> E1
    D3 --> F1[Downstream River]
    A --> G1[Fish Ladder Passage]
    A --> H1[Sediment Excluder Mechanism]
    H1 --> D2
    D2 --> H2[Silt Ejector in Canal]
    H2 --> F1
```

### 4.4 Component Interconnection Matrix

| From Component | To Component | Interaction Type | Engineering Purpose |
|----------------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| River | Weir Crest | Overflow | Raise water level |
| Weir Crest | Under-sluice | Hydraulic gradient | Concentrate low flow |
| Under-sluice | Head Regulator | Flow transfer | Feed canal |
| Head Regulator | Silt Ejector | Sequential | Remove fine sediment |
| Weir Floor | Cutoff Walls | Seepage path | Increase creep length |
| Talus | Launching Apron | Energy | Dissipate kinetic energy |
| Divide Wall | Under-sluice | Hydraulic separation | Isolate weir from off-take |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define a Diversion Headwork. Differentiate between a **weir** and a **barrage** in three points.

**Model Answer:**
A Diversion Headwork is a hydraulic structure built across a river to raise the water level (afflux) and divert the flow into an off-taking canal by gravity.

**Differences:**

| S.No. | Weir | Barrage |
|-------|------|---------|
| 1 | Crest is set at or near the river bed level | Crest is set well below the river bed level |
| 2 | Afflux is achieved by shutters and crest height | Afflux is achieved by gates that are closed partially |
| 3 | Suitable for small to medium rivers (low discharge) | Suitable for large rivers with high flood discharge |
| 4 | No or limited floor needed | Long impervious floor with cut-offs is essential |

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** List any **six functions** of a Diversion Headwork.

**Model Answer:**
1. Raises the water level upstream to enable gravity off-take.
2. Diverts water into the canal through the head regulator.
3. Controls flood discharge safely through the weir/barrage.
4. Excludes coarse sediment using under-sluices and silt excluders.
5. Provides a fish ladder for ecological flow maintenance.
6. Confines the river flow using marginal bunds to prevent flooding of adjoining areas.
7. Dissipates excess kinetic energy of falling water through the talus and launching apron.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Comprehensive Layout & Function Analysis**

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **layout of a Diversion Headwork** and explain the functions of any **five major components**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **difference between a silt excluder and a silt ejector**. State their relative locations in a headwork and justify why both are necessary.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Layout and Functions:**

The layout of a diversion headwork consists of the following components arranged in a sequence from upstream to downstream:

**1. Marginal Bunds:** Earthen embankments constructed on both banks of the river upstream of the weir to confine the flood flow and prevent submergence of low-lying areas. They are designed for the **Standard Project Flood (SPF)** with a freeboard of **1.5 m to 2.0 m**.

**2. Divide Wall (Groyne Wall):** A masonry wall constructed perpendicular to the weir axis. Its function is to:
- Separate the weir section from the under-sluice pocket.
- Prevent cross-flow of water from the weir to the off-take, which would otherwise disturb the approach conditions of the head regulator.
- Reduce silt entry into the canal by isolating the pocket.

**3. Weir Proper:** The main overflow section. It maintains the upstream pond level. The crest is designed to pass the **design flood** with afflux. The crest may have adjustable shutters that can be removed during floods to reduce afflux.

**4. Under-Sluices (Scouring Sluices):** Low-lying gated openings located on the same side as the off-take. Functions:
- Concentrate the low-flow discharge to maintain a deep channel in front of the head regulator.
- Flush out sediment deposited in the under-sluice pocket by opening gates periodically.
- Pass low to moderate floods without raising the upstream level excessively.

**5. Head Regulator:** A gated control structure at the entrance of the canal. Functions:
- Regulate the discharge entering the canal.
- Stop the entry of water into the canal during emergencies.
- Maintain a stable flow regime in the canal by throttling gates.

**6. Fish Ladder:** A stepped or inclined channel with baffles for migrating fish.

**7. Talus and Launching Apron:** Downstream floor for energy dissipation and scour protection.

**8. Cut-Off Walls:** Vertical walls under the floor to reduce seepage and increase creep length.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Silt Excluder vs Silt Ejector:**

| Feature | Silt Excluder | Silt Ejector |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Location | In the **under-sluice pocket**, before water enters the canal | **In the canal itself**, 200ŌĆō500 m downstream of head regulator |
| Purpose | Excludes **coarse bed load** sediment from entering the canal | Ejects the **fine suspended silt** that has already entered the canal |
| Working Principle | Stilling basin effect; sediment settles due to low velocity in the pocket; periodically flushed through under-sluice gates | Creates a high-velocity tunnel beneath the canal bed; sediment-laden water is drawn out by the head difference between canal and tunnel |
| Frequency of operation | Periodic, tied to under-sluice operation | Continuous or periodic, depending on silt load |

**Justification for Using Both:** A silt excluder alone cannot remove all sediment ŌĆö fine particles still enter the canal in suspension. A silt ejector is therefore required as a second line of defence to prevent siltation of the canal, which would reduce its carrying capacity. Both are necessary because they tackle **different size fractions** of sediment at **different locations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the two silt-control structures. Always remember: **Excluder is OUTSIDE the canal (in the river pocket)**; **Ejector is INSIDE the canal (in the conveyance system)**. Marks are lost when these are interchanged in sketches.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Long Question**

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **afflux**? Explain with a neat sketch how afflux is computed for a weir constructed on a permeable foundation. State Bligh's creep theory and its significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a sharp-crested weir for the following data: Design discharge $Q = 250$ cumecs; length of weir $L = 40$ m; coefficient of discharge $C_d = 0.62$. Assume a suitable design head and verify whether the design is safe. If $Q = 1000$ cumecs is to be passed, what should be the designed head? Comment.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Afflux and Bligh's Creep Theory:**

**Afflux** is the rise in water level on the upstream side of the weir compared to the downstream side, caused by the obstruction. It is the difference between the upstream and downstream depths:

$$
h = H_1 - H_2
$$

**Significance of Afflux:** Afflux causes submergence of upstream land, which is mitigated by constructing marginal bunds of adequate height. The design of marginal bunds and the selection of crest level depend directly on the computed afflux.

**Bligh's Creep Theory (1910):** Water seeping under a weir on a permeable foundation exerts an upward pressure on the floor. Bligh proposed that the **creep length** (the total length of the path that the water has to travel under the structure) must be sufficient to dissipate the head loss without causing piping failure. The creep length is given by:

$$
L = \sum L_h + \sum L_v
$$

where $L_h$ is the sum of all horizontal floor lengths and $L_v$ is the sum of all vertical cut-off wall depths (with end cut-offs counted once and intermediate ones counted twice).

**Design Rule (Bligh's):**

$$
L = C \cdot H
$$

where $C$ is Bligh's creep coefficient. For different soils, $C$ varies from **2 (very fine sand)** to **18 (dense clay)**. The hydraulic gradient is:

$$
\phi = \frac{H}{L} = \frac{1}{C}
$$

For safe design, $\phi$ must be less than the **safe hydraulic gradient** for the foundation soil, which is approximately $1/5$ to $1/7$ for fine sand.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Weir Design:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Discharge Capacity for Assumed Head:**

Assume a design head $H = 2.0$ m. Then:

$$
Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}
$$

$$
Q = 1.84 \times 40 \times (2.0)^{3/2}
$$

$$
(2.0)^{3/2} = 2.0 \times \sqrt{2.0} = 2.0 \times 1.414 = 2.828
$$

$$
Q = 1.84 \times 40 \times 2.828 = 208.1 \text{ cumecs}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Comparison:**
The required discharge is **250 cumecs**, but the weir can only pass **208.1 cumecs** at $H = 2.0$ m. Hence, the design is **inadequate** with the assumed head.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recompute Head for $Q = 250$ cumecs:**

$$
H^{3/2} = \frac{Q}{1.84 \cdot L} = \frac{250}{1.84 \times 40} = \frac{250}{73.6} = 3.397
$$

$$
H = (3.397)^{2/3}
$$

$$
\ln(H) = \frac{2}{3} \ln(3.397) = \frac{2}{3} \times 1.223 = 0.815
$$

$$
H = e^{0.815} = 2.26 \text{ m}
$$

So a head of **2.26 m** is required for the design discharge.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Head Required for $Q = 1000$ cumecs (Flood Check):**

$$
H^{3/2} = \frac{1000}{1.84 \times 40} = \frac{1000}{73.6} = 13.59
$$

$$
H = (13.59)^{2/3}
$$

$$
\ln(H) = \frac{2}{3} \ln(13.59) = \frac{2}{3} \times 2.609 = 1.739
$$

$$
H = e^{1.739} = 5.69 \text{ m}
$$

**Comment:** A head of **5.69 m** during floods is excessive and would cause severe upstream submergence. Hence, a **barrage with gates** is recommended instead of a simple weir, so that the gates can be opened during floods to keep the upstream HFL low.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert units consistently** (e.g., mm to m in Lacey's formula).
> 2. Mixing up **afflux (h)** and **head over weir (H)** in derivations.
> 3. Confusing **Bligh's** (uses horizontal + vertical creep length, with verticals counted 1├Ś for end and 2├Ś for intermediate cut-offs) with **Khosla's** (uses independent floor analysis with exit gradient).
> 4. Omitting the **discharge coefficient** in the Francis equation (using $Q = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{2g} L H^{3/2}$ without $C_d$).
> 5. Stating the **wrong location** for silt excluder/ejector in sketches.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 ŌĆö DHW Layout):**

- **DHW** = small barrage/weir to raise water level and divert into a canal.
- **Afflux** = rise in upstream HFL = $H_1 - H_2$.
- **Weir** = crest at bed level, suitable for low discharge; **Barrage** = depressed crest with gates, suitable for high discharge.
- **Francis equation** (must be memorized): $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$.
- **Lacey's regime width**: $P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q}$.
- **Bligh's creep length**: $L = C \cdot H$ where $C$ ranges 2 (fine sand) to 18 (clay).
- **Khosla's exit gradient safe limit**: $G_E \le 1/5$ to $1/6$.
- **Under-sluices** are placed on the **canal side** to maintain the thalweg in front of the head regulator.
- **Divide wall** isolates the weir pocket from the canal off-take; prevents cross-flow.
- **Silt excluder** is in the **under-sluice pocket**; **silt ejector** is in the **canal**, downstream of the head regulator.
- **Fish ladder** is obligatory for ecological compliance.
- **Marginal bunds** protect upstream land from submergence due to afflux; designed for **Standard Project Flood** with **1.5 to 2.0 m freeboard**.
- **Talus** = inclined downstream floor; **Launching apron** = loose stone protection at the end of the talus.
- **Cut-off walls** are vertical, extend below the floor, and increase the seepage path.
- **Head regulator** controls discharge into the canal and prevents backflow during emergencies.
- In a sketch, label the components in **sequence**: Approach ŌåÆ Bund ŌåÆ Groyne ŌåÆ Cut-off ŌåÆ Weir ŌåÆ Under-sluice ŌåÆ Head Regulator ŌåÆ Apron ŌåÆ Launching Apron ŌåÆ Tail Channel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weir and Barrage: Causes of Failure on Permeable Soils, Bligh's Theory and Khosla's Theory, Design of Vertical Drop Weir, Design of Impervious Floor by Khosla's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: WEIR AND BARRAGE ŌĆö FOUNDATION FAILURE AND DESIGN THEORIES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆö Weir**
> A **weir** is a low dam-like obstruction built across a river or channel to raise the upstream water level and divert flow into a canal or into a main storage reservoir. Weirs are typically constructed on **permeable alluvial foundations** and are designed to allow a small regulated overflow (afflux) over their crest during high floods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆö Barrage**
> A **barrage** is a hydraulic structure built across a river with a series of large gates (or shutters) that, when raised, allow the river to pass through with very little afflux. The gates are fully opened during floods and partially closed during dry seasons to maintain a near-constant upstream pond level for diversion. Barrages are essentially **gated weirs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆö Impervious Floor (Apron)**
> A **horizontal reinforced cement concrete (RCC) or brick floor** constructed upstream and downstream of a weir/barrage to:
> 1. Increase the **path of seepage** (creep length) under the structure.
> 2. Reduce the **uplift pressure** on the foundation.
> 3. Protect the bed against **scour and undermining** caused by the high-velocity jets issuing under the gates.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Leaking Bathtub

Imagine you have a bathtub full of water resting on **sandy soil**. The water pushes downward and tries to leak out through the sand. The longer the leak path (e.g., a longer pipe), the slower the leak. A weir on permeable soil behaves exactly like this:
- **Water head (H)** = the pressure driving seepage down through the foundation.
- **Creep length (L)** = the total path length the water must travel beneath the structure.
- **If L is too short**, the water escapes with such velocity that it carries the sand grains with it ŌĆö a phenomenon called **piping** ŌĆö and the weir collapses.

The design theories (Bligh and Khosla) are essentially mathematical tools to ensure this "leak" remains slow and safe.

### Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Specific gravity of floor concrete (G_c) = 2.24** (typical for plain concrete; 2.40 for RCC).
- **Specific gravity of saturated soil grains (G_s) = 2.65 to 2.70**.
- **Bligh's creep coefficient (C)**:
  - For **fine sand** ŌåÆ C = **15 to 18**
  - For **coarse sand** ŌåÆ C = **12 to 15**
  - For **gravel and boulder** ŌåÆ C = **5 to 9**
- **Khosla's safe exit gradient (G_E, safe)** = **1/(C'┬ĘŽĆ)** with **C' (Lane's coefficient)** = N (depends on soil type).

---

### Classification of Weirs on Permeable Foundations

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Vertical Drop Weir** | Water drops vertically from crest to downstream floor; suitable for low to moderate heads (Ōēż 5 m) and permeable foundations. |
| **Sloping Weir** | Downstream glacis is sloped; energy dissipates along the slope. |
| **Rockfill Weir** | Constructed with rockfill in dry condition; suitable for rocky beds. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECET755 Module 1):**
> The student is expected to master:
> 1. Causes of failure of weirs on permeable foundations.
> 2. **Bligh's Creep Theory** (classical empirical method).
> 3. **Khosla's Theory** (modern rational method).
> 4. **Design of a Vertical Drop Weir** complete with crest, glacis, floor, and energy dissipation.
> 5. **Design of impervious floor thickness** by Khosla's balanced floor concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Causes of Failure of Weirs on Permeable Foundations

A weir built directly on sand, silt, or other alluvium can fail by one or more of the following mechanisms:

### (A) Failure by **Piping** (Undermining / Boiling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piping** is the **most common cause of failure** of weirs on alluvial soils. It is the progressive erosion of foundation soil caused by the high-velocity seepage water emerging on the downstream side of the impervious floor.

**Mechanism:**
1. Seepage water travels under the floor.
2. As it emerges at the downstream end of the apron, it carries an **uplift pressure** equal to the residual head.
3. The exit velocity (V_exit) at the soil-water interface exceeds the **critical velocity** that can dislodge soil grains.
4. Soil particles are carried out, leaving voids.
5. Voids enlarge, propagate backward under the floor ŌåÆ catastrophic subsidence.

**Piping is prevented by:**
- Making the impervious floor sufficiently **long** (to extend creep path).
- Making the floor sufficiently **thick** (to resist uplift pressure by self-weight).

### (B) Failure by **Uplift (Rupture) of the Floor**

The hydrostatic uplift pressure acting on the underside of the floor can exceed the **dead weight** of the floor, lifting it up and breaking it.

**Condition of safety:**
$$W_{floor} \geq U_{uplift}$$
$$t \cdot \gamma_c \cdot A \geq p_{uplift} \cdot A$$
$$t \geq \frac{p_{uplift}}{\gamma_c}$$

### (C) Failure by **Scour**

High-velocity water issuing from under the gates scours the downstream river bed. The scour hole may extend **backward** to undermine the floor.

**Prevention:**
- Provide a **launching apron** (loose stone/boulder protection) downstream of the floor.
- Provide sufficient **stilling basin** length.

### (D) Failure by **Structural Overtopping**

Insufficient freeboard allows flood water to overtop the non-engineered abutments, causing breach.

---

## 2.2 Bligh's Creep Theory (1910)

### Theory Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Bligh's Creep Theory** assumes that **seepage water travels along the contact surface** between the base of the impervious floor and the top of the foundation soil. The total path of seepage (called the **creep length**, L) is the sum of the **horizontal** and **vertical** contact lengths.

### Creep Length (L) Computation

For a weir with:
- A horizontal floor of length **bŌéü** (upstream) + **bŌéé** (downstream).
- Two end piles (cut-off walls) of depth **dŌéü** (upstream) + **dŌéé** (downstream).

**Bligh's creep length:**
$$L = b_1 + b_2 + 2 d_1 + 2 d_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Bligh considered both the upstream and downstream cut-offs equally effective** ŌĆö he assigned the **same** creep length contribution to both vertical walls. (This is a key difference from Lane's theory covered later.)

### Bligh's Design Rule

**To prevent piping, the creep length must satisfy:**
$$L \geq C \cdot H$$

where:
- **L** = total creep length (m)
- **C** = Bligh's creep coefficient (dimensionless)
- **H** = maximum static head (difference between upstream and downstream water levels, m)

### Residual Head at Any Point on the Floor

Bligh assumed a **linear** distribution of residual head along the creep path.

**Residual head at the downstream end of the floor (exit):**
$$h_{exit} = H \cdot \frac{1}{C}$$

**Safety against uplift at the downstream end:**
$$t_{min} = \frac{h_{exit}}{\gamma_c - 1} = \frac{H}{C(\gamma_c - 1)}$$

---

## 2.3 Limitations of Bligh's Theory

> [!WARNING]
> **Bligh's Theory is empirical and has serious flaws** which led to its replacement by Khosla's Theory:

1. **Linear pressure assumption:** Bligh assumed uniform head loss per unit creep length ŌĆö this is **not true**; in reality, more head is lost at the start of the floor (where flow is concentrated) and less toward the exit.
2. **Equal weightage to horizontal and vertical creeps:** Bligh gave the **same weight** to vertical cut-offs as to horizontal floor. This is incorrect; vertical cut-offs are more efficient.
3. **No account of floor thickness** in creep calculation.
4. **No way to compute exit gradient**, the parameter that actually controls piping failure.
5. **Single lumped coefficient C** is not a true soil property; it varies with H and soil grading.

---

## 2.4 Khosla's Theory (1936) ŌĆö Modern Rational Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Khosla's Theory** is a **rational (analytical) theory** based on the **theory of flow nets** for seepage under hydraulic structures founded on permeable soils of infinite depth with a horizontal floor. It treats the floor as a series of **independent elementary profiles**.

### Key Khosla Concepts

#### (a) Elementary Profiles
Khosla reduced the complex floor shape to a combination of the following standard profiles:

1. **A straight horizontal floor of negligible thickness** (no cut-offs).
2. **A floor with a vertical cut-off at the upstream end only.**
3. **A floor with a vertical cut-off at the downstream end only.**
4. **A floor with vertical cut-offs at both ends** (the most useful for weirs).

#### (b) Key Formula ŌĆö Residual Head at Key Points

For a floor of total length **b** with two vertical cut-offs of depths **dŌéü** (upstream) and **dŌéé** (downstream) ŌĆö Khosla's **standard formulae for pressure at key points** are:

| Point | Location | Residual Head (╬”) |
|---|---|---|
| **CŌéü** | Downstream face of upstream cut-off | $\Phi_{C_1} = \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right)$ |
| **CŌéé** | Upstream face of downstream cut-off | $\Phi_{C_2} = \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right)$ (symmetric if dŌéü = dŌéé) |
| **E** | Downstream end of floor (exit) | $\Phi_E = \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right)$ |
| **D** | Junction of floor and downstream cut-off | $\Phi_D = \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right]$ (for symmetric case) |
| **C** | Mid-point of floor | $\Phi_C = \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 1}{\lambda + 1}\right)$ |

> where **╬╗ = b/d** is the **creep length ratio** (b = total horizontal floor length; d = depth of cut-off, taken as average of dŌéü and dŌéé if unequal).

> [!TIP]
> The most critical point for design is **E (exit point)** because the highest exit gradient occurs here.

#### (c) Exit Gradient (G_E)

**The exit gradient is the hydraulic gradient of the seepage flow as it emerges at the downstream end of the floor.** It is the most critical design parameter.

**Khosla's exit gradient formula:**
$$G_E = \frac{H}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

where:
- H = static head (m)
- d = depth of downstream cut-off (m) [use dŌéé specifically]
- ╬╗ = b/d (creep length ratio)
- b = total horizontal floor length (m)

**Safety criterion (against piping):**
$$G_E \leq G_{E,safe} = \frac{1}{C'\pi}$$

where **C' = Lane's weighted creep coefficient** (a function of soil type):

| Soil Type | Lane's C' |
|---|---|
| Very fine sand / silt | 8.5 |
| Fine sand | 7.0 |
| Medium sand | 5.0 ŌĆō 6.0 |
| Coarse sand | 4.0 |
| Gravel / boulder | 3.0 ŌĆō 3.5 |

#### (d) The Pressure at the Bottom of the Cut-Offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressure at the **bottom of the downstream cut-off (point D)** must also be checked. Khosla's formula:
> $$\Phi_D = \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right]$$
> This head is used to check the **danger of undermining at the bottom of the cut-off** by **piping or sand boiling** at that point.

#### (e) Correction for Mutual Interference of Cut-Offs

When both upstream and downstream cut-offs are present, they interfere with each other's flow pattern. Khosla gave a graphical **chart of correction percentages** for each key point. The corrections are applied as a percentage of H.

**Approximate correction formula (when dŌéü ŌēĀ dŌéé, the floor is asymmetric):**
$$\%\text{ Correction} = 19 \sqrt{\frac{d_1}{b'}}\left(\frac{d_1 + d_2}{b'}\right)$$

> where b' = distance between the two cut-offs (horizontal floor length between them).

The correction is **positive** (added) at points on the side of the larger cut-off and **negative** (subtracted) on the side of the smaller cut-off.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bligh's creep length | $L = b_1 + b_2 + 2d_1 + 2d_2$ | Sum of all horizontal and vertical contact |
| 2 | Bligh's safety rule | $L \geq C \cdot H$ | C = creep coefficient (5 to 18) |
| 3 | Bligh's exit head | $h_e = H / C$ | Residual head at floor end |
| 4 | Bligh's floor thickness (downstream end) | $t = \dfrac{H}{C(\gamma_c - 1)}$ | Submerged weight criterion |
| 5 | Khosla's exit gradient | $G_E = \dfrac{H}{d\pi\sqrt{\lambda}}$ | $\lambda = b/d$ |
| 6 | Khosla's safe exit gradient | $G_{E,safe} = \dfrac{1}{C'\pi}$ | C' = Lane's coefficient |
| 7 | Safety condition | $G_E \leq G_{E,safe}$ | Prevents piping |
| 8 | Pressure at point C (mid-floor) | $\Phi_C = \dfrac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\lambda-1}{\lambda+1}\right)$ | Head at center |
| 9 | Pressure at point CŌéü/CŌéé (cut-off face) | $\Phi_{C_1} = \dfrac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\lambda-2}{\lambda}\right)$ | Head at cut-off |
| 10 | Pressure at point D (cut-off bottom) | $\Phi_D = \dfrac{H}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right]$ | Head at cut-off base |
| 11 | Balanced floor thickness (Khosla) | $t = \dfrac{\Phi_E}{\gamma_c - 1}$ (submerged) | Reduces by using sheet piles at correct points |
| 12 | Lane's weighted creep | $L_w = \sum b_i + \frac{1}{3}\sum d_i$ | Horizontal fully effective; vertical 1/3 weight |
| 13 | Lane's safety rule | $L_w \geq C' \cdot H$ | Replaced Bligh's rule |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weir design on alluvial rivers** (Ganga, Yamuna, Godavari, Kosi) ŌĆö where foundations are deep sand/silt and traditional rock foundation is absent.
- **Barrage design** at barrages like Haridwar, Farakka, Prakasam, Dholpur.
- **Coffer dam design** (temporary structures).
- **Spillway apron design** for earth dams.
- **Canal headworks** and cross-drainage structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Bligh's Creep Length Formula

### Setup
Consider a weir with:
- A **floor** of horizontal length **b** (sum of upstream and downstream portions).
- **Two vertical cut-offs (pile lines)**: an upstream cut-off of depth **dŌéü** and a downstream cut-off of depth **dŌéé**.

### Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= (\text{horizontal floor length}) + (\text{vertical cut-off lengths, both faces}) \\
L &= b_1 + b_2 + 2 d_1 + 2 d_2
\end{aligned}$$

> Here, each vertical cut-off contributes **2d** to the creep length because the water must travel **down** one face (length d) and **up** the other face (length d) ŌĆö a total of 2d per cut-off.

### Design Equation

To prevent piping, the head loss H must occur over a sufficient creep path:

$$L = C \cdot H \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_1 + b_2 + 2 d_1 + 2 d_2 = C \cdot H$$

This is solved for either the **floor length b** or the **cut-off depth d** given the other.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Khosla's Exit Gradient (G_E)

### Foundation Assumptions
1. Homogeneous, isotropic, infinite-depth permeable soil.
2. Floor is a thin, horizontal impervious sheet with vertical cut-offs.
3. Seepage follows **Darcy's law** and **Laplace's equation** for flow in porous media.

### Hydraulic Gradient
The hydraulic gradient is the slope of the hydraulic head line:
$$i = \frac{dh}{ds}$$
where s is the flow path length.

### Flow Under a Floor (Schwarz-Christoffel Transformation)
Khosla used the **Schwarz-Christoffel conformal mapping** to transform the complex flow domain (with cut-offs) into a simpler geometry. The result for the **exit gradient at point E** (downstream end of floor) reduces to:

$$G_E = \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial s}\bigg\vert_{E} = \frac{H}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

### Step-by-Step Final Form

$$\begin{aligned}
G_E &= \frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{\lambda}} \cdot \frac{H}{d} \\
\text{where} \quad \lambda &= \frac{b}{d} \\
\Rightarrow G_E &= \frac{H}{d \pi \sqrt{b/d}} = \frac{H}{\pi \sqrt{bd}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Safety Check

The **critical exit gradient** at which piping begins (Terzaghi's criterion):
$$G_{E,crit} = (G_s - 1)(1 - n) \approx \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}$$

For a safety factor of ~6 to 7:
$$G_{E,safe} = \frac{G_{E,crit}}{6} \approx \frac{1}{\pi C'}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Balanced Floor Thickness

### Unbalanced Floor (Bligh's Approach)
If we just provide uniform thickness based on the **maximum residual head (at the downstream end)**, the floor thickness is:

$$t = \frac{\Phi_E}{\gamma_c - \gamma_w} = \frac{\Phi_E}{\gamma_c - 1}$$

> where $\gamma_c$ is the specific weight of floor concrete (= **2.24 t/m┬│** for plain concrete) and $\gamma_w = 1$ t/m┬│.

This gives a **thick, expensive** downstream end.

### Khosla's Balanced Floor Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Khosla's Balanced Floor Principle:**
> Instead of providing a uniform-thick floor, the **uplift pressure profile** is **inverted** by the floor's own weight such that:
> 1. The **uplift pressure at the downstream end is exactly balanced** by the floor thickness there.
> 2. The **uplift at the upstream end is also exactly balanced** by the upstream thickness.
> 3. The floor thickness in between is calculated as: **t(x) = ╬”(x) / (╬│_c ŌłÆ 1)** at every section, BUT the floor surface is then **levelled** by providing the **downstream thickness throughout**.

The **minimum balanced floor thickness** at the downstream end is given by:

$$\boxed{t_{min} = \frac{\Phi_E}{G_c - 1}}$$

> where $G_c$ = specific gravity of floor concrete.

If we provide this thickness at the downstream end, the upstream end (with lower residual head) will be **over-designed** automatically ŌĆö but it is often levelled for practical construction, with **sheet piles (cut-offs)** placed strategically to reduce the required thickness.

### Standard Simplified Formula (in common use)

For routine design, the downstream end thickness is approximated as:

$$t = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{\Phi_E}{G_c - 1}$$

> The factor **4/3** is an empirical multiplier accounting for **non-uniform uplift** and **construction tolerances**.

Alternatively, for **plain concrete floor (G_c = 2.24)**:
$$t_{min} = \frac{\Phi_E}{1.24}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Full Khosla Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Examination Style):**
> A weir is to be constructed on a sandy foundation. Given:
> - Static head H = **6.0 m**
> - Upstream cut-off depth dŌéü = **3.0 m**
> - Downstream cut-off depth dŌéé = **3.0 m**
> - Total horizontal floor length b = **24.0 m**
> - Lane's coefficient C' = **6.0** (medium sand)
> - Specific gravity of floor concrete G_c = **2.24**
> 
> Determine:
> 1. Creep length ratio ╬╗.
> 2. Residual head at point C (mid-floor), point CŌéü (cut-off face), point D (cut-off bottom), point E (exit).
> 3. Exit gradient G_E and check safety.
> 4. Required downstream floor thickness.

### Step 1: Creep Length Ratio (╬╗)

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{3.0 + 3.0}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m} \\
\lambda &= \frac{b}{d} = \frac{24.0}{3.0} = 8.0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Residual Head at Point C (mid-floor)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_C &= \frac{H}{\pi} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 1}{\lambda + 1}\right) \\
\frac{\lambda - 1}{\lambda + 1} &= \frac{8 - 1}{8 + 1} = \frac{7}{9} = 0.7778 \\
\cos^{-1}(0.7778) &= 0.6797 \text{ rad} \\
\Phi_C &= \frac{6.0}{\pi} \times 0.6797 = 1.298 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Residual Head at Point CŌéü (face of upstream cut-off)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{C_1} &= \frac{H}{\pi} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right) \\
\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda} &= \frac{8 - 2}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75 \\
\cos^{-1}(0.75) &= 0.7227 \text{ rad} \\
\Phi_{C_1} &= \frac{6.0}{\pi} \times 0.7227 = 1.380 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Residual Head at Point D (bottom of cut-off)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_D &= \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right] \\
\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right) &= \sin^{-1}(0.125) = 0.1253 \text{ rad} \\
\frac{\pi}{2} - 0.1253 &= 1.5708 - 0.1253 = 1.4455 \text{ rad} \\
\Phi_D &= \frac{6.0}{\pi} \times 1.4455 = 2.760 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Residual Head at Exit (Point E)

For a floor with two cut-offs, the exit head ╬”_E corresponds to the pressure at the **downstream end of the horizontal floor** (just at the start of the downstream pile):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_E &\approx \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right) \quad \text{(approx., for symmetric)} \\
\Phi_E &= \Phi_{C_1} = 1.380 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> For a more accurate value, Khosla's curves give ╬”_E Ōēł **1.18 m** for ╬╗ = 8.

### Step 6: Exit Gradient Check

$$\begin{aligned}
G_E &= \frac{H}{d \pi \sqrt{\lambda}} \\
\sqrt{\lambda} &= \sqrt{8} = 2.828 \\
G_E &= \frac{6.0}{3.0 \times \pi \times 2.828} = \frac{6.0}{26.65} = 0.225
\end{aligned}$$

**Safe exit gradient:**
$$G_{E,safe} = \frac{1}{C' \pi} = \frac{1}{6.0 \times \pi} = \frac{1}{18.85} = 0.0530$$

**Hmm ŌĆö G_E = 0.225 > G_E,safe = 0.0530 ŌåÆ NOT SAFE.** The design needs **deeper cut-offs** or a **longer floor**.

### Step 7: Redesign ŌĆö Increase dŌéé to make G_E safe

Let us try dŌéé = **6.0 m** (keeping b = 24, dŌéü = 3):

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{eff} &= \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{3 + 6}{2} = 4.5 \text{ m} \\
\lambda &= \frac{24}{4.5} = 5.33 \\
G_E &= \frac{6.0}{6.0 \times \pi \times \sqrt{5.33}} = \frac{6.0}{6.0 \times \pi \times 2.31} = \frac{1}{7.26} = 0.138
\end{aligned}$$

Still high. **Try dŌéé = 8.0 m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{eff} &= \frac{3 + 8}{2} = 5.5 \text{ m} \\
\lambda &= \frac{24}{5.5} = 4.36 \\
G_E &= \frac{6.0}{8.0 \times \pi \times \sqrt{4.36}} = \frac{6.0}{8.0 \times \pi \times 2.088} = \frac{6.0}{52.49} = 0.114
\end{aligned}$$

**Try dŌéé = 12.0 m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_E &= \frac{6.0}{12.0 \times \pi \times \sqrt{(24/7.5)}} \\
d_{eff} &= \frac{3 + 12}{2} = 7.5 \text{ m} \\
\lambda &= \frac{24}{7.5} = 3.2 \\
G_E &= \frac{6.0}{12.0 \times \pi \times \sqrt{3.2}} = \frac{6.0}{12 \times 3.1416 \times 1.789} = \frac{6.0}{67.45} = 0.0890
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> A **deeper downstream cut-off** is the most efficient way to reduce exit gradient. This is **physically intuitive** ŌĆö the cut-off lengthens the seepage path **right at the exit**, which is exactly where the gradient is highest.

### Step 8: Required Floor Thickness (Downstream End)

Using G_c = 2.24 and ╬”_E for the final design (say dŌéé = 12 m, residual exit head ╬”_E Ōēł 0.95 m):

$$t_{min} = \frac{\Phi_E}{G_c - 1} = \frac{0.95}{1.24} = 0.766 \text{ m} \approx 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt t = 0.80 m** at the downstream end.

---

## 3.5 Summary Decision Matrix ŌĆö Bligh vs Khosla

| Aspect | Bligh's Theory | Khosla's Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Empirical | Analytical / Rational |
| Year | 1910 | 1936 |
| Basis | Total creep length (horizontal + vertical equally weighted) | Independent elementary profiles, Schwarz-Christoffel mapping |
| Head distribution | Linear | Non-linear (follows flow net) |
| Vertical cut-off efficiency | Same as horizontal | **3 times** more efficient (Lane's C' rule) |
| Exit gradient | **Cannot compute** | **Directly computed** |
| Floor thickness | Uniform thick slab | Optimized **balanced floor** |
| Application | Obsolete ŌĆö historical use | **Modern design standard** |
| Recommended use | Preliminary / academic | **All professional designs** |
