| 3-mark Part A (1 question) | 3 | Understand, Apply |
| 14-mark Part B (with choice) | 14 ├Ś 1 = 14 | Apply, Analyze, Evaluate |
| **Total** | **ŌĆö** | **ŌĆö** |

(Note: The 3-mark questions in the bank above correspond to the KTU 14-mark question style where the first sub-part is often a 3ŌĆō4 mark conceptual pre-cursor. Final mark distribution as per the 2024 KTU scheme regulation.)

---

## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision List ŌĆö Module 1: Canal Regulators**

### Core Definitions
- ŌśÉ **Regulator** = structure to control discharge and water level in a canal.
- ŌśÉ **Head Regulator** = at off-take; sill raised to exclude silt.
- ŌśÉ **Cross Regulator** = across parent canal; ponds water.
- ŌśÉ **Regulator-cum-Road Bridge** = combined regulator + bridge on common substructure.

### Design Theory
- ŌśÉ **Khosla's Theory** is used (not Bligh's) for modern floor design.
- ŌśÉ **Exit gradient** $G_E$ is the **most critical** design parameter.
- ŌśÉ **Safe $G_E$** for fine sand = 1/6, coarse sand = 1/5, shingle = 1/4.
- ŌśÉ **Khosla's formula:** $G_E = (H/d) \cdot 1/(\pi\sqrt{\lambda})$.
- ŌśÉ **Residual heads** at D and E are computed using $\phi$ formula.
- ŌśÉ **Floor thickness** = $\dfrac{1}{G_s - 1} \left[u_x - \gamma_w h_s\right]$ at every Khosla point.
- ŌśÉ **Minimum floor thickness** = 0.4 m to 0.6 m (RCC) ŌĆö never below 0.3 m.

### Practical / Exam Hints
- ŌśÉ **Afflux** should be **< 0.3 m** in most cases.
- ŌśÉ **Standard gate spans**: 1.0 m, 1.5 m, 2.0 m.
- ŌśÉ **Crest of HR**: keep 0.6 m to 1.0 m above bed.
- ŌśÉ **RBR advantages**: land economy + foundation economy.
- ŌśÉ **Khosla's assumptions**: 2D flow, isotropic soil, horizontal floor, finite depth.
- ŌśÉ **Bligh's coefficient C**: 18 (fine sand), 12 (coarse sand), 5ŌĆō9 (gravel).
- ŌśÉ Always check **stability**: sliding, overturning, base pressure, uplift.

### Common Numerical Errors to Avoid
- ŌśÉ Mixing up $b$ and $2b$ (floor length is $2b$, half-floor is $b$).
- ŌśÉ Forgetting $\pi$ in the exit gradient denominator.
- ŌśÉ Using $\lambda$ instead of $\sqrt{\lambda}$.
- ŌśÉ Confusing **discharge** units (cumecs vs. cusecs ŌĆö KTU uses cumecs).

### Diagrams to Practice
- ŌśÉ Half-sectional elevation of HR with uplift diagram.
- ŌśÉ Khosla floor plan with all points (E, D, A, B, F, G, C).
- ŌśÉ Functional flowchart of regulator system (as in Section 4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions, **always start with a neat sketch** (3ŌĆō4 marks reserved for it). Then proceed with **Khosla calculations** in **tabular form** ŌĆö this signals to the examiner that you are following the systematic design procedure. **End the answer with a clear "DESIGN IS SAFE / NOT SAFE" statement** and **provide remedial measure** if unsafe.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Design and Drawing of the Following Hydraulic Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Design and Drawing of the Following Hydraulic Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hydraulic Design of Structures

**Hydraulic Design** of hydraulic structures refers to the systematic engineering process of proportioning, sizing, and detailing water-retaining or water-handling structures so that they perform their intended function safely, efficiently, and economically under all probable operating conditions, including extreme floods, sustained flows, sediment transport, and seepage forces.

The design integrates principles of **open channel flow**, **fluid mechanics**, **hydrology**, **soil mechanics**, and **structural analysis** to yield a safe geometry that satisfies both **functional** (conveyance, storage, regulation) and **structural** (stability, strength) criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hydraulic design involves the determination of the physical dimensions (plan, profile, and cross-section) of hydraulic structures based on hydraulic parameters such as discharge, head, velocity, energy dissipation, and bed configuration. Drawing refers to the preparation of detailed engineering drawings (plan, elevation, and sectional views) showing all design dimensions.

## 1.2 Scope of Module 2

This module deals with the hydraulic design of the following major structures typically encountered in water resources engineering:

| Sl. No. | Structure | Primary Hydraulic Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Gravity Dam** | Storage and retention of water across a river valley |
| 2 | **Earthen Dam / Embankment Dam** | Storage of water using earth/rockfill materials |
| 3 | **Spillway and Energy Dissipator** | Safe disposal of surplus water downstream |
| 4 | **Diversion Headworks (Barrage / Weir)** | Diversion of river water into a canal |
| 5 | **Cross Drainage Works** | Carrying one waterway over/under another |
| 6 | **Canal Falls and Regulators** | Dissipating excess head / controlling canal flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of hydraulic structures as the "plumbing architecture" of a watershed. Just as a building needs correctly sized pipes, tanks, valves, and traps to deliver water safely, a river system needs dams, spillways, weirs, and falls to manage water safely. **Hydraulic design** is the engineering process of choosing the right "pipe diameter" (or spillway width, weir crest length, fall height) for the expected "flow rate" (flood discharge). **Drawing** is the engineering blueprint that translates the design into construction-ready geometry.

## 1.3 Fundamental Hydraulic Parameters

The design of any hydraulic structure begins with the determination of these key parameters:

- **Design Discharge (Q):** The peak or characteristic flow rate the structure must handle, often expressed in **cubic metres per second (cumecs or m┬│/s)**.
- **Head (H):** The energy head (in **metres, m**) available or to be dissipated.
- **Velocity (V):** Flow velocity in **m/s**, governed by continuity $Q = A \cdot V$.
- **Specific Energy (E):** Energy relative to the channel bed, $E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$.
- **Froude Number (Fr):** Indicator of flow regime, $Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$.
- **Bed Slope (S):** Longitudinal slope of the channel/river bed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **afflux** (rise in upstream water level) caused by a structure and the **hydraulic jump** (transition from supercritical to subcritical flow) are the two most recurring concepts in the design of barrages, weirs, falls, and spillways.

## 1.4 Drawing Requirements (KTU Board Examination Pattern)

A typical KTU drawing sheet for hydraulic structures includes:

1. **Plan View** ŌĆö Top view showing alignment, abutments, and appurtenant works.
2. **Longitudinal Section / Elevation** ŌĆö Section along the flow direction showing crest, floor, cutoff, and energy dissipator.
3. **Cross-Section** ŌĆö Section perpendicular to flow showing piers, gates, crest shape, and floor details.
4. **Key Dimensions** ŌĆö All hydraulic dimensions (crest length, height of fall, stilling basin length, etc.) clearly annotated.
5. **Hydraulic Table** ŌĆö Listing design discharge, head, velocity, afflux, and energy loss values.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, students are often required to **draw to scale** (1:100, 1:200, or 1:500) and **dimension** every hydraulic element. Missing a single critical dimension often results in a 1ŌĆō2 mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Every Equation

The hydraulic design of any structure is governed by a three-tier logic chain:

1. **Determine the design input** ŌåÆ Flood/discharge estimate (using hydrology, often **empirical formulas** or **rational method**).
2. **Apply governing hydraulic equation** ŌåÆ Mostly derived from **Bernoulli's energy equation** and **continuity equation**.
3. **Check stability and functional criteria** ŌåÆ No scour, no overtopping, no excessive afflux, no cavitation.

Every design step in this module is a combination of these three principles.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### A. Gravity Dam ŌĆö Hydraulic Design Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base width ($B$) | $B = \frac{H}{\sqrt{S - 1} \cdot C}$ (approximate) | Simplified formula (Terzaghi), $S$ = specific gravity of dam material |
| Uplift pressure | $U = \frac{\gamma_w \cdot h}{2} \cdot B$ | Triangular distribution assumption |
| Overflow spillway discharge | $Q = C_d \cdot L_e \cdot H_e^{3/2}$ | With $L_e = L - 2(n K_p + K_a)H_e$ |
| Profile of ogee spillway | $X^{n} = K \cdot H_d^{n-1} \cdot Y$ | Standard crest equation (WES profile, $n = 1.85$, $K = 2.0$) |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### B. Spillway and Energy Dissipator

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Discharge over spillway | $Q = C \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | $C = 1.7$ to $2.2$ (ogee crest) |
| Velocity at toe | $V_t = \sqrt{2g \cdot (H + h_a)}$ | Where $h_a$ is the approach depth |
| Sequenced depth (conjugate) | $\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$ | Hydraulic jump equation (B├®langer) |
| Stilling basin length (USBR Type II) | $L_B \approx 4.5 \cdot y_2$ | Based on USBR recommendations |
| Froude Number | $Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$ | If $Fr > 4.5$, jump is well-developed and stable |

### C. Diversion Headworks (Barrage / Weir)

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Discharge (broad-crested weir) | $Q = 1.71 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Francis formula (suppressed weir) |
| Discharge (sharp-crested weir) | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Rehbock formula approximated |
| Afflux | Determined by backwater analysis | Rise in upstream water level |
| Lacey's normal scour depth | $D = 1.34 \cdot \left(\frac{q^2}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | $q$ = discharge intensity, $f$ = silt factor |
| Undersluice discharge | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | Orifice equation with $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ |

### D. Cross Drainage Works

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cross drainage (aqueduct) | Designed as a channel with barrel flow | Barrel acts as a small channel |
| Syphon / Superpassage | Designed for pipe/culvert flow | Uses orifice/weir equations |
| Afflux in cross drainage | Limited to 0.1 m to 0.3 m (KTU norm) | Permissible backwater |

### E. Canal Falls and Regulators

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flumed glacis fall discharge | $Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | $b$ = flumed width, $d$ = depth |
| Notch fall (Cippoletti) | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | Trapezoidal notch with side slope 1H:4V |
| Sarda fall discharge | $Q = 1.84 \cdot (L - 0.2H) \cdot H^{3/2}$ | With end contractions |
| Specific energy at fall | $E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Constant energy through fall |
| Crest of fall | Designed as a broad-crested weir | $Q = 1.71 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic important for a KTU civil engineer?**
> 1. **Dam safety:** Under-designing a spillway leads to overtopping ŌåÆ dam failure (e.g., **Oroville Dam, USA, 2017**).
> 2. **Flood control:** Properly designed barrages and cross-drainage works prevent urban flooding (relevant to **Kerala's recurring floods**).
> 3. **Irrigation efficiency:** Canal falls and regulators maintain command and prevent erosion in the distributary network.
> 4. **Exam relevance:** KTU frequently asks 14-mark problems combining **hydraulic design + drawing** of any one structure.

---

## 2.4 Design Steps (Universal Framework)

The hydraulic design of any structure in this module follows these universal steps:

1. **Step 1:** Fix the **design discharge** ($Q$) and other input data ($H$, bed level, discharge intensity).
2. **Step 2:** Assume trial dimensions (e.g., crest length $L$, crest width, stilling basin depth).
3. **Step 3:** Apply the governing equation to check the assumed dimensions.
4. **Step 4:** Verify **hydraulic jump** conditions and **scour depth**.
5. **Step 5:** Check **uplift, afflux, and stability** (for dams/barrages).
6. **Step 6:** Finalize dimensions, prepare the **drawing**, and annotate all values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Over an Ogee Spillway

An ogee spillway is the most common control structure for gravity dams. Its profile conforms to the lower nappe of a sharp-crested weir with the same design head $H_d$.

**Starting Point ŌĆö Sharp-Crested Weir Equation:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

**Step 1:** Combine the constants. Empirically, for a standard ogee crest, the coefficient $C$ is taken as:

$$C = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.2$$

**Step 2:** The governing practical formula is:

$$Q = C \cdot L_e \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

Where:
- $L_e$ = **effective crest length** = $L - 2(n K_p + K_a) H_e$
- $H_e$ = **effective head** = $H + h_a$ (head over crest + approach velocity head)
- $n$ = number of piers
- $K_p, K_a$ = pier and abutment coefficients
- $L$ = **net crest length**

**Step 3:** Substituting $L_e$:

$$Q = C \cdot \left[ L - 2(n K_p + K_a) H_e \right] \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

**Step 4:** Unit check:

$$Q = (m^{0.5}/s) \cdot (m) \cdot (m^{1.5}) = m^{3}/s = \text{cumecs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The WES (Waterways Experiment Station) standard profile equation is derived by fitting a polynomial to the lower nappe of a sharp-crested weir:
> $$X^{1.85} = 2 \cdot H_d^{0.85} \cdot Y$$
> Where $H_d$ is the design head, $X$ is horizontal coordinate measured from the apex of the crest, and $Y$ is vertical coordinate below the crest.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conjugate Depth (Hydraulic Jump) ŌĆö B├®langer Equation

A hydraulic jump occurs when supercritical flow transitions to subcritical flow, dissipating kinetic energy. The conjugate depth relation is essential for designing stilling basins.

**Starting Point ŌĆö Momentum Equation** (applied across the jump):

$$\frac{Q^2}{g \cdot A_1} + A_1 \cdot \bar{y}_1 = \frac{Q^2}{g \cdot A_2} + A_2 \cdot \bar{y}_2$$

**Step 1:** For a rectangular channel of width $b$:

$$A_1 = b y_1, \quad A_2 = b y_2, \quad \bar{y}_1 = y_1/2, \quad \bar{y}_2 = y_2/2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the momentum equation:

$$\frac{Q^2}{g \cdot b y_1} + \frac{b y_1^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{g \cdot b y_2} + \frac{b y_2^2}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $b/2$:

$$\frac{2 Q^2}{g \cdot b^2 y_1} + y_1^2 = \frac{2 Q^2}{g \cdot b^2 y_2} + y_2^2$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $q = Q/b$ and $Fr_1^2 = V_1^2/(g y_1) = q^2/(g y_1^3)$:

$$\frac{2 q^2}{g y_1} + y_1^2 = \frac{2 q^2}{g y_2} + y_2^2$$

**Step 5:** Using the identity $q^2 = g y_1^3 \cdot Fr_1^2$:

$$2 y_1^2 \cdot Fr_1^2 + y_1^2 = \frac{2 y_1^3 \cdot Fr_1^2}{y_2} + y_2^2$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by dividing by $y_1^2$ and letting $\eta = y_2/y_1$:

$$2 Fr_1^2 + 1 = \frac{2 Fr_1^2}{\eta} + \eta^2$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange to a quadratic in $\eta$:

$$\eta^2 - (2 Fr_1^2 + 1) + 2 Fr_1^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\eta} = 0$$

Multiplying by $\eta$:

$$\eta^3 - (2 Fr_1^2 + 1)\eta + 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$$

**Step 8:** Solving this cubic (by quadratic formula on $\eta^2$ substitution) yields the **B├®langer equation**:

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right)$$

This is the foundational equation for designing **stilling basins**, **falls**, and **barrage floors**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Scour Depth by Lacey's Method

Lacey's regime theory gives the maximum scour depth downstream of a barrage/weir.

**Step 1:** Lacey's normal scour depth formula:

$$D = 1.34 \cdot \left( \frac{q^2}{f} \right)^{1/3}$$

Where:
- $D$ = scour depth below HFL (in **m**)
- $q$ = discharge intensity per unit width = $Q/L$ (in **m┬│/s per m**)
- $f$ = silt factor (dimensionless, often 0.8 to 1.5 for Kerala rivers)

**Step 2:** Maximum scour depth (for design of cutoff):

$$D_{max} = 1.5 \cdot D = 2.01 \cdot \left( \frac{q^2}{f} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3:** Verification of units:

$$\left[ \frac{(m^3/s/m)^2}{1} \right]^{1/3} = \left[ \frac{m^4/s^2}{1} \right]^{1/3} = m^{4/3} / s^{2/3}$$

Multiplying by the constant 1.34 (which absorbs the $s^{2/3}$ factor through Lacey's empirical calibration) gives the result in **metres**.

---

## 3.4 Energy Loss in Hydraulic Jump

The energy loss $\Delta E$ across a hydraulic jump in a rectangular channel:

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \left( y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \right) - \left( y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \right)$$

**Simplified result:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$

This is a useful relation for designing the **depth of the stilling basin** and **apron protection**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choice of Energy Dissipator

| Froude Number ($Fr_1$) | Jump Type | Recommended Dissipator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $1.0 < Fr < 1.7$ | Undular jump | No basin needed (smooth transition) |
| $1.7 \le Fr < 2.5$ | Weak jump | Still acceptable, minor dissipation |
| $2.5 \le Fr < 4.5$ | Oscillating jump | Avoid ŌĆö needs USBR Type III basin |
| $4.5 \le Fr < 9.0$ | Steady jump | **USBR Type II** (best practical range) |
| $Fr \ge 9.0$ | Strong jump | USBR Type IV or forced hydraulic jump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU problems, when the computed $Fr$ lies in the **oscillating range (2.5ŌĆō4.5)**, examiners expect students to **redesign** the structure (e.g., add appurtenances like chute blocks, baffle piers) to avoid this region.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydraulic Design Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **interactions** between the various hydraulic design processes and the typical engineering decisions in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Module 2] --> A1[Collect Input Data: Q, H, Bed Level, Soil]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Type of Structure Required]
    A2 --> B1[Storage Structure]
    A2 --> B2[Conveyance Structure]
    A2 --> B3[Control Structure]
    B1 --> C1[Gravity Dam]
    B1 --> C2[Earthen Dam]
    B2 --> C3[Canal System]
    B2 --> C4[Cross Drainage Works]
    B3 --> C5[Spillway and Energy Dissipator]
    B3 --> C6[Diversion Headworks Barrage or Weir]
    B3 --> C7[Canal Falls and Regulators]
    C1 --> D1[Apply Stability Checks]
    C1 --> D2[Profile Design Ogee Crest]
    C2 --> D3[Seepage and Phreatic Line]
    C3 --> D4[Kennedy or Lacey Design]
    C4 --> D5[Type Selection Aqueduct Syphon]
    C5 --> D6[Stilling Basin Design]
    C6 --> D7[Afflux and Scour Analysis]
    C7 --> D8[Sarda Notch or Flumed Fall]
    D1 --> E1[Final Drawing Plan Elevation Section]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    D7 --> E1
    D8 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[End Hydraulic Design and Drawing]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Components of a Typical Diversion Headworks (Barrage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Upstream River] --> H2[Guide Bund]
    H2 --> H3[Weir or Main Barrage]
    H3 --> H4[Undersluice]
    H4 --> H5[Head Regulator]
    H5 --> H6[Canal Offtake]
    H3 --> H7[Energy Dissipator Stilling Basin]
    H7 --> H8[Downstream River]
    H3 --> H9[Floor with Cutoff]
    H9 --> H10[Impervious Apron]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Energy Dissipator Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Compute Fr1 at Toe] --> J2{Fr1 between 1.0 and 1.7}
    J2 --> J3[Undular Jump: No Basin]
    J1 --> J4{Fr1 between 1.7 and 2.5}
    J4 --> J5[Weak Jump: Mild Basin]
    J1 --> J6{Fr1 between 2.5 and 4.5}
    J6 --> J7[Avoid: Add Appurtenances]
    J1 --> J8{Fr1 between 4.5 and 9.0}
    J8 --> J9[USBR Type II Basin Recommended]
    J1 --> J10{Fr1 greater than 9.0}
    J10 --> J11[USBR Type IV or Forced Jump]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Block of a Cross-Drainage Work

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Identify Two Waterways Crossing] --> K2{Natural Stream Above Canal}
    K2 --> K3[Stream is Higher]
    K2 --> K4[Stream is Lower]
    K3 --> K5[Superpassage]
    K4 --> K6[Syphon Aqueduct]
    K1 --> K7{Canal Above Natural Stream}
    K7 --> K8[Canal is Higher]
    K7 --> K9[Canal is Lower]
    K8 --> K10[Aqueduct]
    K9 --> K11[Canal Syphon]
    K1 --> K12{Equal Levels}
    K12 --> K13[Level Crossing]
```

---

## 4.5 Plan + Section Layout Convention (For Drawing Sheets)

| Drawing Element | What to Show | KTU Marking Emphasis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Plan** | Alignment, piers, abutments, gates, divide walls | Width of structure, pier thickness |
| **Longitudinal Section** | Crest, downstream apron, energy dissipator, cutoff | Total length, floor depths, jump location |
| **Cross Section** | Pier shape, gate slot, crest profile | Gate dimensions, crest shape |
| **Hydraulic Table** | All computed Q, H, V, yŌéü, yŌéé, Fr values | Most KTU questions mandate this table |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**Define the term "afflux" as applied to diversion headworks. What is its typical permissible value in the design of a barrage in India?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Afflux** is the rise in the upstream water level (HFL) of a river caused by the construction of a weir or barrage across it. It is the difference between the **pre-construction HFL** and the **post-construction HFL** at a given upstream section.

Mathematically: $Afflux = HFL_{upstream}^{post} - HFL_{upstream}^{pre}$.

Typical permissible value in Indian practice (CPWD / IS design norms) is **0.1 m to 0.3 m**, with the upper limit of about **0.5 m** considered the maximum acceptable rise to avoid flooding of upstream property.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**What is a hydraulic jump? Under what conditions is it stable and useful for energy dissipation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied flow phenomenon in open channels where the flow transitions abruptly from **supercritical** (high velocity, shallow depth) to **subcritical** (low velocity, greater depth) state.

A hydraulic jump is **stable and useful for energy dissipation** when the upstream Froude number $Fr_1$ is in the range **4.5 to 9.0** (steady jump, USBR Type II basin). In this range:
- The jump stays in a fixed position relative to the structure.
- Energy dissipation is 45% to 70% of the incoming energy.
- The conjugate depth ratio $y_2/y_1$ is well-defined by B├®langer's equation.

If $Fr_1 < 4.5$ (oscillating jump), the jump is unstable and may wander, causing damage. If $Fr_1 > 9.0$ (strong jump), the jump is rough but practical designs need appurtenances.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the effective crest length formula** $L_e = L - 2(nK_p + K_a)H_e$ ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**
> 2. **Not checking the Froude number** before designing the stilling basin ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**
> 3. **Missing the hydraulic table** in the drawing ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**
> 4. **Not drawing the hydraulic jump profile** in the stilling basin ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**
> 5. **Confusing $H$ (head over crest) with $H_d$ (design head of ogee profile)** ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**
> 6. **Forgetting units** in intermediate steps ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Design of an Ogee Spillway with Stilling Basin**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / Model Paper Module 2]**

A concrete gravity dam has a spillway with the following data:
- Design discharge, $Q = 4500$ m┬│/s
- Length of spillway, $L = 60$ m
- Number of piers, $n = 4$ (pier coefficient $K_p = 0.02$)
- Abutment coefficient, $K_a = 0.10$
- Height of spillway crest above downstream floor = 14 m
- Bed level downstream of dam = RL 100.0 m
- Manning's roughness $n = 0.016$ (for tailwater calculation)
- Tailwater rating curve (approximate): $y_t = 0.05 \cdot Q^{0.5}$ where $Q$ is in m┬│/s and $y_t$ is tailwater depth in m

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Design the ogee spillway crest profile and determine the head over the crest. Assume a coefficient of discharge $C = 2.0$.**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Check the formation of the hydraulic jump at the toe and design the length and depth of the USBR Type II stilling basin.**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Apply the ogee spillway discharge equation:

$$Q = C \cdot L_e \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

**Step 2:** Effective crest length:

$$L_e = L - 2(n K_p + K_a) H_e$$

$$L_e = 60 - 2(4 \times 0.02 + 0.10) H_e$$

$$L_e = 60 - 2(0.08 + 0.10) H_e = 60 - 0.36 H_e$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the discharge equation:

$$4500 = 2.0 \cdot (60 - 0.36 H_e) \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

$$4500 = (120 - 0.72 H_e) \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve by trial. Try $H_e = 4$ m:

$$(120 - 0.72 \times 4) \times 4^{1.5} = (120 - 2.88) \times 8 = 117.12 \times 8 = 936.96 \text{ (too low)}$$

Try $H_e = 8$ m:

$$(120 - 0.72 \times 8) \times 8^{1.5} = (120 - 5.76) \times 22.627 = 114.24 \times 22.627 = 2584.2 \text{ (low)}$$

Try $H_e = 12$ m:

$$(120 - 0.72 \times 12) \times 12^{1.5} = (120 - 8.64) \times 41.569 = 111.36 \times 41.569 = 4629.7 \text{ (slightly high)}$$

Try $H_e = 11.7$ m:

$$(120 - 0.72 \times 11.7) \times 11.7^{1.5} = (120 - 8.424) \times 40.0 = 111.576 \times 40 = 4463.0 \text{ (close)}$$

**Step 5:** Refine: $H_e \approx 11.75$ m ŌåÆ $Q \approx 4500$ m┬│/s Ō£ō

**Step 6:** Crest profile (WES standard):

$$X^{1.85} = 2 \cdot H_d^{0.85} \cdot Y$$

For the **design head** (typically $H_d = 1.0$ to $1.2 \times H_e$ as the maximum expected head), take $H_d = H_e = 11.75$ m.

For plotting, compute a few points:

| $X$ (m) | $Y$ (m) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.5 | 0.025 |
| 1.0 | 0.092 |
| 2.0 | 0.338 |
| 3.0 | 0.728 |
| 5.0 | 1.91 |
| 7.0 | 3.61 |
| 10.0 | 7.05 |

**Result:** Head over crest $H_e \approx 11.75$ m, crest profile coordinates as above.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Calculate the velocity at the toe of the spillway (energy conservation from upstream to toe):

$$V_t = \sqrt{2g(H + h_a)}$$

Where $H = 14$ m (fall height) and $h_a$ = approach depth above crest. For simplicity, take $h_a \approx 0.1 H = 1.4$ m.

$$V_t = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times (14 + 1.4)} = \sqrt{302.05} \approx 17.38 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Sequenced depth at the toe (assume rectangular section, sequential depth $y_1$ using the energy equation at the toe):

Using specific energy approach, with $V_t = 17.38$ m/s, $E_t = y_1 + V_t^2/(2g) = 14 + 1.4 = 15.4$ m.

$$y_1 + \frac{17.38^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 15.4$$

$$y_1 + 15.4 = 15.4$$

This gives $y_1 \approx 1.0$ m (very small sequent depth, characteristic of steep fall).

More accurately, with $Fr_1$ calculation:

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_t}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}} = \frac{17.38}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.0}} = \frac{17.38}{3.132} \approx 5.55$$

Since $Fr_1 = 5.55$ lies in the range $4.5 < Fr < 9.0$, the jump is **steady and stable** Ō£ō

**Step 3:** Compute conjugate depth $y_2$ using B├®langer's equation:

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 5.55^2} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left( \sqrt{1 + 246.4} - 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \sqrt{247.4} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2}(15.73 - 1) = 7.36$$

$$y_2 = 7.36 \times 1.0 = 7.36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Check tailwater depth from the rating curve:

$$y_t = 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{4500} = 0.05 \times 67.08 = 3.354 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Since $y_2 = 7.36$ m $>$ $y_t = 3.35$ m, the jump is **swept downstream** (drowned jump). To ensure a stable jump inside the basin, raise the apron level so that the effective tailwater matches $y_2$.

**Step 6:** Required raising of apron:

$$\Delta = y_2 - y_t = 7.36 - 3.35 = 4.01 \text{ m}$$

Provide a depressed stilling basin floor at **4.0 m below the original downstream bed level**.

**Step 7:** Stilling basin length (USBR Type II):

$$L_B \approx 4.5 \cdot y_2 = 4.5 \times 7.36 = 33.1 \text{ m}$$

Adopt $L_B = 33$ m.

**Step 8:** Provide **chute blocks** (height $\approx y_1$, spacing $\approx y_1$) at the entrance and **baffle piers** (height $\approx 0.8 y_2$) within the basin for USBR Type II.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Design of a Diversion Headworks (Barrage)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 / Model Paper Module 2]**

A barrage is proposed across a river with the following data:
- Maximum flood discharge, $Q = 3000$ m┬│/s
- High flood level (HFL) before construction = RL 105.0 m
- Low water level (LWL) = RL 99.0 m
- River bed level = RL 96.0 m
- Permissible afflux = 0.3 m
- Silt factor, $f = 1.0$
- Coefficient of discharge for barrage crest, $C = 1.7$

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Determine the crest level of the barrage and the effective waterway. Assume a maximum flood intensity of 12 m┬│/s/m for the design.**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Calculate the scour depth using Lacey's formula and design the depth of the downstream cutoff.**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute the effective waterway (length of barrage):

$$L = \frac{Q}{q_{intensity}} = \frac{3000}{12} = 250 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Adopt a practical waterway by dividing the barrage into bays with piers. With bay width = 12 m and pier thickness = 2 m, number of bays = 20, total crest length = $20 \times 12 + 21 \times 2 = 240 + 42 = 282$ m. For design simplicity, we treat the effective waterway as 250 m (piers and gates may reduce net).

**Step 3:** For the crest level, use the broad-crested weir equation with the permissible afflux of 0.3 m (the afflux equals the head H over the crest in a simplified sense):

$$Q = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$3000 = 1.7 \times 250 \times H^{3/2}$$

$$H^{3/2} = \frac{3000}{425} = 7.0588$$

$$H = 7.0588^{2/3} = (7.0588)^{0.6667}$$

Taking log: $\log H = 0.6667 \times \log(7.0588) = 0.6667 \times 0.8488 = 0.5659$

$$H = 10^{0.5659} = 3.68 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Crest level of barrage:

$$\text{Crest level} = \text{HFL}_{pre} - H = 105.0 - 3.68 = 101.32 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Adopt **crest level = RL 101.30 m** (rounded to nearest 5 cm).

**Step 6:** Post-construction HFL = 101.30 + 3.68 = 104.98 m Ōēł 105.0 m ŌåÆ afflux Ōēł 0 (since H is the head available, and HFL remains at 105.0).

> **Note:** Strictly, with pre-construction HFL = 105.0 m and a 0.3 m afflux, the post-construction HFL = 105.3 m, but the head over the crest equals 105.3 ŌłÆ 101.32 = 3.98 m. The simplified calculation above is the standard KTU approach.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Lacey's normal scour depth:

$$D = 1.34 \cdot \left( \frac{q^2}{f} \right)^{1/3}$$

Where $q = Q/L = 3000/250 = 12$ m┬│/s/m.

$$D = 1.34 \cdot \left( \frac{12^2}{1.0} \right)^{1/3} = 1.34 \cdot (144)^{1/3}$$

$$(144)^{1/3} = 5.241$$

$$D = 1.34 \times 5.241 = 7.02 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum scour depth (1.5 ├Ś normal):

$$D_{max} = 1.5 \times 7.02 = 10.53 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Depth of downstream cutoff below the bed level:

Adopt $D_{cutoff} = 1.5 \times D = 10.5$ m below the downstream bed level (which is at RL 96.0 m).

$$\text{Cutoff bottom level} = 96.0 - 10.5 = 85.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, the **upstream cutoff** is typically designed at $1.0 \times D$ to $1.25 \times D$:

Adopt $D_{u/s} = 1.25 \times 7.02 = 8.78$ m Ōēł 8.8 m

$$\text{U/S cutoff bottom level} = 96.0 - 8.8 = 87.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Provide an impervious floor of length approximately $2 \times D = 14$ m on the upstream side and $2.5 \times D$ to $3 \times D$ on the downstream side.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2)**

### Core Hydraulic Equations
- Ō£ģ **Ogee spillway:** $Q = C \cdot L_e \cdot H_e^{3/2}$, with $L_e = L - 2(nK_p + K_a)H_e$
- Ō£ģ **B├®langer's equation:** $\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$
- Ō£ģ **Energy loss in jump:** $\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$
- Ō£ģ **Lacey's scour depth:** $D = 1.34 \left( q^2 / f \right)^{1/3}$
- Ō£ģ **USBR Type II basin length:** $L_B \approx 4.5 \cdot y_2$
- Ō£ģ **Broad-crested weir:** $Q = 1.71 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$
- Ō£ģ **Cippoletti notch:** $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$
- Ō£ģ **Sarda fall:** $Q = 1.84 \cdot (L - 0.2H) \cdot H^{3/2}$

### Critical Design Constraints
- Ō£ģ **Stable jump range:** $Fr_1 = 4.5$ to $9.0$ ŌåÆ USBR Type II basin
- Ō£ģ **Avoid oscillating range:** $Fr_1 = 2.5$ to $4.5$ ŌåÆ requires appurtenances
- Ō£ģ **Permissible afflux:** 0.1 m to 0.3 m (KTU/IS norm)
- Ō£ģ **Maximum scour factor:** $1.5 \times$ normal scour depth
- Ō£ģ **Design head of ogee profile:** $H_d \approx 1.0$ to $1.2 \times H_e$ (maximum expected)

### Drawing Sheet Requirements
- Ō£ģ Plan + Longitudinal Section + Cross-Section + Hydraulic Table
- Ō£ģ All dimensions annotated in **metres**
- Ō£ģ Crest profile traced (for ogee) using WES coordinates
- Ō£ģ Hydraulic jump profile shown in stilling basin
- Ō£ģ Cutoff depths marked on both upstream and downstream sides
- Ō£ģ Pier thickness and bay width clearly shown

### Common KTU Mark-Loss Areas
- ŌØī Forgetting to compute $L_e$ (effective length) ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ2 marks**
- ŌØī Drawing the spillway crest as a sharp angle instead of smooth ogee ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ1 mark**
- ŌØī Missing the **hydraulic table** with all computed values ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ1 mark**
- ŌØī Not checking whether the jump forms within the basin ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ2 marks**
- ŌØī Confusing $H$ (head over crest) and $H_d$ (design head for ogee profile) ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ2 marks**
- ŌØī Forgetting Lacey's silt factor in scour depth calculation ŌĆö penalty: **ŌłÆ1 mark**

### Kerala-Specific Context
- ­¤īŖ Most Kerala rivers have **silt factor $f \approx 0.8$ to $1.2$** (alluvial sandy-silty beds).
- ­¤īŖ High-intensity rainfall demands **higher flood factors** in design discharges.
- ­¤īŖ Permissible afflux in **low-lying paddy areas** of Kerala is strictly **0.1 m to 0.2 m**.
- ­¤īŖ USBR Type II basins are the most common energy dissipators in Idukki, Mullaperiyar-type dams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tank Sluice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tank Sluice # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Tank Sluice** (also called a **body sluice** or **irrigation sluice**) is a small regulatory hydraulic structure constructed through the earthen embankment (bund) of a tank (a small storage reservoir) to convey stored water to the command area (irrigation fields) under controlled conditions. It essentially functions as a **controlled outlet conduit** that operates under low to moderate heads, drawing water from the tank's storage and discharging it into the irrigation distribution system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A tank sluice is classified as a **minor irrigation structure** under the *Kerala State Irrigation Manual* and follows design philosophy similar to canal falls and regulators, but on a much smaller scale with headwater depths typically ranging from **1.5 m to 4.0 m**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large water storage tank on a rooftop** (the irrigation tank), and a **pipe coming out of its lower side** (the sluice barrel), connected to a **tap (the gate)** that can be opened or closed to release water into the **downstream tap connections (the field channel)**. The water level inside the rooftop tank is constantly changing, but the pipe ensures water reaches the crops only when needed. A tank sluice is precisely this setup ŌĆö but built in massive earth mounds (bunds) of an irrigation tank! - The **bund (embankment)** = the wall of the storage - The **sluice barrel (conduit pipe)** = the controlled pipe - The **gate (vertical lift shutter)** = the tap - The **field channel (distributary)** = the downstream irrigation system ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Range (KTU Practice) | |-----------|-------------------------------| | Design Discharge | **0.05 to 3.0 cumecs** | | Barrel Diameter/Width | **0.6 m to 1.5 m** | | Headwater Depth | **1.5 m to 4.0 m** | | Barrel Velocity | **1.5 m/s to 2.5 m/s** | | Floor Thickness | **0.30 m to 0.60 m** | > [!NOTE] > **Why is it important?** Without a tank sluice, water from a tank cannot be regulated for irrigation. The sluice is the **lifeline outlet** that converts stored water into productive irrigation supply. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Tank Sluices Tank sluices are broadly classified based on their function and hydraulic behavior: 1. **Head Sluice (Direct Outlet Sluice):** Located at the lowest point of the tank bund; releases water directly into the main canal/field channel. Operates under **submerged/sometimes free-flow** conditions. 2. **Surplus Sluice (Escape Sluice):** Built on a saddle/spill section of the bund to release excess water during over-topping situations. 3. **By-wash Sluice:** Combines surplus water removal with channel feeding, often used in chain-of-tank systems. > [!TIP] > For KTU exam purposes, the term *"Tank Sluice"* without qualification **almost always refers to the Head Sluice**, and the typical design question revolves around the hydraulic design of the barrel, apron, and floor protection. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Tank Sluice ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic A typical tank sluice consists of the following structural and hydraulic elements. Each is critical to the safe, efficient conveyance of water: ### (a) **Head Regulator (Upstream Structure)** - Includes the **entrance bell-mouth** or **square-edged entry** with properly designed wing walls. - Wing walls retain the bund earth and provide a smooth approach flow. - A **screen (trash rack)** prevents debris from entering the barrel. - Stilling well provisions upstream stabilize flow and reduce turbulence. ### (b) **Barrel (Conduit Section)** - The **flow-carrying passage** through the bund, typically rectangular or circular (box culvert type). - Acts as a **pipe flow conduit** under pressure for small sluices, or as a free-flow channel for larger ones. - Generally constructed in **RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete)** or masonry for small tanks. ### (c) **Gate / Shutter Arrangement** - **Vertical lift gate (sluice gate)** is the most common. - Operated manually for small sluices, or via screw-gear hoists for larger ones. - May be a single-leaf or multi-leaf gate depending on head and discharge. ### (d) **Downstream Floor (Apron) and Energy Dissipator** - A **horizontal impervious floor** of RCC or brick masonry in cement mortar extends downstream of the barrel exit. - Prevents **piping / undermining** due to seepage uplift. - The **hydraulic jump** (if supercritical flow exits) is suppressed using a **rubble masonry apron, baffle wall, or toe wall**. ### (e) **Cut-off Walls & Sheet Piles** - Provided at the entry and exit of the barrel to extend the seepage path. - Critical for **uplift control** under the floor ŌĆö KTU frequently tests this. ## 2.2 The "Why & How" of Each Component | Component | **Why** It Is Needed | **How** It Functions | |-----------|---------------------|----------------------| | Wing walls | Retain earth of the bund at entry | Transfers lateral earth pressure to foundation | | Trash rack | Prevent clogging | Mesh bars reject floating debris | | Barrel | Carry water through the bund | Provides a controlled, low-loss passage | | Gate | Regulate discharge | Adjusts effective flow area | | Downstream apron | Resist uplift & scour | Distributes uplift pressure over larger area | | Cut-off wall | Increase seepage path | Forces longer percolation path, reducing exit gradient | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **must-memorize** arsenal for any tank sluice problem in the KTU examination. Every formula below is directly applicable. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Discharge through sluice (orifice flow) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | cumecs | $C_d \approx 0.62$ (sluice gate) | | 2 | Discharge (weir flow, gate fully open) | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \cdot b \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | cumecs | $C_d \approx 0.62$ to $0.66$ | | 3 | Velocity in barrel | $V = Q/A$ | m/s | Should be $\le 2.5$ m/s | | 4 | Head loss (entry) | $h_e = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Square-edged entry, $K_e = 0.5$ | | 5 | Head loss (sudden contraction) | $h_c = 0.5 \left(1-\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | m | Standard formula | | 6 | Head loss (sudden expansion) | $h_{exp} = \dfrac{(V_1-V_2)^2}{2g}$ | m | BordaŌĆōCarnot loss | | 7 | Head loss (friction, DarcyŌĆōWeisbach) | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | $f \approx 0.02$ for RCC | | 8 | Manning's head loss (open channel flow) | $h_f = \dfrac{V^2 n^2 L}{R^{4/3}}$ | m | $n = 0.014$ for RCC | | 9 | Uplift pressure (Bligh's creep theory) | $\phi = \dfrac{H}{L} \cdot 100\%$ | % | $L$ = total creep length | | 10 | Residual uplift at distance $x$ | $p_x = H \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right)$ | m of water | Linear distribution (Bligh) | | 11 | Floor thickness (Bligh) | $t = \dfrac{p_x}{\rho_s - 1}$ (in m) | m | $\rho_s = 2.24$ (concrete) | | 12 | Khosla's exit gradient | $G_E = \dfrac{H}{d} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = (1 + \sqrt{1+\alpha^2})/2$ | | 13 | Floor thickness (Khosla) | $t = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{H \cdot G_E}{\rho_s - 1}$ | m | Practice: use $2/3$ of $G_E$ | | 14 | Afflux at sluice | $A_f = H - h$ | m | Difference in upstream/downstream depths | | 15 | Specific energy at exit | $E = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Used to determine flow profile | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TIP:** For a tank sluice, the **upstream water depth ($H$) is small (1.5ŌĆō4.0 m)**, so the hydraulic jump formation is often weak or partially drowned. Many KTU problems simplify this as **subcritical exit flow** with no jump. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Tank sluices are vital in: - **Irrigation engineering** (small/medium tanks in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh). - **Watershed management** projects. - **Drought-proofing** of rain-fed agricultural regions. - **Flood routing** in cascade tank systems (e.g., *Kuttanad below sea-level farming* in Kerala). In professional practice, tank sluices are designed per the **IS 7784 (Code of Practice for Design of Cross Drainage Works)** and **IS 4997 (Criteria for Design of Hydraulic Jump Type Stilling Basins)**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Discharge Through a Tank Sluice (Submerged Orifice) **Scenario:** The gate is partially open, the barrel is fully submerged upstream, and free flow occurs downstream. **Assumptions:** - Steady, incompressible flow. - Head $H$ above the centroid of the opening. - Coefficient of discharge $C_d$ accounts for contraction and viscous effects. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream free water surface (point 1) and the vena contracta of the jet (point 2): $$ P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2 $$ Both points are at atmospheric pressure, so $P_1 = P_2 = 0$. The upstream velocity $V_1$ is negligible (large tank reservoir). Take $z_1 - z_2 = H$: $$ 0 + 0 + \rho g H = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_t^2 + 0 $$ Solve for the **theoretical velocity** $V_t$: $$ V_t = \sqrt{2gH} $$ The **actual velocity** at the vena contracta is reduced by the coefficient of velocity $C_v$, and the **actual area** is the contracted area $A_c = C_c \cdot A$: $$ V_{actual} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH} $$ The **discharge** is then: $$ Q = A_c \cdot V_{actual} = (C_c A) \cdot (C_v \sqrt{2gH}) $$ Define the **coefficient of discharge**: $$ C_d = C_c \cdot C_v $$ Therefore, the standard orifice flow equation: $$ Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH} $$ For a sluice opening of width $b$ and opening height $a$: $$ Q = C_d \cdot (b \cdot a) \cdot \sqrt{2gH} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Floor Thickness Using Bligh's Creep Theory **Scenario:** Seepage water flows beneath the floor of a tank sluice under a head difference $H$. Determine the floor thickness required to counter uplift pressure. **Assumption:** The hydraulic gradient is linear along the total creep length $L$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1:** Define the **hydraulic gradient** $i$: $$ i = \frac{H}{L} $$ This represents the head loss per unit length of creep. **Step 2:** At any distance $x$ from the upstream end of the floor, the **residual head** (uplift pressure head) is: $$ h_x = H - i \cdot x = H - \frac{H \cdot x}{L} = H \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) $$ **Step 3:** The **uplift pressure** at that point (in metres of water column): $$ p_x = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_x = \gamma_w \cdot H \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) $$ **Step 4:** For equilibrium of the floor slab, the **weight of the floor** must equal or exceed the **uplift pressure**. The required floor thickness $t$ at distance $x$ is: $$ \gamma_c \cdot t = \gamma_w \cdot h_x $$ Therefore: $$ t = \frac{\gamma_w}{\gamma_c} \cdot h_x = \frac{h_x}{\rho_s} $$ where $\rho_s$ is the relative density of floor material ($\approx 2.24$ for plain concrete, $\approx 2.4$ for RCC). **Step 5:** Substituting the uplift head expression: $$ t = \frac{H}{\rho_s} \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) $$ **The critical section is at the exit end** ($x = L$ gives $h_x = 0$, but actually the **maximum uplift** is at the entry). The **most critical** section is where uplift is highest, i.e., at $x = 0$ (entry): $$ t_{max} = \frac{H}{\rho_s - 1} \quad \text{(when submergence correction is applied)} $$ The **subtraction of 1** comes from balancing against submerged unit weight (since floor is below the downstream water table). The standard design formula is: $$ \boxed{t = \frac{p_x}{\rho_s - 1}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Khosla's Exit Gradient for a Tank Sluice Floor **Scenario:** Refined approach using Khosla's theory (more accurate than Bligh's for non-homogeneous foundations). **Step 1:** Define the **hydraulic parameters**: - $H$ = static head causing seepage - $d$ = depth of downstream cut-off wall - $b$ = floor length - $\alpha = b/d$ (floor length ratio) **Step 2:** Compute the **Khosla constant** $\lambda$: $$ \lambda = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}{2} $$ **Step 3:** The **exit gradient** is the gradient at the downstream end of the floor (the most critical point): $$ G_E = \frac{H}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}} $$ **Step 4:** For **safe design**, the exit gradient must satisfy: $$ G_E \le \frac{1}{C_s} $$ where $C_s$ is the **specific gravity of the soil particles** (typically $C_s \approx 2.65$ for sand, $\approx 2.70$ for gravel). **Step 5:** Floor thickness from Khosla's theory (using $2/3$ rule for safe practice): $$ t = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{H \cdot G_E}{\rho_s - 1} $$ > [!TIP] > **Why "2/3"?** Khosla recommended using only $2/3$ of the theoretical exit gradient as a **safety factor** because exit gradient is computed for a simplified 2-D case, while real foundations are 3-D. ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Practice Problem) **Problem:** Design the floor thickness of a tank sluice floor for the following data: - Head difference $H = 2.5$ m - Floor length $L = 12$ m - Floor material: plain concrete ($\rho_s = 2.24$) - Use Bligh's creep theory. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the hydraulic gradient: $$ i = \frac{H}{L} = \frac{2.5}{12} = 0.208 $$ **Step 2:** Compute the uplift head at the **entry section** ($x = 0$): $$ h_0 = H = 2.5 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3:** Floor thickness at entry: $$ t_0 = \frac{h_0}{\rho_s - 1} = \frac{2.5}{2.24 - 1} = \frac{2.5}{1.24} = 2.016 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4:** Floor thickness at mid-floor ($x = L/2 = 6$ m): $$ h_6 = 2.5 \left(1 - \frac{6}{12}\right) = 2.5 \times 0.5 = 1.25 \text{ m} $$ $$ t_6 = \frac{1.25}{1.24} = 1.008 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5:** Floor thickness at exit ($x = 12$ m): $$ h_{12} = 2.5 \left(1 - \frac{12}{12}\right) = 0 \text{ m} $$ $$ t_{12} = 0 $$ **Step 6:** Provide a **practical minimum** of $0.3$ m to avoid zero thickness. So exit thickness $t_{12} = 0.3$ m (adopt minimum). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Design Approach:** The actual floor is constructed with a **uniform thickness** (avg. $\approx 0.6$ŌĆō$1.0$ m for small tank sluices) to avoid variable construction complexity. Use the **maximum computed thickness** as the design thickness. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Bligh vs. Khosla | Criterion | Bligh's Theory | Khosla's Theory | |-----------|----------------|-----------------| | **Foundation** | Homogeneous soil | Stratified/non-homogeneous | | **Approach** | Empirical, simple | Analytical, rigorous | | **Path assumption** | Weighted creep length | Streamlines + equipotentials | | **Floor profile** | Linear uplift | Non-linear uplift | | **Exit gradient** | Not directly given | Directly computed | | **Accuracy** | Conservative | More realistic | | **KTU exam preference** | Common in 3-mark Qs | Standard in 14-mark Qs | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Tank Sluice System The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of water through a tank sluice and the interaction of its components. This serves as a high-level schematic for KTU exam sketches. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Tank Reservoir
Upstream Storage] --> B[Trash Rack
Debris Removal] B --> C[Entrance Bell Mouth
Smooth Entry] C --> D[Sluice Gate
Vertical Lift Shutter] D --> E[Barrel Conduit
RCC Box Section] E --> F[Downstream Apron
RCC Floor] F --> G[Toe Wall
Cut-off Barrier] G --> H[Field Channel
Irrigation Distributary] F -.-> I[Seepage Path
Beneath Floor] I --> J[Weep Holes
Relief Pressure] J --> K[Filter Media
Sand Gravel Layer] ``` ## 4.2 Sectional Elevation of a Typical Tank Sluice ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UPSTREAM["UPSTREAM SIDE TANK"] W1[Full Supply Level FSL]:::level end subgraph SLUICE["SLUICE STRUCTURE IN BUND"] S1[Wing Wall Entry]:::struct S2[Barrel Length L]:::struct S3[Gate Hoist Platform]:::struct S4[Wing Wall Exit]:::struct end subgraph DOWNSTREAM["DOWNSTREAM SIDE CHANNEL"] W2[Tail Water Level TWL]:::level A[Impervious Apron]:::struct TW[Toe Wall Cut off]:::struct end UPSTREAM --> SLUICE --> DOWNSTREAM classDef level fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef struct fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Component Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Water Enters Sluice]) --> EntryHydraulics[Entry Head Loss He] EntryHydraulics --> BarrelFlow[Barrel Flow under Pressure] BarrelFlow --> GateRegulation[Gate Controls Discharge Q] GateRegulation --> FrictionLoss[Friction Loss Hf in Barrel] FrictionLoss --> ExitHydraulics[Exit Velocity Head V squared 2g] ExitHydraulics --> UpliftCheck{Floor Uplift Check} UpliftCheck --> Safe[Safe Design] UpliftCheck --> Unsafe[Increase Floor Thickness] Unsafe --> Redesign[Add Cut off Wall] Redesign --> Safe Safe --> End([Water Discharged to Channel]) ``` ## 4.4 Hydrological-Hydraulic Decision Tree (Design Process) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Design] --> B[Determine Design Discharge Q] B --> C[Choose Barrel Velocity V 1.5 to 2.5 m per sec] C --> D[Compute Barrel Area A Q by V] D --> E[Choose Barrel Dimensions b by d] E --> F[Verify Barrel Length via Bund Width] F --> G[Compute Uplift via Bligh or Khosla] G --> H[Determine Floor Thickness t] H --> I[Check Exit Gradient G E] I --> J{GE Safe?} J -->|Yes| K[Finalize Floor Design] J -->|No| L[Add Cut off Wall or Increase Floor Length] L --> G K --> End([Design Complete]) ``` > [!TIP] > **For KTU Exam Diagrams:** Always draw a clean **sectional elevation** of the sluice showing the FSL, gate, barrel, apron, TWL, and cut-off walls. Label all hydraulic parameters ($H$, $L$, $V$, $h_f$, etc.). A well-labeled sketch often earns **2ŌĆō3 easy marks** even if numerical work has minor errors. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **PART A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions** ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)] **Define a tank sluice. Mention its principal components.** **Model Answer:** A **tank sluice** is a small regulatory hydraulic structure constructed through the earthen embankment (bund) of an irrigation tank to convey stored water to the command area in a controlled manner. **Principal components:** 1. **Wing walls** (entry and exit) ŌĆō retain the bund earth and guide flow. 2. **Trash rack** ŌĆō prevents floating debris from entering the barrel. 3. **Sluice gate (vertical lift shutter)** ŌĆō regulates discharge. 4. **Barrel (conduit)** ŌĆō carries water through the bund. 5. **Impervious floor (apron)** ŌĆō resists uplift pressure and scour. 6. **Cut-off walls / sheet piles** ŌĆō increase seepage path length. 7. **Hoist / operating platform** ŌĆō for gate operation. *(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for components ŌĆö name any 4 for full credit.)* --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)] **Differentiate between a tank sluice and a surplus weir in a tank scheme.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Tank Sluice | Surplus Weir | |--------|-------------|--------------| | **Function** | Releases stored water for irrigation | Discharges excess water during over-topping | | **Location** | Through the bund, at lowest point | On a saddle/elevated section of bund | | **Head** | Operates under full FSL head | Operates only when water rises above FSL | | **Control** | Gated, manually operated | Uncontrolled (free overflow) | | **Hydraulic type** | Orifice/pipe flow | Weir flow | | **Discharge range** | 0.05 ŌĆō 3.0 cumecs | Variable, often large in floods | --- ## **PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]** **A tank sluice is to be designed for the following data:** - Design discharge: $Q = 2.0$ cumecs - Headwater depth above the sluice invert: $H = 3.0$ m - Barrel length: $L_b = 10$ m - Width of the sluice opening: $b = 1.2$ m - Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = 0.62$ **Answer the following:** **(a)** Determine the **height of the sluice opening** required to pass the design discharge. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the **head loss in the barrel** due to friction using Manning's formula, and the **floor thickness** at the entry using Bligh's theory. Take Manning's $n = 0.014$, relative density of concrete $\rho_s = 2.24$. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A** #### **Part (a): Height of Sluice Opening** **Step 1:** Use the orifice flow equation for a submerged sluice: $$ Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH} $$ **Step 2:** Compute $\sqrt{2gH}$: $$ \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.0} = \sqrt{58.86} = 7.672 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange the orifice equation to find $A$: $$ A = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{2.0}{0.62 \times 7.672} = \frac{2.0}{4.757} = 0.4205 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the height of opening $a$: $$ a = \frac{A}{b} = \frac{0.4205}{1.2} = 0.350 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5:** Adopt a practical height of $a = 0.40$ m (round up for construction tolerance). Recompute the actual discharge with $a = 0.40$ m: $$ Q_{actual} = 0.62 \times (1.2 \times 0.40) \times 7.672 = 0.62 \times 0.48 \times 7.672 = 2.283 \text{ cumecs} $$ Since $Q_{actual} > Q_{design}$, the design is safe. > **Answer (a):** Height of opening $a = 0.40$ m (adopted); theoretical requirement is 0.35 m. --- #### **Part (b): Head Loss in Barrel and Floor Thickness** **Step 1:** Compute the actual velocity in the barrel with adopted opening: $$ V = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A} = \frac{2.283}{0.48} = 4.756 \text{ m/s} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Note:** $V = 4.756$ m/s is **higher than the recommended limit** of 2.5 m/s. This indicates that the barrel area needs to be increased. Let us redesign using a wider opening $b = 1.5$ m, keeping $a = 0.40$ m. **Redesign Step (Correction):** - New area: $A = 1.5 \times 0.40 = 0.60$ m┬▓ - New velocity: $V = 2.0 / 0.60 = 3.33$ m/s (still high) - Adopt $b = 2.0$ m, $a = 0.40$ m: $A = 0.80$ m┬▓, $V = 2.5$ m/s Ō£ō **Using $b = 2.0$ m, $a = 0.40$ m, $V = 2.5$ m/s:** **Step 2:** Manning's head loss in the barrel: Assume barrel is rectangular with $b = 2.0$ m, $a = 0.40$ m, $L = 10$ m. Wetted perimeter: $P = b + 2a = 2.0 + 0.8 = 2.8$ m Hydraulic radius: $R = A/P = 0.80/2.8 = 0.2857$ m $$ h_f = \frac{V^2 n^2 L}{R^{4/3}} = \frac{(2.5)^2 (0.014)^2 (10)}{(0.2857)^{4/3}} $$ Compute $R^{4/3}$: $$ (0.2857)^{4/3} = (0.2857)^{1.333} = 0.2857 \times (0.2857)^{0.333} $$ Since $0.2857^{1/3} \approx 0.6586$: $$ R^{4/3} = 0.2857 \times 0.6586 = 0.1882 $$ Substitute: $$ h_f = \frac{6.25 \times 0.000196 \times 10}{0.1882} = \frac{0.01225}{0.1882} = 0.0651 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3:** Head loss is negligible (6.5 cm), confirming the design. **Step 4:** Compute the floor thickness at the **entry** using Bligh's theory: Assume the floor length $L_f = 10$ m and head $H = 3.0$ m. $$ t_{entry} = \frac{H}{\rho_s - 1} = \frac{3.0}{2.24 - 1} = \frac{3.0}{1.24} = 2.419 \text{ m} $$ This is impractically thick. **Reduce head** by providing an upstream cut-off wall and check again with the residual head. **Step 5:** Recalculate with a **reducing floor** profile: At mid-length $x = 5$ m: $$ h_5 = 3.0 \left(1 - \frac{5}{10}\right) = 1.5 \text{ m} $$ $$ t_5 = \frac{1.5}{1.24} = 1.210 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6:** Adopt a **uniform practical floor thickness** of $0.6$ m (since the bund is small and the structure is for low-head conditions). Provide a **vertical cut-off wall** of depth $1.0$ m at both ends to control seepage. > **Answer (b):** Head loss $h_f \approx 0.065$ m (negligible); theoretical floor thickness at entry = 2.42 m; **practical adopted thickness = 0.60 m with cut-off walls of 1.0 m depth** for safety. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Model)]** **A tank sluice has a barrel of length 8 m and total creep length of 18 m. The headwater depth is 2.0 m. The downstream floor is constructed with plain concrete ($\rho_s = 2.24$).** **Answer the following:** **(a)** Compute the **uplift pressure** at the entry, middle, and exit of the floor using Bligh's creep theory. (7 marks) **(b)** Determine the **corresponding floor thicknesses** at these three locations, and check whether the design is safe using Khosla's exit gradient. Given: downstream cut-off depth $d = 1.0$ m, soil specific gravity $C_s = 2.65$. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B** #### **Part (a): Uplift Pressure Distribution** **Step 1:** Compute the hydraulic gradient using Bligh's theory: $$ i = \frac{H}{L} = \frac{2.0}{18} = 0.111 $$ **Step 2:** Compute uplift head at any distance $x$: $$ h_x = H \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) = 2.0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{18}\right) $$ **At entry ($x = 0$):** $$ h_0 = 2.0 \left(1 - 0\right) = 2.0 \text{ m} $$ **At middle ($x = 9$ m):** $$ h_9 = 2.0 \left(1 - \frac{9}{18}\right) = 2.0 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ m} $$ **At exit ($x = 18$ m):** $$ h_{18} = 2.0 \left(1 - \frac{18}{18}\right) = 0 \text{ m} $$ > **Answer (a):** Uplift heads: **2.0 m at entry, 1.0 m at middle, 0.0 m at exit.** --- #### **Part (b): Floor Thicknesses and Exit Gradient Check** **Step 1:** Compute floor thickness using $t = h_x / (\rho_s - 1)$: **At entry:** $$ t_0 = \frac{2.0}{2.24 - 1} = \frac{2.0}{1.24} = 1.613 \text{ m} $$ **At middle:** $$ t_9 = \frac{1.0}{1.24} = 0.806 \text{ m} $$ **At exit:** $$ t_{18} = \frac{0}{1.24} = 0 \text{ m (use minimum } 0.3 \text{ m)} $$ **Step 2:** Compute Khosla's exit gradient: Given $b = 8$ m (floor length), $d = 1.0$ m (downstream cut-off depth): $$ \alpha = \frac{b}{d} = \frac{8}{1.0} = 8 $$ **Step 3:** Compute $\lambda$: $$ \lambda = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}{2} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 64}}{2} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{65}}{2} = \frac{1 + 8.062}{2} = 4.531 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the exit gradient: $$ G_E = \frac{H}{d \pi \sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{2.0}{1.0 \times \pi \times \sqrt{4.531}} = \frac{2.0}{\pi \times 2.129} = \frac{2.0}{6.689} = 0.299 $$ **Step 5:** Safe exit gradient limit: $$ G_{E, safe} = \frac{1}{C_s} = \frac{1}{2.65} = 0.377 $$ **Step 6:** Compare: $$ G_E = 0.299 < 0.377 = G_{E, safe} $$ The design is **safe** against piping. **Step 7:** Practical recommendation: - Adopt a **variable floor profile**: 1.0 m at entry ŌåÆ 0.7 m at middle ŌåÆ 0.4 m at exit. - Or use a **uniform 0.6 m RCC floor** with a downstream cut-off wall of 1.0 m depth. > **Answer (b):** Floor thicknesses: **1.61 m (entry), 0.81 m (middle), 0.0 m (exit, adopt 0.3 m minimum)**. Exit gradient $G_E = 0.299 < 0.377$ ŌåÆ **Design is safe**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1 from $\rho_s$** in floor thickness formula (very common! $t = h_x/\rho_s$ is wrong ŌĆö the correct form is $t = h_x/(\rho_s - 1)$). > 2. **Mixing up $H$ and $H/2$** when computing orifice discharge ŌĆö for a fully submerged sluice, the full $H$ above the centroid is used. > 3. **Ignoring the $2/3$ safety factor** in Khosla's theory ŌĆö students often quote the theoretical exit gradient directly, losing 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting to compute residual head** at multiple sections ŌĆö KTU expects a tabular form (entry, middle, exit) for full marks. > 5. **Unit errors** in $C_d$ and $g$ ŌĆö $C_d$ is dimensionless, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓ (not 9.8 or 10). Always state units clearly. > 6. **Drawing unlabeled diagrams** ŌĆö a sectional elevation with **no labels of FSL, TWL, $H$, $L$** will cost 2 marks easily. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Tank Sluice** - **Definition:** Regulatory outlet through tank bund; conveys water to command area. - **Two main types:** Head sluice (irrigation outlet) and Surplus sluice (escape). - **Standard components:** Wing walls ŌåÆ trash rack ŌåÆ gate ŌåÆ barrel ŌåÆ apron ŌåÆ toe wall. - **Design discharge formula:** $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ (orifice flow). - **Velocity in barrel:** Should be **1.5ŌĆō2.5 m/s**; if higher, increase barrel area. - **Head losses:** Entry ($K_e = 0.5$), friction (Manning/Darcy), expansion (BordaŌĆōCarnot). - **Bligh's theory:** Linear uplift, $t = h_x/(\rho_s - 1)$, $\rho_s = 2.24$ for plain concrete. - **Khosla's theory:** Exit gradient $G_E = \dfrac{H}{d \pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$, safe if $G_E \le 1/C_s$. - **Khosla's 2/3 rule:** Use $2/3$ of computed $G_E$ for safe design floor thickness. - **Practical minimum floor thickness:** **0.3 m** (for construction feasibility). - **Cut-off walls:** Always provide at entry and exit; depth typically 0.5ŌĆō1.5 m. - **Energy dissipation:** Often minimal due to small head; no major stilling basin needed for $H < 3$ m. - **IS Code references:** IS 7784, IS 4997 (cross-reference for design). - **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Discharge computation, head loss, Bligh's theory, Khosla's exit gradient. - **Common 3-mark topics:** Definition, components, Bligh's formula, types of sluice. - **Common 14-mark topics:** Full design with discharge + floor thickness + exit gradient check. - **Exam mantra:** "Memorize orifice equation, Bligh's floor thickness formula, and Khosla's $G_E$ formula ŌĆö these are asked repeatedly."

topics.title: Canal Fall (Trapezoidal Notch Type)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Canal Fall (Trapezoidal Notch Type)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Canal Fall?

A **Canal Fall** (also called a *waterfall* or *drop structure*) is a hydraulic structure constructed across an open channel (canal) to provide a sudden drop in the bed level whenever the natural ground slope suddenly steepens beyond the permissible longitudinal slope of the canal. It safely dissipates the excess kinetic energy and protects the canal bed from scour/erosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET755, Module 2):**
> A canal fall is a structure designed to lower the water level in a canal where the natural ground slope exceeds the design slope, by allowing the water to drop from a higher elevation to a lower one while dissipating surplus energy.

## 1.2 Classification of Canal Falls (KTU Board-Relevant)

Canal falls are broadly classified as:

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Vertical Drop Falls** | Sarda Fall, Straight Glacis Fall, Inglis Fall (Baffle) |
| **Notch Falls** | Trapezoidal Notch, Rectangular Notch, V-Notch |
| **Flumed Falls** | Chute, Spillway |

This lesson covers the **Trapezoidal Notch Fall** ŌĆö a type of *notch fall*.

## 1.3 Trapezoidal Notch Fall ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Trapezoidal Notch Fall** is a fall in which the crest (or baffle wall) is pierced with one or more **trapezoidal-shaped openings (notches)** of a specified bottom width and inclined sides. Water passes through these notches and drops into a cistern (stilling basin) below.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Formal Statement:**
> A trapezoidal notch fall is a masonry/ concrete fall in which a number of trapezoidal notches of pre-determined dimensions are cut through the crest wall. The discharge through each notch is the sum of (i) the discharge through a rectangular portion of width *L* and head *H*, plus (ii) the discharge through the two triangular end portions of included angle *╬Ė*.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **staircase down a hill** with the water running along the top. The hill is too steep to let the water flow safely downhill in a smooth slide. So, you build a **wall across the path with a few door-shaped cutouts (windows)**. The water flows out through these windows and lands on a concrete cushion (a *pool*) that absorbs the impact. The cushion then lets the water run off gently. That is, in essence, a trapezoidal notch fall.

Each "window" is a **trapezoid** ŌĆö wider at the top, narrower at the bottom ŌĆö because this shape:
- Lets a *large discharge pass safely* during floods (top portion is wide).
- Maintains a *small minimum discharge* in dry season (bottom narrow portion).

## 1.5 Why Use a Trapezoidal Notch Specifically?

- It **regulates discharge** more uniformly across varying heads.
- Acts as a **broad-crested weir with side contractions**, so the discharge is fairly predictable.
- **Self-cleaning** ŌĆö silt does not accumulate easily because side slopes are gentle.
- The discharge coefficient (Cd) is **fairly stable** (Ōēł **0.60 ŌĆō 0.65**) across heads.

## 1.6 Standard Physical Constants to Remember

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s┬▓**
- Recommended coefficient of discharge: **Cd Ōēł 0.60 ŌĆō 0.65** (trapezoidal notch in crest wall)
- Side slope commonly adopted: **1H : 1V** (i.e., ╬Ė/2 = 45┬░ from vertical)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Trapezoidal Notch Fall

A typical trapezoidal notch fall consists of the following components (labelled for KTU diagram-based questions):

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Crest Wall (or Baffle Wall)** | Vertical wall across the canal containing the notches; supports the upstream water column |
| 2 | **Trapezoidal Notches** | Openings through the crest wall through which water passes |
| 3 | **Wing Walls** | Retain the canal banks and guide flow into the notches |
| 4 | **Cistern / Stilling Basin** | Concrete pool below the crest that dissipates kinetic energy |
| 5 | **Talus (Apron / Floor)** | Sloped bed downstream of the cistern that joins back to the natural bed |
| 6 | **Cut-off Walls (Curtain Walls)** | Vertical walls at the upstream and downstream ends of the apron to prevent piping |

## 2.2 Design Philosophy ŌĆö "Why" Behind the Geometry

1. **Why trapezoidal?** A pure rectangle notch under-modulates the dry-season flow; a pure V-notch under-modulates flood flow. A *trapezoid* is a **hybrid** (rectangle + triangle) that performs well across a wide range of discharges.

2. **Why multiple notches?** In wide canals, several smaller notches are preferable to one large notch because:
   - Structural stability of the crest wall is preserved.
   - Local scour is more easily controlled.
   - Maintenance is easier.

3. **Why include a cistern?** The free-falling water has *very high kinetic energy*; without dissipation, it would scour the downstream bed. The cistern acts as a **hydraulic jump absorber**.

## 2.3 Theoretical Head-Discharge Relationship

Consider a **single trapezoidal notch** with:
- Bottom width = **L**
- Side slope half-angle = **╬Ė/2** (where ╬Ė is the full included angle of the triangular end)
- Head of water over the notch crest = **H**

A trapezoidal notch is conceptually the **superposition** of:
- A **rectangular notch** of width *L* and head *H* (gives the central rectangular portion).
- A **V-notch** of included angle *╬Ė* and head *H* (gives the two triangular end portions).

**Discharge (Q) is therefore:**

The total discharge is the linear sum of these two component discharges, both multiplied by the same Cd.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table ŌĆö it forms the basis of every numerical problem in this topic.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Discharge through rectangular portion | $Q_1 = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ | m┬│/s |
| 2 | Discharge through two triangular ends | $Q_2 = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot H^{5/2}$ | m┬│/s |
| 3 | **Total discharge through one trapezoidal notch** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\sqrt{2g}\!\left[L\,H^{3/2} + \dfrac{4}{5}\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) H^{5/2}\right]$ | m┬│/s |
| 4 | Equivalent (combined) form | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\sqrt{2g}\,L\,H^{3/2} + \dfrac{8}{15}\,C_d\sqrt{2g}\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) H^{5/2}$ | m┬│/s |
| 5 | For multiple notches (n notches) | $Q_{total} = n \cdot Q_{single}$ | m┬│/s |
| 6 | Typical coefficient of discharge | $C_d = 0.60 - 0.65$ | ŌĆö |
| 7 | Standard gravity | $g = 9.81$ | m/s┬▓ |
| 8 | Common side slope | $1H : 1V \Rightarrow \theta/2 = 45^{\circ}$ | ŌĆö |

**Note (╬Ė convention used here):** *╬Ė* is the **full included angle at the apex** of the equivalent V-notch (i.e., angle between the two inclined sides). Most KTU textbooks use *╬Ė* as full included angle.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- Used in **canal distribution networks** in India, especially in the **Indira Gandhi Nahar Pariyojana (IGNP)** and the Upper Ganga Canal systems.
- Applied in **barrage tail channels** where slope changes abruptly.
- Adopted in **cross-drainage works** at the tail-end of minor irrigation schemes in Kerala's laterite terrain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Discharge Through a Trapezoidal Notch (Full Derivation ŌĆö No Steps Skipped)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry Setup

Consider a single trapezoidal notch in the crest wall.

Let:
- *L* = horizontal length of the bottom (crest) of the notch (m)
- *H* = head of water above the crest of the notch (m)
- *╬Ė* = full included angle of the triangular end portions (degrees or radians)
- *g* = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s┬▓**

At any height *h* above the bottom of the notch, the width of the water prism inside the notch is:

$$b(h) = L + 2h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The bottom is *L* wide. As you go up by a height *h*, each side moves outward horizontally by *h┬Ętan(╬Ė/2)*. The factor 2 accounts for both sides.

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Velocity at the Elementary Strip

The depth of water above the elementary strip (measured from the free water surface) is:

$$y = H - h$$

By **Torricelli's Law**, the theoretical velocity through the elementary strip is:

$$v = \sqrt{2gy} = \sqrt{2g(H-h)}$$

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Area of the Elementary Strip

The area of the strip of height *dh* is:

$$dA = b(h)\,dh = \left[L + 2h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right] dh$$

---

### Step 4 ŌĆö Elementary Discharge

The theoretical (un-coefficient) elemental discharge is:

$$dQ_{th} = v \cdot dA = \sqrt{2g(H-h)} \cdot \left[L + 2h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right] dh$$

Applying the discharge coefficient *CŌéÉ* (or *CŌéÉ*):

$$dQ = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{H-h} \cdot \left[L + 2h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right] dh$$

---

### Step 5 ŌĆö Total Discharge by Integration

$$Q = C_d \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H \left[L + 2h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right]\,(H-h)^{1/2}\,dh$$

Split into two integrals:

$$Q = C_d \sqrt{2g} \left[\, L \int_0^H (H-h)^{1/2}\,dh \;+\; 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\int_0^H h\,(H-h)^{1/2}\,dh \,\right]$$

---

### Step 6 ŌĆö Evaluate the First Integral (Rectangular Portion)

$$I_1 = \int_0^H (H-h)^{1/2}\,dh$$

Let *u = H ŌłÆ h*, so *du = ŌłÆdh*. Limits: *h = 0 ŌćÆ u = H*; *h = H ŌćÆ u = 0*.

$$I_1 = \int_H^0 u^{1/2}\,(-du) = \int_0^H u^{1/2}\,du = \left[\frac{2}{3}u^{3/2}\right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2}$$

---

### Step 7 ŌĆö Evaluate the Second Integral (Triangular End Portions)

$$I_2 = \int_0^H h\,(H-h)^{1/2}\,dh$$

Let *u = H ŌłÆ h* ŌćÆ *h = H ŌłÆ u*, *dh = ŌłÆdu*. Limits: *h = 0 ŌćÆ u = H*; *h = H ŌćÆ u = 0*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \int_H^0 (H-u)\,u^{1/2}\,(-du) \\
&= \int_0^H (H-u)\,u^{1/2}\,du \\
&= H\int_0^H u^{1/2}\,du \;-\; \int_0^H u^{3/2}\,du \\
&= H \cdot \left[\frac{2}{3}u^{3/2}\right]_0^H \;-\; \left[\frac{2}{5}u^{5/2}\right]_0^H \\
&= \frac{2}{3}H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2} \\
&= \left(\frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5}\right)H^{5/2} \\
&= \frac{10 - 6}{15}\,H^{5/2} = \frac{4}{15}\,H^{5/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 8 ŌĆö Assemble the Final Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_d \sqrt{2g} \left[\, L \cdot \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2} \;+\; 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{4}{15}H^{5/2} \,\right] \\
&= C_d \sqrt{2g} \left[\, \frac{2L}{3}\,H^{3/2} \;+\; \frac{8}{15}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) H^{5/2} \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Boxed Result (Kotlin-formatted for the formula sheet):**

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{2}{3}\,C_d \sqrt{2g}\,L\,H^{3/2} \;+\; \frac{8}{15}\,C_d \sqrt{2g}\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) H^{5/2}\,}$$

Equivalently (factored form):

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{2}{3}\,C_d \sqrt{2g} \left[\, L\,H^{3/2} + \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) H^{5/2} \,\right]\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical ŌĆö KTU Pattern (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A trapezoidal notch fall has 3 notches, each of bottom width *L = 0.80 m*, side slope 1H:1V, and a head of *H = 0.50 m* above the notch crest. Take *Cd = 0.62*. Compute the total discharge.

**Solution:**

Given:
- *n = 3*, *L = 0.80 m*, *H = 0.50 m*, side slope 1H:1V ŌćÆ *╬Ė/2 = 45┬░*, so *tan(45┬░) = 1*
- *CŌéÉ = 0.62*, *g = 9.81 m/s┬▓*

**Step 1 ŌĆö ŌłÜ(2g):**

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294\,\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rectangular component (per notch):**

$$Q_1 = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 \times 0.80 \times (0.50)^{3/2}$$

$$(0.50)^{3/2} = 0.50 \times \sqrt{0.50} = 0.50 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$$

$$Q_1 = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 \times 0.80 \times 0.3536$$

Step-by-step:
- $\frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 = 0.4133$
- $0.4133 \times 4.4294 = 1.8310$
- $1.8310 \times 0.80 = 1.4648$
- $1.4648 \times 0.3536 = 0.5180$ m┬│/s

**Step 3 ŌĆö Triangular component (per notch):**

$$Q_2 = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 \times \tan(45┬░) \times (0.50)^{5/2}$$

$$(0.50)^{5/2} = (0.50)^{2.5} = 0.50^2 \times \sqrt{0.50} = 0.25 \times 0.7071 = 0.1768$$

$$Q_2 = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.62 \times 4.4294 \times 1.0 \times 0.1768$$

Step-by-step:
- $\frac{8}{15} = 0.5333$
- $0.5333 \times 0.62 = 0.3307$
- $0.3307 \times 4.4294 = 1.4648$
- $1.4648 \times 0.1768 = 0.2589$ m┬│/s

**Step 4 ŌĆö Per-notch discharge:**

$$Q_{single} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.5180 + 0.2589 = 0.7769 \approx 0.777\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total (3 notches):**

$$Q_{total} = 3 \times 0.7769 = 2.3307\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 2.33\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Sensitivity of Discharge to Parameters

| Parameter Increased | Effect on Q | Reason |
|--------------------|-------------|--------|
| Head *H* | Q increases sharply (H^(3/2) + H^(5/2)) | Larger wetted area + higher velocity |
| Bottom width *L* | Q increases linearly via rectangle term | Wider rectangular section passes more flow |
| Side slope angle *╬Ė* | Q increases (triangle term) | Wider top opening |
| Coefficient *CŌéÉ* | Q increases linearly | Better hydraulic efficiency |
| Number of notches *n* | Q increases linearly | More parallel openings |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of a Trapezoidal Notch Fall

The schematic below illustrates the **functional flow** of water and the role of each structural element in a typical trapezoidal notch fall. (Adapted as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow per protocol, since the physical drawing of masonry/cracks is best shown in a textbook figure.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UPCanalChannel] --> B[ApproachTransition]
    B --> C[WingWallUpstream]
    C --> D[CrestWallBaffleWithNotches]
    D --> D1[Notch1TrapezoidalOpening]
    D --> D2[Notch2TrapezoidalOpening]
    D --> D3[Notch3TrapezoidalOpening]
    D1 --> E[FreeFallJet]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[EnergyDissipationCistern]
    F --> G[StillingBasinHydraulicJump]
    G --> H[TalusApronSlopedBed]
    H --> I[CutoffWallDownstream]
    I --> J[DownstreamCanal]
    K[CutoffWallUpstream] --> B
    L[BottomWidthL] -.-> D1
    M[SideSlopeTanThetaOverTwo] -.-> D1
    N[HeadHoverNotch] -.-> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Node A (Up Canal Channel)** ŌåÆ water enters the fall.
- **Node D (Crest Wall With Notches)** ŌåÆ the core hydraulic element. Each *Notch_i* has a *bottom width L*, *side slope tan(╬Ė/2)*, and *head H*.
- **Node E ŌåÆ F ŌåÆ G** ŌåÆ water falls as a free jet, hits the cistern, and dissipates energy via a hydraulic jump.
- **Node I ŌåÆ J** ŌåÆ cutoff wall prevents undermining; flow resumes in the downstream canal.

## 4.2 Mermaid Component Hierarchy (Crest Wall Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CW[CrestWallMasonry] --> N1[Notch1]
    CW --> N2[Notch2]
    CW --> N3[Notch3]

    N1 --> P2[TriangularEndPortionAngleTheta]
    P1 --> FR[DischargeTwoThirdCdSqRootTwoGLHPowerThreeByTwo]
    P2 --> FT[DischargeEightFifteenthCdSqRootTwoGTanThetaTwoHPowerFiveByTwo]
    FR --> ST[TotalDischargePerNotchQ]
    FT --> ST
    ST --> MT[MultiplyByNForTotalDischarge]
```

            |<----- Top width of notch (L + 2H┬Ętan ╬Ė/2) ----->|

   =========|==========|===============================|=========  ŌåÉ Crest wall base

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]**
