Differentiating $\phi$ along the floor at $x = b$: $$ G_E = \frac{d\phi}{ds}\bigg|_{exit} = \frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{\lambda}} \cdot \frac{H}{d} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,G_E = \frac{H}{d \pi \sqrt{\lambda}}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This expression **decouples** the floor geometry ($b/d$) from the head $H$, allowing a designer to size $b$ and $d$ independently of $H$ for a chosen safe gradient. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Floor Thickness from Unbalanced Uplift **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the unbalanced head at a floor section:** The unbalanced head is the difference between the **uplift pressure** (from seepage) and the **downstream water pressure** above the floor. $$ h_w = p_{\text{uplift}} - h_{\text{water above}} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Equilibrium of the floor slab (vertical forces):** For a unit area of floor in masonry of specific gravity $G_m$ and submerged condition: $$ \text{Weight of floor (submerged)} = \text{Net uplift force} $$ $$ t \cdot (G_m - 1) \cdot \gamma_w = h_w \cdot \gamma_w $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $t$:** $$ \boxed{\,t = \frac{h_w}{G_m - 1}\,} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Typical values (KTU 2024):** - For plain concrete: $G_m \approx 2.4$, so $t \approx h_w / 1.4$ - For brick masonry: $G_m \approx 2.2$, so $t \approx h_w / 1.2$ - Minimum $t = 0.5$ m for **rigidity and constructability**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Khosla's Pressure at an Intermediate Sheet Pile Suppose a **vertical intermediate sheet pile of depth $d'$** is located at distance $b_1$ from the **downstream** end of the floor. Define: $$ \alpha_1 = \frac{b_1}{d_1}, \qquad \lambda_1 = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha_1^2}}{2} $$ The **residual seepage head** at the **tip of this intermediate pile** is: $$ p_d = \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_1 - 2}{\lambda_1}\right) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_1 - 2}{\lambda_1}\right)\right] $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Combine inverse trig using identity:** Using $\cos^{-1} x + \sin^{-1} x = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ when $x \in [0, 1]$: But here the argument $\dfrac{\lambda_1 - 2}{\lambda_1}$ is **negative** (since $\lambda_1 \geq 1$ implies $\lambda_1 - 2 \leq -1$ for small $\alpha_1$). So we use the form valid for negative arguments: $$ \cos^{-1}(-x) = \pi - \cos^{-1}(x) $$ Let $x_1 = \dfrac{2 - \lambda_1}{\lambda_1}$ (positive). Then: $$ p_d = \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\pi - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2 - \lambda_1}{\lambda_1}\right) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_1 - 2}{\lambda_1}\right)\right] $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation:** In KTU problems, substitute the values of $H, b_1, d_1$ and compute $p_d$ directly using a calculator in **radian mode** (or convert to degrees). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Design Matrix ŌĆö Khosla's Theory | Design Step | Input Parameter | Output | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H$ (from UWL, DWL) | Head difference | Given | | 2 | Trial $b, d$ | Geometric ratio | $\alpha = b/d$ | | 3 | $\alpha$ | Khosla function | $\lambda = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}{2}$ | | 4 | $\alpha$ | Critical gradient | $G_{E,LBS} = \frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{\lambda}}$ | | 5 | $H, d, \lambda$ | Actual exit gradient | $G_E = H \cdot G_{E,LBS} / d$ | | 6 | $G_E$ vs. $G_{E,safe}$ | Go / Revise | $G_E \leq G_{E,safe} / FOS$ | | 7 | $\alpha, H$ | Residual head | $\phi_E = \frac{H}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda-2}{\lambda}\right)$ | | 8 | $\phi_E$, downstream water depth | $h_w$ at downstream | $h_w = \phi_E - h_{dw}$ | | 9 | $h_w, G_m$ | Floor thickness | $t = h_w / (G_m - 1)$ | | 10 | $G_{E,safe}$, $V_{scour}$ | Sheet pile depth | $d = H / (\pi\sqrt{\lambda} \cdot G_{E,safe})$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cross-Section of a Typical Canal Regulator > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram renders a **functional block-level architecture** of a canal regulator's design flow, mirroring Khosla's design procedure. A true cross-section would require CAD drawing; the block flow captures all functional components. ```mermaid graph TD A[Start: Canal Regulator Design] --> B[Step 1: Fix Hydraulic Data
UWL, DWL, H, Q] B --> C[Step 2: Select Trial Floor
b and pile depth d] C --> D[Step 3: Compute Ratio
alpha equals b divided by d] D --> E[Step 4: Compute Khosla Function
lambda equals 1 plus sqrt 1 plus alpha squared over 2] E --> F[Step 5: Calculate Exit Gradient
GE equals H divided by d pi sqrt lambda] F --> G{Check GE Safe} G -- No --> H[Revise d or b
Increase floor length or pile depth] H --> D G -- Yes --> I[Step 6: Compute Residual Head
phiE equals H over pi cos inverse lambda minus 2 over lambda] I --> J[Step 7: Calculate Unbalanced Head
hw equals phiE minus downstream water depth] J --> K[Step 8: Compute Floor Thickness
t equals hw divided by Gm minus 1] K --> L[Step 9: Check Sliding and Overturning
Stability Checks] L --> M[Step 10: Finalise Design
Crest, Abutments, Sheet Piles] M --> N[End: Construction Drawings] ``` **Interpretation of the Flow:** - **Node A (Start):** Design initiation with project specifications. - **Node C (Trial Floor):** Engineer's first guess based on empirical rules ($b \approx 2$ to $3 \times H$). - **Node G (Critical Decision):** If $G_E > G_{E,safe}$, the design **fails the piping check** and must be revised. - **Node K (Floor Thickness):** Uses Khosla's computed $\phi_E$ to size the **concrete apron**. - **Node M (End):** Finalized cross-section ready for execution. --- ### 4.2 Schematic of a Canal Regulator (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ParentChannel["Parent Channel (Main Canal)"] US["U/S Water Level"] end subgraph RegStructure["Canal Regulator Structure"] A1["Abutment Left
Returns and Wing Walls"] B1["Gated Crest
Multiple Bays with Vertical Gates"] C1["Impervious Floor
Designed via Khosla Theory"] D1["Sheet Piles
U/S and D/S Cutoffs"] E1["Silt Excluder
Tunnel Type"] end subgraph OfftakeCanal["Offtake Canal (Distributary)"] DS["D/S Water Level in Canal"] end US --> A1 A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1 D1 --> E1 E1 --> DS ``` **Components Identified:** 1. **Abutment & Returns** ŌĆö Confine flow and provide structural anchorage. 2. **Gated Crest** ŌĆö Regulates discharge; multiple bays allow maintenance flexibility. 3. **Impervious Floor** ŌĆö Designed using Khosla's theory for uplift safety. 4. **Sheet Piles** ŌĆö Cutoffs to lengthen seepage path and reduce exit gradient. 5. **Silt Excluder** ŌĆö Prevents coarse sediment from entering the canal. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** *State the fundamental assumptions of Khosla's Theory of seepage flow under a hydraulic structure.* **Model Answer:** Khosla's theory is based on the following assumptions: 1. The subsoil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and incompressible**. 2. The flow is **2-D, steady, and laminar**, satisfying Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$. 3. The **impervious boundary** (rock or impervious clay) is **horizontal** and lies at a **finite depth** below the floor. 4. The hydraulic floor is **horizontal** and **impervious** of length $b$. 5. The **sheet pile walls** are assumed to be **thin and vertical**, with negligible thickness compared to floor length. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Mere listing without naming "Laplace's equation" loses 1 mark. Always mention the equation explicitly. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** *Define **Exit Gradient**. How is it related to the safety of a hydraulic structure?* **Model Answer:** **Exit Gradient ($G_E$):** It is defined as the **rate of loss of head per unit length of the seepage flow** at the **downstream end** of the impervious floor, where the percolating water finally emerges into the atmosphere. $$ G_E = \frac{dh}{ds}\bigg|_{\text{at exit}} $$ It is **directly related to safety** because: - If $G_E$ exceeds the **critical (safe) gradient** $G_{E,safe}$ of the subsoil, the soil grains are **carried away** by the upward seepage force. - This phenomenon is called **piping or undermining**, and it leads to the **catastrophic failure** of the hydraulic structure. - For sandy soils, IS code recommends $G_{E,safe} = 1/6$ to $1/8$ (coarse) and $1/5$ (fine), with a factor of safety $\geq 1.5$. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write "exit gradient = head loss / floor length" ŌĆö this is **Bligh's approximation** and is **wrong** for Khosla's theory. The correct expression is $G_E = H / (d \pi \sqrt{\lambda})$. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Canal Regulator Problems):** > - **Marks deducted:** 1 mark for not showing $\alpha$ and $\lambda$ explicitly. > - **Marks deducted:** 2 marks for using **Bligh's creep theory** instead of Khosla's (mentally outdated). > - **Marks deducted:** 1 mark for not **comparing $G_E$ with $G_{E,safe}$** explicitly. > - **Common Mistake:** Confusing the **floor length $b$** with the **sheet pile depth $d$** in the exit gradient formula. Always **re-verify** the geometric ratio $\alpha$. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 ŌĆō Module 2]** A **canal regulator** is to be designed for the following data: - Upstream water level (UWL) = **+112.50 m** - Downstream water level (DWL) = **+109.50 m** - Safe exit gradient, $G_{E,safe} = 1/6$ - Subsoil: Fine sand, depth of impervious stratum = **8.0 m** below floor - Specific gravity of floor masonry, $G_m = 2.4$ - Trial downstream sheet pile depth, $d = 4.0$ m **Tasks:** **(a)** [7 Marks] Determine the **required floor length $b$** using Khosla's theory. Use the iterative Khosla procedure. **(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the **floor thickness** at the downstream end and check the exit gradient. --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A** #### **Part (a): Determination of Floor Length** **Step 1 ŌĆö Head difference:** $$ H = UWL - DWL = 112.50 - 109.50 = 3.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Required safe exit gradient check (Iteration 1):** $$ G_{E,safe} = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667 $$ For safe design: $G_E = G_{E,safe} \times FOS = 0.1667 / 1.5 = 0.111$ (with FOS = 1.5) Or directly use $G_E = G_{E,safe}$ with adequate FOS built in. Take $G_E = 0.1667$ for first trial. **Step 3 ŌĆö Trial values:** - Trial $d = 4.0$ m (given) - Assume $b = 4d = 16.0$ m ŌåÆ $\alpha = b/d = 4.0$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Khosla's function:** $$ \lambda = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}{2} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 16}}{2} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{17}}{2} = \frac{1 + 4.123}{2} = 2.5615 $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute exit gradient per unit head:** $$ G_{E,LBS} = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{1}{\pi \times \sqrt{2.5615}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 1.6005} = 0.1990 $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Compute actual exit gradient:** $$ G_E = G_{E,LBS} \times \frac{H}{d} = 0.1990 \times \frac{3.0}{4.0} = 0.1492 $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Compare with safe gradient:** $$ G_E = 0.1492 < G_{E,safe} = 0.1667 \quad \text{(SAFE)} $$ Ō£ģ **Design is safe with $b = 16.0$ m, $d = 4.0$ m.** **Step 8 ŌĆö Check impervious boundary depth:** - Sheet pile tip at: Floor level $ - d = -4.0$ m - Impervious stratum at: $-8.0$ m - Pile does **not** touch impervious boundary ŌåÆ Khosla's theory valid. **Step 9 ŌĆö Provide floor length:** $$ \boxed{\,b = 16.0 \text{ m (adopt)}\,} $$ --- #### **Part (b): Floor Thickness at Downstream End** **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute residual head $\phi_E$ at downstream pile:** $$ \phi_E = \frac{H}{\pi} \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right) $$ Compute argument: $$ \frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda} = \frac{2.5615 - 2}{2.5615} = \frac{0.5615}{2.5615} = 0.2192 $$ $$ \cos^{-1}(0.2192) = 1.350 \text{ rad} = 77.34^{\circ} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $\phi_E$:** $$ \phi_E = \frac{3.0}{\pi} \times 1.350 = \frac{4.05}{3.1416} = 1.289 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Determine unbalanced head $h_w$ at downstream end:** Assume downstream water depth above floor = **0.0 m** (free emergence at downstream end). $$ h_w = \phi_E - 0.0 = 1.289 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute floor thickness:** $$ t = \frac{h_w}{G_m - 1} = \frac{1.289}{2.4 - 1} = \frac{1.289}{1.4} = 0.921 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Adopt practical thickness:** $$ \boxed{\,t_{downstream} = 1.00 \text{ m (adopted, including construction tolerance)}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > The downstream end has the **maximum uplift** and hence the **maximum floor thickness** in a typical Khosla design. The thickness reduces linearly toward the upstream end (with checks at intermediate points). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Internal Choice** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 ŌĆō Module 2]** A **canal head regulator** has the following design data: - Gross head, $H = 4.5$ m - Downstream sheet pile depth, $d = 3.0$ m - Floor length, $b = 12.0$ m - Specific gravity of masonry, $G_m = 2.2$ - Safe exit gradient, $G_{E,safe} = 1/7$ **Tasks:** **(a)** [7 Marks] Check the **safety of the floor against piping** using Khosla's theory and determine the **residual head** at the downstream pile. **(b)** [7 Marks] An **intermediate sheet pile** of depth $d_1 = 1.5$ m is proposed at a distance $b_1 = 4.0$ m from the downstream end. Calculate the **residual head at the tip** of this pile and comment on the **efficacy** of the intermediate pile. --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B** #### **Part (a): Piping Safety Check and Residual Head** **Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric ratio:** $$ \alpha = \frac{b}{d} = \frac{12.0}{3.0} = 4.0 $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Khosla's function:** $$ \lambda = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 16}}{2} = 2.5615 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Unit exit gradient:** $$ G_{E,LBS} = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{2.5615}} = 0.1990 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Actual exit gradient:** $$ G_E = G_{E,LBS} \times \frac{H}{d} = 0.1990 \times \frac{4.5}{3.0} = 0.2985 $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compare with safe gradient:** $$ G_E = 0.2985 \text{ vs. } G_{E,safe} = 1/7 = 0.1429 $$ ŌØī $G_E > G_{E,safe}$ ŌåÆ **Floor is UNSAFE against piping!** **Step 6 ŌĆö Required modification:** Increase $d$ to make $G_E = 0.1429$: $$ 0.1429 = 0.1990 \times \frac{4.5}{d_{new}} $$ $$ d_{new} = \frac{0.1990 \times 4.5}{0.1429} = \frac{0.8955}{0.1429} = 6.27 \text{ m} $$ **Adopt $d = 6.5$ m** (rounded up, practical) and **increase $b$ to $b = 18$ m** for better factor of safety. **Step 7 ŌĆö Residual head with revised $d = 6.5$ m, $b = 18$ m:** - New $\alpha = 18/6.5 = 2.769$ - $\lambda = (1 + \sqrt{1 + 7.67})/2 = (1 + 2.948)/2 = 1.974$ - $\cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda - 2}{\lambda}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.026}{1.974}\right) = \cos^{-1}(-0.01317) = 1.584 \text{ rad}$ $$ \phi_E = \frac{4.5}{\pi} \times 1.584 = 2.269 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\phi_E = 2.27 \text{ m}\,} $$ --- #### **Part (b): Residual Head at Intermediate Pile** **Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric ratio for intermediate pile:** $$ \alpha_1 = \frac{b_1}{d_1} = \frac{4.0}{1.5} = 2.667 $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Khosla's function:** $$ \lambda_1 = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 7.11}}{2} = \frac{1 + 2.847}{2} = 1.924 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Argument of inverse trig:** $$ \frac{\lambda_1 - 2}{\lambda_1} = \frac{1.924 - 2}{1.924} = \frac{-0.076}{1.924} = -0.0395 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\cos^{-1}$ and $\sin^{-1}$:** $$ \cos^{-1}(-0.0395) = 1.610 \text{ rad} = 92.27^{\circ} $$ $$ \sin^{-1}(-0.0395) = -0.0395 \text{ rad} = -2.26^{\circ} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Pressure at pile tip:** $$ p_d = \frac{H}{\pi}\left[\cos^{-1}(-0.0395) + \sin^{-1}(-0.0395)\right] $$ $$ p_d = \frac{4.5}{\pi} \times (1.610 - 0.0395) = \frac{4.5}{3.1416} \times 1.5705 = 2.249 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Comment on efficacy:** The intermediate pile **reduces** the pressure at the downstream end from $\phi_E = 2.27$ m (without pile) to $p_d = 2.25$ m ŌĆö only marginal reduction (~1%). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Khosla's theory reveals a key principle ŌĆö **intermediate piles are ineffective** in reducing exit gradient. They are useful only for *local* thickness increase or for *guiding flow*. The **downstream sheet pile** is what truly controls $G_E$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):** - Ō£ģ **Khosla's formula for exit gradient:** $G_E = H / (d \pi \sqrt{\lambda})$ - Ō£ģ **Khosla's function:** $\lambda = (1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}) / 2$, where $\alpha = b/d$ - Ō£ģ **Residual head at downstream pile:** $\phi_E = (H / \pi) \cos^{-1}[(\lambda - 2) / \lambda]$ - Ō£ģ **Floor thickness formula:** $t = h_w / (G_m - 1)$, minimum $0.5$ m - Ō£ģ **Safe exit gradient:** $1/6$ to $1/8$ (coarse), $1/5$ (fine sand) ŌĆö divide by FOS for design - Ō£ģ **Bligh's theory is OUTDATED** ŌĆö always use **Khosla's** for KTU 2024 - Ō£ģ **Intermediate piles DO NOT significantly reduce $G_E$** ŌĆö only the downstream pile does - Ō£ģ **Impervious boundary** must be deeper than the pile tip (else pile "touches" ŌĆö Khosla invalid) - Ō£ģ **Floor length** should generally be **2 to 4 times** the pile depth for hydraulic efficiency - Ō£ģ **Maximum floor thickness** is at the **downstream end** (maximum uplift head) - Ō£ģ **Lacey's safe exit gradient:** $G_{E,safe} = 1/(1.76 \sqrt{d_{50}})$ for coarse soils - Ō£ģ **Damping factor of 1.5 (FOS)** is conventionally applied to all Khosla designs - Ō£ģ **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ governs ŌĆö don't forget to mention it in theory answers - Ō£ģ **Sheet pile tip must be Ōēź 20% of floor length** (empirical rule for canal regulators) - Ō£ģ **Canal regulators** are essentially **miniature gated weirs** ŌĆö same design principles apply > [!NOTE] > **Closing Thought (KTU 2024 Context):** Module 2 of PECET755 emphasizes the **shift from Bligh's empirical approach to Khosla's rigorous potential-theory-based design**. The examiner's pattern in recent years (2023, 2024) consistently tests: (1) numerical computation of $G_E$ and $\phi_E$, (2) floor thickness calculation, and (3) **comparison with safe gradient** ŌĆö a step that is non-negotiable in scoring full marks. ---

topics.title: Dams: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dams: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Dam? ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

A **dam** is a massive hydraulic barrier constructed across a river or natural watercourse to impound water on its upstream side, creating a reservoir that serves one or more purposes such as irrigation, water supply, hydroelectric power generation, flood control, navigation, and recreation. As per the **Indian Standard IS 10137 (1982)**, a dam is defined as "a barrier constructed across a river or other waterway to impound or control the flow of water."

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PECET755 ŌĆō Design of Hydraulic Structures**, dams are classified as **appurtenant hydraulic structures** and form the backbone of water resources engineering. A dam essentially exploits the **gravitational potential energy** of stored water, converting it into usable forms like mechanical, electrical, or agricultural potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō Module 3):**
> The classification of dams forms the foundational knowledge required before venturing into the design of **gravity dams, earth dams, rockfill dams, arch dams, and buttress dams**. Students must be proficient in identifying site conditions, material availability, and structural behavior before choosing an appropriate dam type.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are trying to store water in a giant bathtub at the top of a hill so that you can use it later. To do this, you need a **wall** strong enough to hold that water back.

Now think about this:
- If the hill is **flat and made of loose soil**, you cannot build a thin concrete wall ŌĆö it will slide. So instead, you build a wide mound of earth. ŌåÆ **Earth Dam**
- If the hill is made of **solid rock** and the valley is narrow with strong walls, you can build a thin curved wall. ŌåÆ **Arch Dam**
- If the foundation is **strong rock** and you have plenty of concrete, you build a huge wall that resists water by its sheer weight. ŌåÆ **Gravity Dam**
- If concrete is expensive, you use a **sloped wall supported by triangular buttresses** on the downstream side. ŌåÆ **Buttress Dam**
- If the foundation is rock but you want to use locally available rocks, you pile them up with an impervious core. ŌåÆ **Rockfill Dam**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The *type* of dam is not chosen by the engineer on a whim ŌĆö it is dictated by **site topography, geology, foundation conditions, availability of construction materials, and the purpose of the reservoir**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering parameters are universally used in dam classification:

- **Specific Gravity of Water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (or $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- **Specific Gravity of Concrete:** $G_c \approx 2.40$
- **Specific Gravity of Rockfill:** $G_r \approx 1.80$ (compacted)
- **Unit Weight of Compacted Earth:** $\gamma_e \approx 18$ to $20 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- **Standard Concrete Strength (M20ŌĆōM30):** $f_{ck} = 20$ to $30 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- **Freeboard:** $F \geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ (minimum, for small dams); for large dams $F \geq 2.0$ m
- **Classification by Height (IS 10137):**
  * **Small Dam:** Height $H < 15 \text{ m}$
  * **Intermediate Dam:** $15 \text{ m} \leq H \leq 30 \text{ m}$
  * **Large Dam:** $H > 30 \text{ m}$
  * **Major Dam:** $H \geq 45 \text{ m}$ (under International Commission on Large Dams ŌĆō ICOLD norms, $H \geq 15$ m with reservoir capacity $\geq 3$ million m┬│)

---

## 1.4 Primary Classifications of Dams

Dams can be classified under **five principal heads** as mandated by KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus:

| Classification Basis | Major Types |
|----------------------|-------------|
| **(A) Based on Material of Construction** | Earthen / Rockfill / Masonry / Concrete / Steel / Timber |
| **(B) Based on Structural Behavior** | Gravity / Arch / Buttress / Embankment |
| **(C) Based on Hydraulic Design** | Overflow (Spillway) / Non-Overflow |
| **(D) Based on Purpose** | Storage / Diversion / Detention / Coffer |
| **(E) Based on Size** | Small / Intermediate / Large / Major |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Mnemonic ŌĆö "EMS-AB":** Earthfill, Masonry, Steel, Arch, Buttress.
> Most KTU questions frame the classification around **(A)** and **(B)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 Classification (A) ŌĆö Based on Material of Construction

### 2.1.1 Earthen Dam (Earthfill Dam)

**Definition:** A non-rigid dam constructed primarily from **compacted earth (soil)** with an **impervious core** (clay core or membrane) and **pervious shells** on either side.

**Engineering Principle:** Earthen dams rely on **self-weight** and the **shear strength** of compacted soil to resist water pressure. The phreatic line (top flow line) is kept within the body of the dam by an internal drainage system (toe drain, chimney drain, horizontal blanket drain).

**Key Components:**
- **Impervious Core / Membrane** ŌĆö prevents seepage through the dam body
- **Pervious Shells** ŌĆö provide stability and drainage
- **Cut-off Trench** ŌĆö reduces seepage under the foundation
- **Surface Drainage System** ŌĆö protects the downstream slope from erosion
- **Toe Drain** ŌĆö collects seepage water safely

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Earthen dams account for over **80% of all dams worldwide** (per ICOLD). They are preferred when the foundation is **alluvial or weak rock** and when construction materials (soil, clay, sand, gravel) are locally available within an economic haul distance.

---

### 2.1.2 Rockfill Dam

**Definition:** A dam constructed from **compacted rockfill** with an **impervious membrane or core** (concrete, steel, or asphalt) on the upstream face or within the body.

**Engineering Principle:** The rockfill mass provides structural stability through **interlocking of rock fragments** and **frictional resistance**. An impervious upstream membrane (e.g., reinforced concrete, geomembrane) prevents water infiltration.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Rockfill with Impervious Core (Central Core Type):** Core of clay or concrete
- **Rockfill with Upstream Membrane (Deck Slab Type):** Concrete slab on the upstream face
- **Rockfill with Upstream Asphalt-Facing Type**

---

### 2.1.3 Masonry Dam

**Definition:** A dam built from **stone masonry** (coursed or random rubble) bonded with **cement/lime mortar**. Often gravity-type in behavior.

**Engineering Principle:** Combines the strength of stone with the cohesion of mortar. Generally used for **small to medium dams** where skilled labor and good stone are locally available.

---

### 2.1.4 Concrete Dam

**Definition:** A dam constructed primarily from **plain or reinforced concrete**. This is a material-based umbrella that includes structural sub-types (gravity, arch, buttress).

**Engineering Principle:** Concrete provides high **compressive strength** (typically 20ŌĆō30 MPa) and allows complex shaping. Its high **self-weight** is exploited in gravity dams to resist hydrostatic pressure.

---

### 2.1.5 Steel and Timber Dams

- **Steel Dam:** Used in **temporary or low-head applications**. Plates are anchored to a rigid framework.
- **Timber Dam:** Used in **small irrigation works**. Modern usage is rare due to durability concerns.

---

## 2.2 Classification (B) ŌĆö Based on Structural Behavior

This is the most academically rigorous classification and forms the core of KTU Module 3.

### 2.2.1 Gravity Dam

**Definition:** A massive dam constructed of **concrete or masonry** that resists the horizontal hydrostatic pressure and other forces by its **own weight (gravity action)** alone.

**Theoretical Foundation:** The dam behaves as a **rigid monolithic block** under combined loads. The resultant of all forces must pass through the **middle third** of the base to avoid tensile stresses (as per the **middle-third rule / kern limit**).

**Forces Acting on a Gravity Dam:**
1. **Self-weight (W)** ŌĆö vertical, downward
2. **Hydrostatic Pressure (P)** ŌĆö horizontal, on upstream face
3. **Uplift Pressure (U)** ŌĆö upward, distributed across base
4. **Silt Pressure (Ps)** ŌĆö horizontal, on upstream face
5. **Wave Pressure (Pw)** ŌĆö horizontal, near water surface
6. **Ice Pressure (Pi)** ŌĆö horizontal (in cold climates)
7. **Earthquake Forces** ŌĆö horizontal & vertical inertia forces
8. **Wind Pressure** ŌĆö on superstructure

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it work?** The horizontal water thrust tries to **overturn** the dam and **slide** it downstream. The **vertical weight** of the massive concrete block provides a **resisting moment** (against overturning) and a **frictional force** (against sliding) that exceeds the destabilizing forces. Stability is checked using **overturning, sliding, and stress criteria**.

**Typical Cross-Section of a Gravity Dam:**
- **Upstream face:** Vertical or slightly inclined (battered)
- **Downstream face:** Stepped or sloping (battered at $\approx 0.7H : 1V$ to $0.8H : 1V$)
- **Base width:** $B \approx 0.6H$ to $0.8H$ (where $H$ is dam height)

---

### 2.2.2 Arch Dam

**Definition:** A dam that transfers the major portion of the water load to the **abutments** (valley walls) by **arch action** (horizontal compression), rather than relying solely on self-weight.

**Engineering Principle:** Arch dams are designed using the **thin-shell theory** of cylindrical or dome-shaped arches. The water load generates **compressive stresses** transmitted along the arch to the canyon walls.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Cylindrical Arch Dam** ŌĆö constant radius
- **Constant Angle Arch Dam** ŌĆö variable radius, constant central angle
- **Double Curvature Arch Dam** ŌĆö curved both horizontally and vertically
- **Cupola Arch Dam** ŌĆö for very wide valleys

**Site Requirements:**
- Narrow valley (preferably **width-to-height ratio $< 5$**)
- Sound, strong **rock abutments** capable of resisting arch thrust
- Stable rock foundation to minimize deformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Arch dams are **economical in concrete volume** (about 1/5 to 1/10 of gravity dam) but demand **excellent geology**. Examples in India: **Idukki Dam (Kerala)**, **Jog Dam (Karnataka)**.

---

### 2.2.3 Buttress Dam

**Definition:** A dam consisting of a **sloping upstream membrane (deck slab)** supported on the downstream side by a series of **triangular-shaped walls called buttresses**.

**Engineering Principle:** The **deck slab** transfers water load to the **buttresses**, which in turn transmit it to the foundation. The voids between buttresses reduce concrete volume and uplift pressure.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Deck Slab Buttress Dam** ŌĆö flat slab supported by buttresses
- **Multiple Arch Buttress Dam** ŌĆö multiple arches span between buttresses
- **Multiple Dome Buttress Dam** ŌĆö domes between buttresses
- **Mass-head Buttress Dam** ŌĆö solid head (similar to small gravity section)

**Advantages over Gravity Dam:**
- **Reduced concrete volume** (40ŌĆō60% saving)
- **Lower uplift pressure** due to open spaces
- Allows visual inspection of upstream face

**Disadvantages:**
- **Complex formwork and reinforcement**
- **Higher unit cost** of construction
- **Deterioration of reinforcement** if water infiltrates

---

### 2.2.4 Embankment Dam (Earth & Rockfill)

**Definition:** A non-rigid dam constructed from **natural earth or rockfill materials**. This category includes **earthfill** and **rockfill** dams.

**Engineering Principle:** Stability comes from **mass and shear strength** of the material. Failure modes include:
- **Slope failure** (sloughing)
- **Seepage failure** (piping)
- **Overtopping failure** (the most common cause of embankment dam failure)

---

## 2.3 Classification (C) ŌĆö Based on Hydraulic Design

| Type | Description | Example Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Overflow Dam (Spillway Dam)** | Designed to allow water to pass over the crest | Concrete gravity dams with ogee spillways |
| **Non-Overflow Dam** | Designed to retain water without overflow; spillway is separate | Earthen dams with separate spillway |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Earthen dams can **NEVER** be designed as overflow dams. They are **strictly non-overflow**, with a separate **spillway structure** constructed at a saddle or in a side channel.

---

## 2.4 Classification (D) ŌĆö Based on Purpose

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Storage Dam** | Stores water for supply, irrigation, hydropower | Bhakra Dam |
| **Diversion Dam** | Diverts water into canals | A canal headworks weir |
| **Detention Dam** | Delays flood runoff; may be dry in normal times | Small flood-control dams |
| **Coffer Dam** | Temporary barrier to enclose construction site | Used during dam construction |
| **Debris Dam** | Retains debris/sediment | Mountain streams |
| **Check Dam** | Small dam to control gully erosion | Nala training works |

---

## 2.5 Classification (E) ŌĆö Based on Size

| Category | Height (H) | Capacity / Other |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Small Dam** | $< 15$ m | ŌĆö |
| **Intermediate Dam** | $15$ to $30$ m | ŌĆö |
| **Large Dam** | $> 30$ m | ŌĆö |
| **Major Dam** (ICOLD) | $\geq 15$ m | Reservoir $\geq 3$ million m┬│ |
| **Mega Dam** | $\geq 150$ m | ŌĆö |

---

## 2.6 KTU Reference Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Engineering Properties

| Property | Gravity Dam | Earthen Dam | Rockfill Dam | Arch Dam | Buttress Dam |
|----------|:-----------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:--------:|:------------:|
| Material | Concrete / Masonry | Compacted earth | Rockfill + membrane | Concrete | Concrete + reinforcement |
| Structural Action | Self-weight | Self-weight + shear strength | Friction + interlocking | Arch action | Slab + buttress |
| Foundation Required | Strong rock | Alluvial / weak rock acceptable | Rock preferred | Very strong rock | Rock |
| Valley Shape | Wide to moderate | Any shape | Any shape | Narrow (W/H < 5) | Wide |
| Concrete Volume | High | None | Low | Very low | Low |
| Cost of Material | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Cost of Labor | Moderate | High | Moderate | High | High |
| Overturning Check | **Yes** | No (sliding circle) | No (sliding) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Sliding Check | **Yes** | Yes (phreatic) | Yes | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Can be Overflow | **Yes** | **No** | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Max Height Built | $> 250$ m | $> 300$ m | $> 250$ m | $> 300$ m | $\approx 130$ m |
| Example (India) | Bhakra Nangal | Hirakud | Tehri (lower) | Idukki (KL) | Krishnarajasagara |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The classification guides the **preliminary design phase** of any hydraulic project. The choice of dam type directly influences the **construction cost, time schedule, environmental impact, and long-term safety** of the project. A wrong classification at the outset can result in cost overruns exceeding **40%** of the project budget.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the topic *"Dams: Types"* is predominantly **descriptive and classification-based**, the analytical content here is focused on:
> (a) **The Middle-Third Rule** ŌĆö a foundational stability criterion for gravity dams.
> (b) **Sliding Factor of Safety** ŌĆö universal across gravity, arch, and buttress dams.
> (c) **Seepage Line Equation (Kozeny's parabola)** ŌĆö foundational for earthen dam design.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Middle-Third Rule (for Gravity Dams)

### 3.1.1 Statement

> *"For a gravity dam, the resultant of all forces acting on the dam (including uplift) must pass through the **middle third** of the base width to ensure that **no tensile stress** develops at the base."*

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation

Let:
- $B$ = base width of the dam
- $W$ = total vertical force (self-weight minus uplift)
- $P$ = horizontal hydrostatic pressure
- $e$ = eccentricity of the resultant from the centerline of the base
- $\sigma_{max}$, $\sigma_{min}$ = maximum and minimum vertical stresses at the base

**Step 1 ŌĆö Direct Stress at Base (No Eccentricity):**
If the resultant is **axial** (no eccentricity), the stress is uniformly distributed:

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{W}{B \cdot 1}$$

where the unit length = 1 m into the page.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Direct Stress + Bending Stress (With Eccentricity):**
By the **principle of superposition**, the stress at the base is the sum of:
- **Direct compressive stress:** $\sigma_d = \dfrac{W}{B}$
- **Bending stress** (linear distribution due to eccentricity $e$): $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 W \cdot e}{B^2}$

The maximum and minimum stresses at the **toe** and **heel** respectively are:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{W}{B} \left( 1 + \frac{6e}{B} \right)$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{W}{B} \left( 1 - \frac{6e}{B} \right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Condition for No Tension:**
To ensure $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$ (no tension at the heel):

$$\frac{W}{B} \left( 1 - \frac{6e}{B} \right) \geq 0$$

Since $W > 0$ and $B > 0$:

$$1 - \frac{6e}{B} \geq 0$$

$$\frac{6e}{B} \leq 1$$

$$e \leq \frac{B}{6}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation:**
The eccentricity $e$ is measured from the **centerline** of the base. The condition $e \leq B/6$ means the resultant must lie within $\pm B/6$ from the centerline, which is precisely the **middle third** of the base.

$$\boxed{e \leq \frac{B}{6} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Resultant lies in the middle third}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** If the resultant falls outside the middle third, the bending moment exceeds the direct compression, and the **heel** of the dam will develop **tensile stress**. Since concrete and masonry have **poor tensile strength** (only about 8ŌĆō10% of compressive strength), this would cause **cracking** at the heel, leading to seepage and progressive failure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sliding Factor of Safety (Universal Stability Criterion)

### 3.2.1 Statement

> *"The factor of safety against sliding of a gravity (or arch/buttress) dam is the ratio of **resisting forces** to **driving forces** at the base."*

### 3.2.2 Derivation

Let:
- $\Sigma V$ = sum of all vertical forces (self-weight, vertical component of water on inclined upstream face)
- $\Sigma H$ = sum of all horizontal forces (hydrostatic pressure, silt pressure, wave pressure)
- $U$ = total uplift force at the base
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at base (typically 0.65 to 0.75 for concrete on rock)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Resisting Forces:**

The **resisting force** is the **frictional force** developed at the base (per Coulomb's friction law):

$$F_{resist} = \mu \cdot (\Sigma V - U)$$

Note: $U$ acts upward, so it **reduces** the effective vertical force on the base.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify Driving Forces:**

The **driving force** is the **net horizontal force** trying to slide the dam downstream:

$$F_{drive} = \Sigma H$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Factor of Safety:**

$$\boxed{FS_{sliding} = \frac{\mu \cdot (\Sigma V - U)}{\Sigma H}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö KTU Standard Acceptance Criterion:**

For gravity dams, **IS 6512 (1984)** specifies:

$$FS_{sliding} \geq 1.5 \quad \text{(normal loading)}$$

$$FS_{sliding} \geq 1.2 \quad \text{(unusual/seismic loading)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö If FS is Insufficient:**

If $FS_{sliding} < 1.5$, the dam is widened at the base (increases $\Sigma V$ and $B$), or **shear keys** are provided at the base to engage additional **passive earth resistance** and shear strength of foundation rock.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Phreatic Line in Earthen Dams (Kozeny's Parabola)

### 3.3.1 Statement

> *"The free surface of seepage water through a homogeneous earthen dam without a horizontal drain follows a **parabolic curve**, first derived by Kozeny in 1931."*

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Consider a homogeneous earthen dam with a **vertical upstream face**, no toe drain. Let:
- $h$ = head of water above the base on the upstream side
- $d$ = height of the parabola above the base on the downstream side (the *focus height*)
- $x, y$ = coordinates of any point on the phreatic line (origin at the *focus*)
- $L$ = horizontal distance from the focus to the discharge point

**Step 1 ŌĆö Basic Parabola Equation:**

A parabola has the equation (focus at origin, vertex at $(-y_0, 0)$):

$$x^2 = 4 y_0 \cdot y$$

where $y_0$ is the **focal distance** (vertex to focus).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Boundary Conditions:**

At the **discharge point (D)** on the downstream slope:
- $x = L$, $y = d$ (the focus is at height $d$ above the base, at the discharge point)

Substituting:

$$L^2 = 4 y_0 \cdot d$$

$$y_0 = \frac{L^2}{4d}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Phreatic Line Equation:**

$$\boxed{x^2 = \frac{L^2}{d} \cdot y \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y^2 = (d \cdot x^2) / L^2 \text{ (parametric form)}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Discharge Point Elevation $d$:**

By the **Dupuit assumption** (parabolic flow, Kozeny):

$$d = \frac{L}{\sqrt{1 + m^2}}$$

where $m$ is the slope of the downstream face ($\text{H : V} = 1 : m$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The phreatic line must remain **well within the downstream toe** of the dam, not emerging on the surface, to prevent **sloughing, piping, and erosion failures**. A **toe drain or chimney drain** is provided to lower the phreatic line.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Forces on a Gravity Dam (Numerical Reference)

A KTU standard problem: A concrete gravity dam of height **30 m**, vertical upstream face, with **Top water level (TWL)** at full reservoir level and **Tail water level (TWL)** at **5 m above the base** (downstream). Assume:
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Unit weight of water $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.7$

The **base width** is taken as $B = 0.7 H = 21 \text{ m}$ (typical design).

| Force | Magnitude (kN/m run) | Direction | Lever Arm from Toe |
|-------|:--------------------:|-----------|:------------------:|
| Self-weight of dam ($W$) | $0.5 \cdot B \cdot H \cdot \gamma_c = 0.5 \cdot 21 \cdot 30 \cdot 24 = 7{,}560$ | Ōåō Vertical | $B/2 = 10.5$ m |
| Horizontal water pressure ($P$) | $0.5 \cdot \gamma_w \cdot H^2 = 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 30^2 = 4{,}414.5$ | ŌåÆ Horizontal | $H/3 = 10$ m |
| Uplift ($U$) | $0.5 \cdot \gamma_w \cdot H \cdot B = 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 30 \cdot 21 = 3{,}090.15$ | Ōåæ Vertical | $B/3 = 7$ m |
| Tail-water pressure ($P_t$) | $0.5 \cdot \gamma_w \cdot 5^2 = 122.625$ | ŌåÉ Horizontal | $5/3 = 1.67$ m |

**Net Vertical Force:**
$$\Sigma V = W - U = 7{,}560 - 3{,}090.15 = 4{,}469.85 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Net Horizontal Force:**
$$\Sigma H = P - P_t = 4{,}414.5 - 122.625 = 4{,}291.875 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Sliding Factor of Safety:**

$$FS_{sliding} = \frac{\mu \cdot \Sigma V}{\Sigma H} = \frac{0.7 \cdot 4{,}469.85}{4{,}291.875} = \frac{3{,}128.9}{4{,}291.875} \approx 0.729$$

> [!WARNING]
> **FS = 0.729 < 1.5** ŌĆö The dam is **UNSAFE** against sliding! In practice, the upstream face is **battered** (inclined) to add vertical component of water weight, or **shear keys** are added, or the **base width is increased**.

This matrix demonstrates the importance of **classification and sizing** in dam design ŌĆö a dam of the wrong type or dimension fails to satisfy the most basic safety criterion.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023 Model]**

**Q1. Classify dams based on the material of construction. Give two examples of earthen dams in India.**

**Model Answer:**

Dams are classified based on material of construction into the following types:
1. **Earthen Dams** ŌĆö constructed from compacted earth; e.g., **Hirakud Dam (Odisha)**, **Mettur Dam (Tamil Nadu)**.
2. **Rockfill Dams** ŌĆö constructed from compacted rock fragments with an impervious membrane; e.g., **Tehri Dam (Uttarakhand)**.
3. **Masonry Dams** ŌĆö built from stone masonry bonded with cement/lime mortar; e.g., **Nagarjuna Sagar Dam (Andhra Pradesh/Telangana)**.
4. **Concrete Dams** ŌĆö built from plain or reinforced concrete; e.g., **Bhakra Dam (Himachal Pradesh)**.
5. **Steel and Timber Dams** ŌĆö used for small, temporary, or low-head works; e.g., **Tansa Dam (Maharashtra)** uses some masonry, but pure steel dams are rare in India.

> (Any **four** types with one example each are sufficient for 3 marks.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a gravity dam and an arch dam in terms of structural action and site requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Gravity Dam | Arch Dam |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Material** | Concrete or masonry | Reinforced concrete |
| **Structural Action** | Resists water load by **self-weight** (gravity action) | Transfers water load to **abutments** by **arch action** (horizontal compression) |
| **Foundation** | Requires good rock, but is less critical | Requires **very strong, sound rock** abutments |
| **Valley Shape** | Suitable for **wide and narrow** valleys | Suitable for **narrow** valleys (W/H < 5) |
| **Concrete Volume** | High (full mass) | Low (thin shell) ŌĆö only 1/5 to 1/10 of gravity dam |
| **Stress Distribution** | Compressive throughout the section | Mainly compressive along the arch |
| **Example** | **Bhakra Dam** | **Idukki Dam (Kerala)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 ŌĆō Module 3 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of dams** based on (i) material of construction, and (ii) structural action. Discuss the **suitability of each type** with respect to site conditions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **concrete gravity dam** has a vertical upstream face and a height of **25 m**. The **top width** is 5 m and the **base width** is 18 m. The downstream face is inclined. Calculate:
- (i) The **horizontal hydrostatic pressure** at the base.
- (ii) The **self-weight** of the dam per meter run.
- (iii) The **factor of safety against sliding**, assuming $\mu = 0.7$ and neglecting uplift for simplicity.
Take $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Classification of dams is presented in two main categories:

**(i) Material of Construction:**
- **Earthen Dams** ŌĆö natural soil materials; preferred when soil is locally available and foundation is weak.
- **Rockfill Dams** ŌĆö compacted rock with impervious core/membrane; suitable where quarry stone is available.
- **Masonry Dams** ŌĆö stone + mortar; for small/medium heights.
- **Concrete Dams** ŌĆö plain or RCC; for medium/large heights on strong rock.
- **Steel/Timber Dams** ŌĆö temporary or low-head applications.

**(ii) Structural Action:**
- **Gravity Dam** ŌĆö resists loads by self-weight; suitable for moderate valleys with rock foundation.
- **Arch Dam** ŌĆö arch action transfers load to abutments; requires narrow valley with strong rock.
- **Buttress Dam** ŌĆö slab + buttress system; for wide valleys where concrete economy is desired.
- **Embankment Dam** ŌĆö earth/rockfill mass; for any valley shape with weak foundation.

Suitability is dictated by **topography, geology, material availability, and purpose**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Height $H = 25 \text{ m}$, Top width = 5 m, Base width $B = 18 \text{ m}$
- Upstream face vertical, downstream inclined
- $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.7$
- Uplift neglected

**(i) Horizontal Hydrostatic Pressure at Base:**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (25)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 625$$

$$P = 3{,}065.625 \text{ kN/m}$$

**(ii) Self-weight of the Dam per Meter Run:**

The cross-section is a **trapezoid** with:
- Top width = 5 m
- Base width = 18 m
- Height = 25 m

Area of trapezoid:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\text{Top} + \text{Base}) \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (5 + 18) \cdot 25 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 23 \cdot 25 = 287.5 \text{ m}^2$$

Self-weight per meter run:

$$W = A \cdot \gamma_c = 287.5 \cdot 24 = 6{,}900 \text{ kN/m}$$

**(iii) Factor of Safety Against Sliding (Uplift Neglected):**

$$FS_{sliding} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{P} = \frac{0.7 \cdot 6{,}900}{3{,}065.625}$$

$$FS_{sliding} = \frac{4{,}830}{3{,}065.625} = 1.5756 \approx 1.58$$

**Result:** $FS_{sliding} = 1.58 > 1.5$ ŌĆö **The dam is SAFE against sliding** (normal loading criterion, as per IS 6512).

> (If uplift were considered, FS would drop, and the dam might require a wider base or shear key.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a **buttress dam**? Explain its **types and merits** over a gravity dam. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of dams based on hydraulic design and purpose**. State the **Kreager criteria** for dam size. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A **buttress dam** is a type of dam in which a sloping **upstream membrane (deck slab)** is supported on the downstream side by a series of **triangular walls called buttresses**. The buttresses transfer the water load to the foundation.

**Types of Buttress Dams:**

1. **Deck Slab Buttress Dam** ŌĆö flat reinforced concrete slab supported by buttresses.
2. **Multiple Arch Buttress Dam** ŌĆö inclined arches span between buttresses; concrete economy.
3. **Multiple Dome Buttress Dam** ŌĆö domes span between buttresses; high-strength concrete.
4. **Mass-head Buttress Dam** ŌĆö massive upstream head resembling a small gravity section.

**Merits over Gravity Dam:**

- **Material Economy** ŌĆö 40ŌĆō60% saving in concrete volume.
- **Reduced Uplift** ŌĆö open spaces between buttresses reduce uplift pressure.
- **Visual Inspection** ŌĆö upstream face is accessible for inspection and repair.
- **Lighter Foundation Loading** ŌĆö reduced dead weight on the foundation.
- **Slab Design Optimization** ŌĆö deck slab acts as a continuous beam.

**Demerits:**

- Higher formwork and reinforcement cost.
- Skilled labor required.
- Risk of corrosion of reinforcement.
- Not suitable for very high dams (> 130 m).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Classification by Hydraulic Design:**

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Overflow Dam (Spillway Dam)** | Water passes over the crest; e.g., concrete gravity dams with ogee spillways. |
| **Non-Overflow Dam** | Retains water without passing it over the crest; requires a separate spillway. Earthen and rockfill dams are always non-overflow. |

**Classification by Purpose:**

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Storage Dam** | Stores water for irrigation, water supply, hydropower. |
| **Diversion Dam (Weir)** | Diverts water into canals at the headworks. |
| **Detention Dam** | Delays flood runoff; may be empty normally. |
| **Coffer Dam** | Temporary barrier enclosing the construction site. |
| **Debris Dam** | Retains sediments and debris. |
| **Check Dam** | Small dam controlling gully erosion. |

**Kreager's Criteria for Dam Size Classification:**

| Category | Height ($H$) | Reservoir Capacity |
|----------|:---:|:---:|
| **Small** | $H < 15 \text{ m}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Medium** | $15 \text{ m} \leq H \leq 30 \text{ m}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Large** | $H > 30 \text{ m}$ | ŌĆö |
| **Major** | $H \geq 15 \text{ m}$ | $\geq 3 \text{ million m}^3$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing Classification Bases** ŌĆö Students often mix up "material-based" and "structural action-based" classifications. (e.g., writing "gravity dam" under material-based is incorrect; it is a structural classification.)
> 2. **Forgetting Earthen Dam Slope Failure Modes** ŌĆö When discussing earthen dams, students forget to mention **piping, sloughing, and overtopping** as primary failure modes.
> 3. **Incorrect Use of Coefficients** ŌĆö In sliding FOS, students often forget to subtract uplift from vertical force.
> 4. **Wrong Units in Numerical Problems** ŌĆö $\gamma_w$ must be in $\text{kN/m}^3$ (not $\text{N/m}^3$) for consistency with force in kN.
> 5. **Neglecting Tail Water Pressure** ŌĆö Always check if the dam has a downstream water level; if yes, **subtract** tail-water pressure from upstream pressure in $\Sigma H$.
> 6. **Forgetting "Non-Overflow" Nature of Earthen Dams** ŌĆö Writing "earthen dam with overflow spillway" is a common error.
> 7. **Skipping Schematic Diagrams in Long Answers** ŌĆö A neatly labeled cross-section diagram earns 1ŌĆō2 extra marks even in descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List for KTU 2024 Module 3 ŌĆö Dams: Types**

- [ ] A **dam** is a barrier that impounds water; classified into 5 main heads: **Material, Structural Action, Hydraulic Design, Purpose, Size**.
- [ ] **Earthen dams** are the most numerous type worldwide (>80% per ICOLD); require local soil and a non-erosive downstream slope.
- [ ] **Gravity dams** resist loads by self-weight; stability checks: **overturning, sliding, base stress (middle-third rule)**.
- [ ] **Middle-Third Rule:** $e \leq B/6$ ŌĆö Resultant must lie within the middle third of the base to prevent tension.
- [ ] **Arch dams** transfer water load to abutments by **arch action** (compression); require **narrow valleys** and **strong rock**.
- [ ] **Buttress dams** use a **deck slab + buttresses**; **40ŌĆō60% concrete saving** over gravity dams.
- [ ] **Rockfill dams** use **impervious membrane** (concrete/steel/asphalt) on upstream face or **clay core** within the body.
- [ ] **Earthen dams CANNOT be designed as overflow dams** ŌĆö they are strictly non-overflow with separate spillways.
- [ ] **Sliding FOS:** $FS = \mu(\Sigma V - U) / \Sigma H \geq 1.5$ (normal) / 1.2 (seismic).
- [ ] **Dam Size Classification (IS 10137):** Small ($< 15$ m), Intermediate (15ŌĆō30 m), Large ($> 30$ m).
- [ ] **Kreager/ICOLD:** Major Dam = $H \geq 15$ m + capacity $\geq 3$ million m┬│.
- [ ] **Phreatic line** in a homogeneous earth dam follows **Kozeny's parabola**: $x^2 = (L^2/d) \cdot y$.
- [ ] **Forces on a gravity dam:** $W, P, U, P_s, P_w, P_i, F_{eq}$ ŌĆö always include **uplift** in design (except in simplified problems).
- [ ] **Indian Examples to Memorize:**
    * Gravity: **Bhakra Nangal (H.P.)**
    * Arch: **Idukki (Kerala)**
    * Earthen: **Hirakud (Odisha)**
    * Rockfill: **Tehri (Uttarakhand)**
    * Buttress: **Krishnarajasagara (Karnataka)**
- [ ] **Site Selection Priority:** Topography (valley shape) > Geology (rock strength) > Material availability > Cost.
- [ ] **Most common dam failures:** **Overtopping (earthen)** > **Foundation failure (gravity)** > **Abutment failure (arch)**.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes ŌĆö Dams: Types**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PECET755 ŌĆö Design of Hydraulic Structures*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gravity Dam: Selection of Site, Forces Acting, Stability Analysis and Modes of Failure, Principal and Shear Stresses, Elementary Profile, Limiting Height of Gravity Dams (High and Low Dams), Practical Profiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gravity Dam: Selection of Site, Forces Acting, Stability Analysis and Modes of Failure, Principal and Shear Stresses, Elementary Profile, Limiting Height of Gravity Dams (High and Low Dams), Practical Profiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Gravity Dam

A **Gravity Dam** is a massive structure of plain concrete or masonry construction, which relies upon its own **self-weight** (gravitational force) for stability. The external forces acting on the dam (primarily the horizontal hydrostatic pressure of the reservoir water) are resisted by the weight of the dam itself, which generates sufficient **overturning resistance** and **shear friction** at the base to prevent failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):** A gravity dam is defined as a structure whose stability against all external loads is ensured solely by its own weight, without relying on arch action (as in arch dams) or earth/rock reinforcement (as in embankment dams). The cross-section is designed such that the resultant of all forces lies within the "middle third" of the base to ensure no tensile stresses develop.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **giant concrete wall** holding back a lake. The deeper the lake, the harder the water pushes horizontally against the wall. Now, if the wall is too light or has a thin base, the water will tip it over (overturning) or slide it away (sliding). 

To prevent this, we make the wall **extremely heavy** and give it a **broad, triangular cross-section** (wide at the base, narrow at the top). This way, the wall's massive weight pushes straight down, creating enormous friction at the base to resist sliding, and creates a counter-moment that resists overturning.

> **Real-World Example:** The **Hoover Dam (USA, 221 m high)** and **Bhakra Dam (India, 226 m high)** are classic gravity dams. The cross-section appears as a right-angled triangle, exactly mimicking the elementary profile we will derive mathematically.

## 1.3 Components of a Gravity Dam (Visual Vocabulary)

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Crest** | Top portion of the dam; often carries a roadway |
| **Heel** | The upstream (water-side) edge of the base |
| **Toe** | The downstream (air-side) edge of the base |
| **Spillway** | Section designed to pass surplus water |
| **Gallery/Drainage** | Internal passages for inspection and seepage control |
| **Free Board** | Vertical distance between the maximum water level and the dam crest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Gravity" Dam?** The term emphasizes that the **weight (W = mg)** is the principal stabilizing force. This is fundamentally different from an arch dam, where horizontal arch action transfers loads to the abutments, or an earth dam, where the soil mass provides stability through shear strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Site Selection Criteria for a Gravity Dam

Selecting a proper site is the first step in the design process. The following factors govern site selection:

### A. Geological Considerations
- **Sound bedrock foundation** capable of carrying the heavy load without excessive settlement.
- **No geological faults, fissures, or weak seams** that may cause sliding or unequal settlement.
- **Impervious foundation** to minimize seepage and uplift pressure.
- Examples of suitable rocks: **Granite, Gneiss, Quartzite, Trap, Compact Limestone**.

### B. Topographical Considerations
- **Narrow valley** (river gorge) to reduce dam length and volume of concrete.
- **Wider at the top** of the gorge (for spillway placement) than at the base.
- A U-shaped or V-shaped valley with firm abutments is ideal.

### C. Hydrological & Environmental
- Adequate catchment area, stable river banks, and minimal silt issues.
- Distance from populated/agricultural areas for safety.

## 2.2 Forces Acting on a Gravity Dam

A gravity dam is subjected to numerous forces. Each must be considered in stability analysis.

### 2.2.1 Major Forces (Primary Loads)

| Force | Symbol | Description |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| **Weight of Dam (W)** | $W$ | Self-weight of concrete/masonry. Acts vertically through CG. |
| **Horizontal Water Pressure (P_H)** | $P_H$ | Hydrostatic pressure on the upstream face. |
| **Uplift Pressure (U)** | $U$ | Seepage water pressure acting on the base. |
| **Vertical Water Pressure (P_V)** | $P_V$ | Weight of water on the inclined upstream face. |
| **Silt Pressure (P_silt)** | ŌĆö | Horizontal pressure due to deposited silt. |
| **Wave Pressure (P_wave)** | ŌĆö | Dynamic force from wind-generated waves. |
| **Ice Pressure (P_ice)** | ŌĆö | Horizontal thrust from ice sheets (cold regions). |
| **Earthquake Forces** | $P_e$ | Inertial forces (horizontal & vertical) during seismic activity. |

### 2.2.2 Minor / Secondary Forces
- Wind pressure, thermal stresses, foundation pressure, and reaction of foundation.

## 2.3 Stability Analysis of a Gravity Dam

The dam must be stable against the following modes of failure:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modes of Failure of a Gravity Dam:**
> 1. **Overturning** about the toe
> 2. **Sliding** along the base or any horizontal plane
> 3. **Crushing/Compression Failure** of the material (max compressive stress)
> 4. **Tension Failure** (tensile stress at heel or any plane)
> 5. **Overturning/Compression at downstream face**

### 2.3.1 Stability Against Overturning

The dam must resist overturning about the **toe**. The factor of safety against overturning is:

$$
FOS_{OT} = \frac{\sum M_R}{\sum M_O} \geq 1.5
$$

where $\sum M_R$ = sum of resisting moments and $\sum M_O$ = sum of overturning moments (both taken about the toe).

### 2.3.2 Stability Against Sliding

The horizontal forces try to slide the dam. The factor of safety against sliding is:

$$
FOS_{SL} = \frac{\mu \sum V}{\sum H} \geq 1.0 \text{ (with uplift considered)}
$$

where:
- $\sum V$ = sum of vertical forces (weight minus uplift)
- $\sum H$ = sum of horizontal forces
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically 0.65 to 0.75 for concrete on rock)

If $FOS_{SL} < 1$, the dam must be keyed into the foundation or designed with a shear key.

### 2.3.3 Stability Against Compression

Maximum compressive stress at the toe (downstream end) must not exceed the **allowable compressive stress** of the material:

$$
\sigma_{c,\max} = \frac{\sum V}{B} \left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) \leq \sigma_{allowable}
$$

### 2.3.4 Stability Against Tension (Middle Third Rule)

For **no tension to develop** at the heel, the **resultant of all forces must lie within the middle third** of the base. This translates to:

$$
e \leq \frac{B}{6}
$$

where $e$ is the eccentricity of the resultant from the centerline of the base, and $B$ is the base width.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_H = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w h^2$ | Horizontal water pressure (triangle distribution) | kN/m |
| 2 | $P_V = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w h \cdot b$ | Vertical water on inclined face | kN/m |
| 3 | $U = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w h \cdot B$ | Uplift pressure (full intensity) | kN/m |
| 4 | $U_{drain} = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w h \cdot B(1 - \alpha + \alpha \beta)$ | Uplift with drainage gallery | kN/m |
| 5 | $FOS_{OT} = \sum M_R / \sum M_O$ | Overturning factor of safety | ŌĆö |
| 6 | $FOS_{SL} = \mu \sum V / \sum H$ | Sliding factor of safety | ŌĆö |
| 7 | $e = B/2 - \bar{x}$ | Eccentricity of resultant from toe | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max/min} = \tfrac{\sum V}{B}\left(1 \pm \tfrac{6e}{B}\right)$ | Vertical stress at toe/heel | kN/m┬▓ |
| 9 | $p_{max} = \sigma_y (1 - \cos\phi)$ | Principal stress (Maxwell's theory) | kN/m┬▓ |
| 10 | $q = \sigma_y - p = \sigma_y \cos\phi$ | Vertical stress at downstream face | kN/m┬▓ |
| 11 | $\tau = (p - q) \sin\phi \cos\phi$ | Shear stress on a horizontal plane | kN/m┬▓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definitions Used in Formulas:**
> - $h$ = depth of water upstream (m)
> - $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water = **9.81 kN/m┬│**
> - $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of concrete = **23.5 to 24 kN/m┬│**
> - $B$ = base width of dam (m)
> - $\phi$ = angle of the downstream face with vertical
> - $\bar{x}$ = distance of resultant from toe

## 2.5 Principal and Shear Stresses (Maxwell's Method)

The analysis of stresses within the body of the dam is performed on horizontal and vertical planes using **Maxwell's Stress Theory**.

- **Principal Stress ($p$):** Acts on a plane that is perpendicular to the resultant stress. It is the **maximum compressive stress** at any point inside the dam.
- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Acts on a horizontal plane due to the difference in stresses at upstream and downstream faces.

The downstream face is generally vertical in the elementary profile. The relationships given in the formula sheet (Eqs. 9-11) describe the stress distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Water Pressure on Upstream Face

Consider a vertical upstream face of height $h$ with water impounded up to the crest. The pressure at any depth $y$ is:

$$
p(y) = \gamma_w \cdot y
$$

The pressure varies linearly from **0 at the top** to **$\gamma_w h$ at the base**. The total horizontal force per unit length is the area of this triangle:

$$
P_H = \int_0^h p(y) \, dy = \int_0^h \gamma_w y \, dy
$$

$$
P_H = \gamma_w \left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^h = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w h^2
$$

This force acts at a height of **$h/3$** from the base (centroid of the triangle).

## 3.2 Derivation: Uplift Pressure on the Base

Water seepses under the dam, creating a pressure head equal to the upstream water depth $h$ at the heel and zero at the toe (if no drainage). The pressure distribution is linear (drainage-modified trapezoidal).

**Case 1: No drainage gallery** (full uplift)

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w h \cdot B
$$

**Case 2: With drainage gallery** (intermediate uplift)

Let:
- $\alpha$ = reduction factor at drainage point (typically 0.25 to 0.5)
- $\beta$ = ratio of (drainage location distance) to base width

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w h \cdot B (1 - \alpha + \alpha \beta)
$$

This force acts **vertically upward** through the centroid of the trapezoidal pressure diagram.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stress Distribution at the Base (Middle Third Rule)

Let the resultant of all vertical forces (weight minus uplift) be $V = \sum V$, and let the resultant of all horizontal forces be $H = \sum H$.

The resultant $R$ intersects the base at a distance $\bar{x}$ from the toe. By moment equilibrium about the toe:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum M_R - \sum M_O}{V}
$$

The eccentricity $e$ from the **center of the base** is:

$$
e = \frac{B}{2} - \bar{x}
$$

For combined direct and bending stress on the base, using the standard beam formula:

$$
\sigma = \frac{V}{B} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

where $M = V \cdot e$, and $I = \frac{B^3}{12}$ (for unit thickness), so $\frac{M}{I} \cdot y = \frac{V \cdot e \cdot (B/2)}{(B^3/12)} = \frac{6 V e}{B^2}$.

Thus:

$$
\sigma_{max,min} = \frac{V}{B} \pm \frac{6 V e}{B^2} = \frac{V}{B} \left(1 \pm \frac{6e}{B}\right)
$$

For **no tension** ($\sigma_{min} \geq 0$):

$$
1 - \frac{6e}{B} \geq 0 \implies e \leq \frac{B}{6}
$$

This is the **Middle Third Rule** ŌĆö the resultant must lie within the middle third of the base.

## 3.4 Derivation: Elementary Profile of a Gravity Dam (Maxwell's Theory)

The **elementary profile** is the theoretical triangular cross-section when the upstream face is vertical and **no tension is allowed** anywhere in the dam, with water at the crest level.

### Assumptions:
1. Upstream face is vertical.
2. No shear stress acts on the upstream face (since water pressure is normal to it).
3. The downstream face is inclined at angle $\phi$ with the vertical.
4. The dam is just full to capacity (no freeboard considered in elementary profile).
5. No uplift is considered (for theoretical analysis).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider a horizontal section at depth $y$ below the water surface. Let the width of the dam at this section be $b$. The vertical weight of the dam above this section is:

$$
W = \gamma_c \cdot b \cdot \frac{y}{2} \quad \text{(area of triangle) where } b = y \tan\phi
$$

**Step 2:** The horizontal water pressure at depth $y$ is:

$$
P_H = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w y^2
$$

**Step 3:** For the elementary profile, the **vertical compressive stress at the upstream face equals the horizontal water pressure** (to ensure no tension and minimum principal stress = 0):

$$
\sigma_v = p_w = \gamma_w y
$$

**Step 4:** Vertical stress at the upstream face due to the weight of the dam:

$$
\sigma_v = \frac{W}{b} = \frac{\gamma_c \cdot (y \tan\phi) \cdot y / 2}{y \tan\phi} = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_c y
$$

**Step 5:** Equating the two expressions (from Steps 3 and 4):

$$
\gamma_w y = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_c y
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\gamma_w}{\gamma_c}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting standard values: $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│ and $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m┬│:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{9.81}{24} \approx 0.408
$$

$$
\phi \approx 22.2^\circ
$$

**Result:** The downstream face makes an angle of ~22┬░ with the vertical for the **theoretical elementary profile** of a gravity dam.

**Step 7:** Base width of elementary profile:

$$
B = h \tan\phi = h \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\gamma_c} = h \cdot \frac{9.81}{24} \approx 0.408 h
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Limiting Height of a Gravity Dam

The **limiting height** is the maximum height at which the elementary profile can theoretically be constructed before the material crushes under its own weight.

**Step 1:** At the base of the elementary profile, the maximum principal stress occurs. By Maxwell's relation:

$$
p_{max} = \sigma_y (1 - \cos\phi)
$$

where $\sigma_y$ is the vertical stress at the downstream face (toe).

**Step 2:** Vertical stress at the toe is:

$$
\sigma_y = \gamma_c y \quad \text{(at depth } y = h \text{)}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the angle for elementary profile, $\cos\phi = \frac{\gamma_c}{\sqrt{\gamma_c^2 + \gamma_w^2}}$:

$$
p_{max} = \gamma_c h \left(1 - \frac{\gamma_c}{\sqrt{\gamma_c^2 + \gamma_w^2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Equating to allowable compressive stress $\sigma_{allowable}$:

$$
h_{lim} = \frac{\sigma_{allowable}}{\gamma_c \left(1 - \frac{\gamma_c}{\sqrt{\gamma_c^2 + \gamma_w^2}}\right)}
$$

For practical values ($\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m┬│, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│, $\sigma_{allowable} = 4200$ kN/m┬▓):

$$
h_{lim} \approx 137 \text{ m (theoretical)}
$$

**Real Significance:** Any gravity dam above this height is called a **High Dam**, and the elementary profile must be modified. Below this, it's a **Low Dam**.

## 3.6 Principal and Shear Stress Derivation (Maxwell's Stress Theory)

At any point inside the dam on a horizontal plane at depth $y$:

**Vertical stress:**
$$
\sigma_v = \frac{W}{b} = \gamma_c y \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right) \tan\phi \cdot \text{... (varies linearly)}
$$

**At the downstream face**, the vertical stress is:
$$
q = \sigma_y \cos^2\phi
$$

**At the upstream face** (which is vertical and where water pressure is horizontal), the principal stress is:
$$
p = \gamma_w y
$$

The **maximum principal stress** (compressive) at the heel:
$$
p_{max} = \sigma_y (1 - \cos\phi)
$$

The **shear stress** on a horizontal plane:
$$
\tau = (p - q) \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forces Acting on a Gravity Dam (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["VERTICAL FORCES (Stabilizing)"]:::vbox
    nodeB["Weight of Dam W"]:::sub
    nodeC["Vertical Water on Upstream Face Pv"]:::sub
    nodeD["Weight of Silt on Upstream Face"]:::sub
    nodeE["HORIZONTAL FORCES (Destabilizing)"]:::hbox
    nodeF["Hydrostatic Water Pressure PH"]:::hsub
    nodeG["Silt Pressure on Upstream Face"]:::hsub
    nodeH["Wave Pressure Pwave"]:::hsub
    nodeI["Ice Pressure Pice"]:::hsub
    nodeJ["Earthquake Forces Pe"]:::hsub
    nodeK["UPLIFT PRESSURE (Destabilizing)"]:::ubox
    nodeL["Seepage Water Uplift U at Base"]:::usub
    nodeM["STABILITY CHECKS"]:::chk
    nodeN["Overturning about Toe"]:::chk2
    nodeO["Sliding on Base Plane"]:::chk2
    nodeP["Compression at Toe"]:::chk2
    nodeQ["Tension at Heel - Middle Third Rule"]:::chk2
    nodeR["FOS OT greater than 1.5"]:::res
    nodeS["FOS SL greater than 1.0"]:::res
    nodeT["Sigma max less than Sigma allowable"]:::res
    nodeU["Eccentricity e less than B by 6"]:::res

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeQ
    nodeN --> nodeR
    nodeO --> nodeS
    nodeP --> nodeT
    nodeQ --> nodeU

    classDef vbox fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#f0fff4,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    classDef hbox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hsub fill:#fff5f5,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef ubox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef usub fill:#fffdf5,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef chk fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chk2 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#007bff,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#e2d5f1,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Elementary Profile of a Gravity Dam (Geometry Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Heel Upstream Toe of Base"]:::p1
    nodeB["Upstream Face Vertical Line of Height h"]:::p1
    nodeC["Water Reservoir Pressure Triangle"]:::p2
    nodeD["Top Crest"]:::p3
    nodeE["Toe Downstream End of Base"]:::p3
    nodeF["Downstream Face Inclined at phi = 22.2 deg"]:::p2
    nodeG["Base Width B = 0.408 h"]:::p4
    nodeH["Resultant Force R within Middle Third"]:::p4
    nodeI["Weight W acting at CG of Triangle"]:::p1

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeD --> nodeF --> nodeE --> nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeC -.presses.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.passes through.-> nodeG
    nodeI -.stabilizes.-> nodeG

    classDef p1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Stability Analysis Workflow (Decision Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Input Dam Geometry and Forces"]:::in
    nodeB["Compute Sum of Vertical Forces Sum V"]:::calc
    nodeC["Compute Sum of Horizontal Forces Sum H"]:::calc
    nodeD["Compute Moments about Toe"]:::calc
    nodeE["Calculate Eccentricity e"]:::chk
    nodeF{"e less than B by 6?"}:::gate
    nodeG["Middle Third Satisfied No Tension"]:::ok
    nodeH["Modify Section or Add Heel Width"]:::err
    nodeI["Compute FOS Overturning"]:::chk
    nodeJ{"FOS OT greater than 1.5?"}:::gate
    nodeK["Safe Against Overturning"]:::ok
    nodeL["Dam Unsafe Redesign"]:::err
    nodeM["Compute FOS Sliding"]:::chk
    nodeN{"FOS SL greater than 1.0?"}:::gate
    nodeO["Safe Against Sliding"]:::ok
    nodeP["Add Shear Key or Increase Base"]:::err
    nodeQ["Check Toe Compression"]:::chk
    nodeR{"Sigma max less than Allowable?"}:::gate
    nodeS["Dam Design Satisfies All Checks"]:::ok
    nodeT["Increase Base Width or Use Better Material"]:::err

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeK
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN -- YES --> nodeO
    nodeN -- NO --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR -- YES --> nodeS
    nodeR -- NO --> nodeT

    classDef in fill:#e2d5f1,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef gate fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```
