# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*List the **four principal functions** of a morning-glory spillway shaft. Why is a bell-mouthed crest preferred over a flat crest?*

**Model Answer:**

The four principal functions of a morning-glory spillway shaft are:

1. **Hydraulic conveyance** of reservoir overflow into the downstream spillway tunnel.
2. **Energy dissipation** at the crest by smoothly accelerating flow along a curved bell-mouth profile.
3. **Self-priming operation** ŌĆö the geometry allows the shaft to flow full without choking.
4. **Flow regulation** when combined with gates or orifice rings.

A **bell-mouthed crest** is preferred over a flat crest because:
- It provides a **gradually contracting flow area**, avoiding flow separation and turbulence.
- It yields a **higher discharge coefficient** ($C_d$ Ōēł 1.9 to 2.2) compared to a flat crest ($C_d$ Ōēł 1.6 to 1.7).
- It **suppresses air-entraining vortices** at low heads, ensuring stable flow conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):** 1 mark for each function (4 ├Ś 0.5 = 2 marks), 1 mark for the bell-mouth advantage.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of **water stops** in a gravity dam. Differentiate between a **dumbbell** and a **center-bulb** water stop.*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Water Stops:**
Water stops provide a **continuous impermeable diaphragm** across construction and expansion joints in a dam. They prevent seepage of water through the joint, which if left unchecked, would lead to uplift pressure buildup, internal erosion, and eventual piping failure of the structure.

**Dumbbell vs. Center-Bulb Water Stop:**

| Feature | Dumbbell | Center-Bulb |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Shape** | Two end bulbs connected by a flat web | Hollow bulb at the centre with two anchor flanges |
| **Movement capacity** | Minimal (Ōēż 5 mm) | Moderate (5ŌĆō25 mm) |
| **Application** | Construction joints | Expansion joints |
| **Stress concentration** | At the web (rigid) | At the bulb (flexible) |
| **Sealing mechanism** | Compression + adhesion | Bulb flexes with joint movement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for role, 1.5 marks for differentiation table.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**(a)** A morning-glory spillway shaft has a **throat diameter of 6 m**. The reservoir head over the crest is **5 m** under free-flow conditions. The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ = **1.95**. Determine:
- (i) The design discharge under weir flow. (3 marks)
- (ii) The design discharge if the shaft operates as a submerged orifice with head $H$ = **12 m** and $C_o$ = **0.72**. (4 marks)

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of galleries** provided in a gravity dam. State **two functions** of each. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B-A (a)

**Given Data:**
- Throat diameter $D$ = **6 m**
- Effective crest length $L_e$ = $\pi D$ = $\pi \times 6$ = **18.85 m**
- Head over crest $H_e$ = **5 m**
- Coefficient of discharge $C_d$ = **1.95**
- For orifice: $H$ = **12 m**, $C_o$ = **0.72**

**(i) Weir Flow Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{weir} &= C_d \cdot L_e \cdot H_e^{3/2} \\[4pt]
         &= 1.95 \times 18.85 \times (5)^{3/2} \\[4pt]
         &= 1.95 \times 18.85 \times 11.18 \\[4pt]
         &= 411.0 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \;\; (\text{cumecs})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Orifice Flow Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6^2}{4} = 28.27 \;\text{m}^2 \\[4pt]
Q_{orifice} &= C_o \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2gH} \\[4pt]
            &= 0.72 \times 28.27 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 12} \\[4pt]
            &= 0.72 \times 28.27 \times \sqrt{235.44} \\[4pt]
            &= 0.72 \times 28.27 \times 15.34 \\[4pt]
            &= 312.2 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
- **Weir flow discharge** = **411 cumecs**
- **Orifice flow discharge** = **312 cumecs**

> [!NOTE]
> The orifice flow is lower because the **submerged regime** restricts the head differential available for flow acceleration.

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B-A (b)

**Types of Galleries in a Gravity Dam and Their Functions:**

| Gallery Type | Location | Function 1 | Function 2 |
|--------------|----------|------------|------------|
| **Foundation Gallery** | At damŌĆōfoundation interface | Drilling and grouting of foundation | Inspection of foundation contact |
| **Drainage Gallery** | 3ŌĆō5 m above base, downstream of upstream face | Collection of seepage water | Reduction of uplift pressure on dam base |
| **Inspection Gallery** | At intermediate heights (every 15ŌĆō20 m) | Visual inspection of dam body | Housing of piezometers and instruments |
| **Grouting Gallery** | Near upstream heel at base | Curtain grouting of foundation | Consolidation grouting of rock mass |
| **Adit Gallery** | In abutments | Access to abutment rock | Drainage of abutment seepage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard gallery cross-section (IS 6512):** 1.5ŌĆō2.5 m wide ├Ś 2.0ŌĆō3.0 m high. Connected by **vertical riser shafts** of 1.0ŌĆō1.2 m diameter.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is a **shear key**? Derive the expression for the **total shear capacity** of a keyed construction joint, clearly stating the **three components** of resistance. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **trapezoidal shear key** for a construction joint with the following data:
- Lift height $L_j$ = **2.5 m**
- Normal force on joint $N$ = **800 kN/m**
- Cohesion $c$ = **0.5 MPa**
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ = **0.75**
- Characteristic concrete strength $f_{ck}$ = **25 MPa**
- Factor of safety required (normal) = **3.0**
- Applied shear $V_{applied}$ = **300 kN/m**

Check the adequacy of the design. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B-B (a)

**Definition of a Shear Key:**
A shear key is a **protrusion (or recess)** cast into the surface of a concrete lift to mechanically interlock with the next lift. Its purpose is to **transfer shear forces** across the construction joint and to **resist sliding** between adjacent pours.

**Derivation of Total Shear Capacity:**

The shear capacity of a keyed joint comprises **three components**:

1. **Adhesion (cohesion) component:** $V_{adhesion} = c \cdot A_j$
2. **Friction component:** $V_{friction} = \mu \cdot N$
3. **Key interlock component:** $V_{key} = 0.5 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b_k \cdot t_k$

By superposition:

$$
\boxed{V_{total} = c \cdot A_j + \mu \cdot N + 0.5 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b_k \cdot t_k}
$$

The Factor of Safety is then:

$$
FoS = \frac{V_{total}}{V_{applied}} \geq 3.0
$$

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B-B (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Key Depth (Standard Practice):**

$$
t_k = \frac{L_j}{15} = \frac{2.5}{15} = 0.167 \;\text{m} \;\; \text{to} \;\; \frac{L_j}{20} = 0.125 \;\text{m}
$$

**Adopt $t_k$ = 0.15 m (150 mm)**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Key Base Width:**

$$
b_k = 2 t_k = 0.30 \;\text{m} \;\; (\text{300 mm})
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Each Shear Component (per metre length of joint):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{adhesion} &= c \cdot A_j = 0.5 \times 10^3 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 500 \;\text{kN/m} \\[4pt]
V_{friction} &= \mu \cdot N = 0.75 \times 800 = 600 \;\text{kN/m} \\[4pt]
V_{key} &= 0.5 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b_k \cdot t_k \\[4pt]
        &= 0.5 \times 25 \times 10^3 \times 0.30 \times 0.15 \\[4pt]
        &= 562.5 \;\text{kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total Shear Capacity:**

$$
V_{total} = 500 + 600 + 562.5 = 1662.5 \;\text{kN/m}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Factor of Safety Check:**

$$
FoS = \frac{1662.5}{300} = 5.54
$$

Since $FoS = 5.54 \geq 3.0$, the **design is safe** and adequate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The trapezoidal shear key of 150 mm depth and 300 mm base width, with the given material properties, provides a factor of safety of **5.54** against sliding at the construction joint, which is well above the KTU 2024 Scheme minimum of **3.0**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity of approach head** in weir flow ŌåÆ underestimation of $H_e$ ŌåÆ under-design.
> 2. **Using $D$ instead of $L_e = \pi D$** in morning-glory discharge ŌåÆ off by a factor of **ŽĆ Ōēł 3.14** ŌåÆ 0.5ŌĆō1 mark lost.
> 3. **Confusing Lane's weight ratio** (1:3 for IS 6512) with the older USBR ratio (1:1) ŌåÆ wrong uplift calculation ŌåÆ 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Forgetting to include the key contribution** in the shear capacity formula ŌåÆ 50% under-design ŌåÆ 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Using linear creep length** instead of weighted creep length in uplift analysis ŌåÆ 0.5 mark lost.
> 6. **Omitting the units** in the final answer ŌåÆ KTU 2024 awards **0.5 mark deduction** per missing unit on numerical questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU 2024 Exam**

### Shafts
- Shafts in dams are **vertical hollow passages** for water intake, access, or control.
- **Morning-glory crest** = bell-mouth spillway with self-priming operation.
- **Weir flow:** $Q = C_d L_e H_e^{3/2}$ (with $L_e = \pi D$).
- **Orifice flow:** $Q = C_o A_s \sqrt{2gH}$ (with $A_s = \pi D^2 / 4$).
- **Transition submergence** at $H_t/D \approx 1.5$ to $1.8$.
- $C_d \approx$ **1.8ŌĆō2.2**; $C_o \approx$ **0.6ŌĆō0.8**.

### Keys
- Keys are **mechanical interlocks** at construction joints to resist shear.
- **Trapezoidal keys** are preferred (1:2 side slope).
- Standard depth: $t_k = L_j / 15$ to $L_j / 20$.
- Total shear capacity: $V_{total} = c A_j + \mu N + 0.5 f_{ck} b_k t_k$.
- Required FoS: **Ōēź 3.0** (normal), **Ōēź 1.5** (seismic).
- $\mu$ for concrete-on-concrete Ōēł **0.7ŌĆō1.0**.

### Water Stops
- Water stops seal **construction and expansion joints** against seepage.
- **Dumbbell** ŌåÆ construction joints (Ōēż 5 mm movement).
- **Center-bulb** ŌåÆ expansion joints (5ŌĆō25 mm movement).
- **Labyrinth** ŌåÆ high-movement joints.
- **Copper strip** ŌåÆ critical water-retaining joints.
- Hydrostatic pressure: $p = \rho_w g h$; tensile demand: $T \geq p \cdot b_{stop}$.
- Materials: PVC, rubber (EPDM), neoprene, copper.

### Galleries
- Galleries are **internal passages** for inspection, drainage, and grouting.
- **Five types:** Foundation, Drainage, Inspection, Grouting, Adit.
- Standard size: **1.5ŌĆō2.5 m wide ├Ś 2.0ŌĆō3.0 m high**.
- Vertical spacing: every **15ŌĆō20 m** of dam height.
- Drainage gallery **reduces uplift** via Lane's weighted creep principle.
- Galleries are connected by **vertical riser shafts** (1.0ŌĆō1.2 m diameter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Liner Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Shafts convey water, Keys transfer shear, Water Stops block seepage, and Galleries let engineers see, drain, grout, and instrument the dam from within."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grouting and Instrumentation in Dams (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grouting and Instrumentation in Dams (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Grouting in Dams

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Grouting in dams is a ground improvement and seepage-control technique in which a fluid mixture (cement grout, chemical grout, or bentonite slurry) is injected under pressure into the fissures, voids, joints, and porous zones of the foundation rock and/or the dam body to reduce permeability, increase shear strength, and seal the contact between the dam structure and its foundation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you have a **leaking concrete water tank** sitting on a brick plinth. Water seeps out through the tiny hairline cracks between the bricks and the tank base. Now imagine you take a syringe full of thick cement paste and inject it into all those cracks ŌĆö the paste fills the gaps, hardens, and becomes part of the structure. *Grouting in dams is exactly this process, but executed deep inside rock foundations, under high pressures, and on a massive engineering scale.* The dam foundation is the "tank," the rock joints are the "cracks," and the grout is the "paste."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECET755):** Grouting is treated as a *concept-only* topic. The student is expected to understand **why** grouting is done, **where** it is done in a dam, **what** materials are used, and **how** the operation is sequenced. Numerical design of grout curtains is *not* expected at this stage.

## 1.2 Instrumentation in Dams

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Instrumentation in dams refers to the systematic installation of measuring devices (piezometers, strain gauges, settlement markers, inclinometers, seepage weirs, plumb lines, accelerographs, etc.) within the dam body, its foundation, abutments, and reservoir perimeter, in order to monitor the structural and hydraulic performance of the dam during construction, first filling, operation, and long-term service life.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a dam as a **patient in the ICU** of a hospital. The patient has multiple sensors attached ŌĆö heart rate (strain gauge), blood pressure (piezometer), body temperature (thermometer), fluid output (seepage weir). Doctors (dam engineers) continuously read these sensors to detect early warning signs of trouble. *A dam cannot "speak," so instrumentation is its "voice" ŌĆö it tells the engineer whether the structure is healthy, stressed, or failing.* Without instrumentation, a dam is a silent giant; with it, the dam becomes a self-reporting structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - Grouting pressure: typically **0.5 MPa to 3.0 MPa** depending on rock type and depth
> - Water-Cement ratio (w/c) of cement grout: ranges from **0.6 to 1.5** (thin to thick mixes)
> - Typical grout curtain depth: **0.5 to 1.0 times the hydraulic head (H)** above the base
> - Pore-water pressure (u): measured in **kN/m┬▓** or **kg/cm┬▓**

## 1.3 Why Are Grouting & Instrumentation Taught Together?

In KTU Module 3, these two topics are paired because they are **complementary halves of dam safety**:
- *Grouting* is the **preventive** arm ŌĆö it strengthens and seals the dam *before* trouble starts.
- *Instrumentation* is the **diagnostic** arm ŌĆö it monitors the dam *continuously* to confirm that grouting (and other design measures) are performing as intended.

Together, they embody the NEP 2020 outcome of **engineering judgment**: a civil engineer must not only *build* a safe dam but also *verify* its safety throughout its lifespan (often **100+ years**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Grouting in Dams

Grouting operations in dams are classified by **location**, **objective**, and **stage of construction**.

| # | Type of Grouting | Location | Primary Objective | Typical Stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Consolidation Grouting** | Shallow foundation rock (under dam base) | Improve bearing capacity, fill near-surface voids, reduce compressibility | Before dam concreting / earth-fill placement |
| 2 | **Curtain Grouting** | Deep foundation, in a single line along dam axis | Form a vertical impervious barrier to cut seepage under the dam | After consolidation grouting, usually from a grouting gallery |
| 3 | **Contact Grouting** | Interface between dam concrete and rock foundation | Seal shrinkage/construction gap at the concrete-rock contact | After dam concrete has cooled and shrunk |
| 4 | **Blanket Grouting** | Broad area beneath dam base | Seal horizontal seams, weathered top rock | Often overlaps with consolidation grouting |
| 5 | **Backfill / Void Grouting** | Behind tunnel linings, around conduits, in cavities | Fill construction voids, stabilize surrounding ground | Construction stage |
| 6 | **Stage Grouting (Descending Stage)** | Deep holes drilled in stages from top down | Allow grouting of deep zones with progressively higher pressures | Used for very deep curtains |

## 2.2 Grouting Materials (Binders)

- **Cement Grout:** Portland cement + water (┬▒ sand for thick mixes). Most common. Used where voids are large (> 0.1 mm).
- **Bentonite Grout:** Clay-based, used for sealing, more flexible than cement.
- **Chemical Grout:** Sodium silicate, polyurethane, acrylics ŌĆö used for very fine fissures where cement cannot penetrate.
- **Composite Grout:** Cement + bentonite + chemical admixture, tailored for specific rock conditions.

## 2.3 Grouting Operation ŌĆö Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Hole Layout:** Drill holes in a planned pattern (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary) ŌĆö usually in a *split-spacing* arrangement.
2. **Stage Selection:** Decide between *upstage* (grout from bottom upward) and *downstage* (drill and grout in descending stages).
3. **Water Test (Lugeon Test):** Conduct a packer test to measure the rock's permeability in *Lugeons* (1 Lugeon = 1 litre of water per metre of hole per minute at an injection pressure of 1 MPa).
4. **Mix Preparation:** Start with a **thin mix** (high w/c) to allow penetration into fine fissures, then progressively thicken.
5. **Injection:** Pump grout under controlled pressure until the **refusal criterion** is met (no more grout accepted at the design pressure).
6. **Closure:** Closer-spacing holes (tertiary, quaternary) are grouted to tighten the curtain.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the Sequence:** Thin grouts penetrate fine cracks first; thick grouts fill larger voids. Pressure is increased gradually to avoid *hydrofracturing* (creating new fractures by over-pressure).

## 2.4 Instrumentation in Dams ŌĆö Classification

Instrumentation is classified by **parameter monitored**:

| Category | Parameter Measured | Typical Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | Pore water pressure, uplift | Piezometers (Casagrande, Vibrating Wire) |
| **Hydraulic** | Seepage quantity | V-notch weirs, Parshall flumes |
| **Deformation** | Vertical settlement | Settlement gauges, plumb lines, surveying markers |
| **Deformation** | Horizontal displacement | Inclinometers, extensometers, geodetic targets |
| **Strain / Stress** | Internal stresses in concrete | Strain gauges, stress meters, no-stress strain meters |
| **Seismic** | Earthquake response | Accelerographs (strong-motion instruments) |
| **Thermal** | Concrete temperature | Embedded thermistors/thermocouples |
| **Environmental** | Reservoir water level, weather | Staff gauges, automatic water level recorders |

## 2.5 Locations of Key Instruments in a Gravity Dam

- **Foundation Gallery:** Hosts piezometers (to read uplift pressure) and seepage measuring weirs.
- **Grouting Gallery:** Used for curtain grouting; later fitted with piezometers at the downstream end to check curtain performance.
- **Dam Body (within concrete):** Embedded strain gauges, thermistors, joint meters between monoliths.
- **Abutments:** Inclinometers and survey pillars to detect abutment movement.
- **Reservoir:** Water level recorders upstream and downstream.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examinations**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Darcy's Law:** $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | $Q$ = seepage discharge, $k$ = permeability, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $A$ = cross-section area | $Q$ in m┬│/s, $k$ in m/s, $A$ in m┬▓ | Quantifies seepage loss through dam foundation |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Gradient:** $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | $h$ = head loss, $L$ = flow path length | dimensionless | Used in flow nets |
| 3 | **Permeability from Lugeon Test:** $Lu = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot p}$ | $Q$ = flow (L/min), $L$ = test length, $p$ = gauge pressure (MPa) | Lugeon | Classifies rock tightness |
| 4 | **Uplift Pressure at a Point:** $u = \gamma_w \cdot h$ | $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water, $h$ = pressure head at the point | kN/m┬▓ | Verified by piezometer readings |
| 5 | **Effective Stress Principle:** $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | $\sigma'$ = effective stress, $\sigma$ = total stress, $u$ = pore pressure | kN/m┬▓ | Used to interpret piezometer data |
| 6 | **Grout Take:** $V_{take} = \dfrac{W_{cement}}{V_{rock}}$ | $W_{cement}$ = dry weight of cement injected; $V_{rock}$ = volume of rock treated | kg/m┬│ | Monitors grouting effectiveness |
| 7 | **Phreatic Line (Dupuit) for Earth Dam:** $y^2 = h_0^2 + \dfrac{2 q L}{k} \cdot (L - x)$ | $y$ = height of phreatic line, $h_0$ = downstream exit head, $q$ = discharge, $L$ = base length | m | Compared with piezometer observations |
| 8 | **Curtain Depth Rule of Thumb:** $D_c = 0.5\,H$ to $1.0\,H$ | $D_c$ = curtain depth, $H$ = hydraulic head | m | Preliminary design guideline |
| 9 | **Strain:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | $\Delta L$ = change in length, $L$ = original length | dimensionless or micro-strain | Read from strain gauges |
| 10 | **Seismic Acceleration (Pseudo-static):** $k_h = \dfrac{a_h}{g}$ | $a_h$ = horizontal peak ground acceleration, $g$ = gravitational accel. | dimensionless | For accelerograph data interpretation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dam Safety Bill (Govt. of India, 2021):** Mandates instrumentation for all "Specified Dams" above 15 m height or with reservoir capacity > 60 million m┬│. KTU expects students to know this regulatory linkage.
- **Failure Case Study ŌĆö Malpasset Dam (France, 1959):** 423 deaths; failure traced to foundation rock sliding. *Had inclinometers been installed in the abutment, movement could have been detected years before collapse.*
- **Failure Case Study ŌĆö Vajont Dam (Italy, 1963):** Overtopping by landslide-induced wave. *Surge and water-level sensors could not prevent it, but the lesson spurred modern reservoir slope instrumentation.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "design life" of a dam is 100ŌĆō150 years, but no material is creep-free forever. Instrumentation provides the *longitudinal data* (over decades) that no finite-element model can fully substitute.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> *Although Module 3 covers "Grouting and Instrumentation (Concept Only)," KTU 2024 examiners often ask for the derivation of the key governing equations that justify why these techniques work. The following derivations are the most-expected ones.*

## 3.1 Derivation: Darcy's Law from First Principles (Why Seepage Is the Enemy)

**Start:** Consider a small soil/rock element of length $\mathrm{d}L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, with a head difference $\mathrm{d}h$ across it.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Hydraulic Gradient Definition:**
The hydraulic gradient is the head loss per unit length of flow path.

$$
i = -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}h}{\mathrm{d}L}
$$

The negative sign indicates head decreases in the direction of flow.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Empirical Observation (Darcy, 1856):**
For laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, the discharge $Q$ is **proportional** to the cross-sectional area $A$ and the hydraulic gradient $i$.

$$
Q \propto A \cdot i
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Introduce the Proportionality Constant (Permeability $k$):**

$$
Q = k \cdot i \cdot A
$$

This is **Darcy's Law**. The constant $k$ has units of velocity (m/s) and is a property of the porous medium.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Discharge Velocity vs. Seepage Velocity:**
The *discharge velocity* $v = Q/A = k \cdot i$ is a *fictitious* velocity (assumes flow is uniform across the entire cross-section). The *actual* seepage velocity is:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{v}{n} = \dfrac{k \cdot i}{n}
$$

where $n$ is the porosity of the medium.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Limitation (Reynolds Number Check):**
Darcy's Law is valid only for laminar flow, typically $Re < 1$ to $10$. In large rock fissures (open joints), flow may become turbulent, and **Forchheimer's equation** applies instead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for Grouting:** Darcy's Law tells us that *if* we can reduce the permeability $k$ (by grouting), the seepage discharge $Q$ reduces linearly. A 10├Ś reduction in $k$ ŌåÆ 10├Ś reduction in seepage. **This is the mathematical justification for grouting.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lugeon Number and Conversion to Permeability

**Start:** A packer test isolates a section of borehole of length $L$ (in metres). Water is injected at a known pressure $p$ (in MPa). The flow rate $Q$ is measured in litres per minute (L/min).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define 1 Lugeon (Lu):**
1 Lugeon = 1 L/min of water injected per metre of test length, at an injection pressure of 1 MPa.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the Lugeon Value:**

$$
Lu = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot p}
$$

where $Q$ is in L/min, $L$ in metres, and $p$ in MPa.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert Lugeon to Permeability $k$:**
Using dimensional analysis (relating $Q$ in m┬│/s to $k$ in m/s):

$$
k = \dfrac{Lu}{12000} \quad \text{(approximate, for the standard 1 MPa pressure)}
$$

A *tight* rock has $Lu < 1$; a *highly fissured* rock has $Lu > 50$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering Decision Rule (Empirical):**

| Lugeon Value (Lu) | Rock Quality | Grouting Decision |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 | Very tight | Skip grouting |
| 1 ŌĆō 5 | Tight | Optional |
| 5 ŌĆō 20 | Moderately fractured | **Grout recommended** |
| 20 ŌĆō 50 | Highly fractured | **Definite grouting** |
| > 50 | Very open | **Heavy grouting, possibly with chemical grout** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks "Why is a Lugeon test conducted?" ŌĆö the answer is the three-in-one purpose: (1) measure in-situ permeability, (2) design grout mix & pressure, (3) decide whether grouting is even necessary.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Uplift Pressure at a Point in the Foundation

**Start:** The uplift pressure acting on the base of a gravity dam is the **pore-water pressure** that develops in the foundation rock and in the construction joints.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth $h$ below the phreatic surface:**

$$
u = \gamma_w \cdot h
$$

where $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│ (often taken as $10$ kN/m┬│ in KTU problems).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Effective Stress in Foundation (Terzaghi's Principle):**

$$
\sigma' = \sigma - u
$$

If $u$ rises due to poor grouting, $\sigma'$ (effective stress that the rock grains carry) **decreases**, reducing shear strength.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Drainage Reduces Uplift:**
A properly designed **drainage curtain** downstream of the grout curtain can reduce uplift by up to **66%** (Terzaghi's classic load distribution for drains). This is why **grouting + drainage** is a paired design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The KTU 2024 syllabus (and IS 6512) recommends that uplift pressure be reduced to:
> $$u_{eff} = \gamma_w \cdot h_{residual}$$
> where $h_{residual}$ is the head *after* accounting for grout curtain + drainage. This is verified using piezometers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Linking Grouting Operations to Instrumentation Verification

This matrix is a KTU-favourite concept question ("Explain how you will verify that grouting has been effective.").

| Phase | Grouting Activity | Instrument Used | Verification Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-grouting** | Lugeon test | Water flow meter | $Lu$ before grouting |
| **During grouting** | Pumping grout | Pressure gauge, flow meter | Grout take (kg cement per m┬│ rock) |
| **Post-grouting check 1** | Repeat Lugeon test | Same flow meter | $Lu$ should drop to < 3 |
| **Post-grouting check 2** | First reservoir filling | Piezometers in foundation | Uplift pressure $u$ should match design |
| **Post-grouting check 3** | Long-term operation | Seepage weir at downstream toe | Seepage discharge $Q$ should be within design limit |
| **Post-grouting check 4** | Annual safety audit | Inclinometer, plumb line, settlement gauge | Displacement < design tolerance |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Computing Seepage Quantity (for Concept Reinforcement)

**Problem:** A concrete gravity dam sits on a pervious rock foundation. The hydraulic head $H = 50$ m. The grout curtain reduces the foundation permeability to $k = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s. The effective seepage path length under the dam is $L = 75$ m, and the cross-sectional area of flow is $A = 200$ m┬▓. Calculate the seepage discharge $Q$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the hydraulic gradient:**

$$
i = \dfrac{H}{L} = \dfrac{50}{75} = 0.667
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Darcy's Law:**

$$
Q = k \cdot i \cdot A
$$

$$
Q = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.667) \times (200)
$$

$$
Q = 1.334 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to litres per day (for perspective):**

$$
Q = 1.334 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 \times 1000 \approx 11{,}526 \;\text{L/day}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering Interpretation:**
A seepage of ~11,500 L/day is **manageable** for a 50 m dam and is within typical design limits (Ōēż 0.1% of reservoir inflow for safety). If $k$ had been $1 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s (no grouting), $Q$ would have been ~1,152,000 L/day ŌĆö *unacceptable*, justifying the grout curtain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away for KTU Students:** Grouting is *economically* justified when the *cost of grouting* is less than the *cost of un-controlled seepage losses* (water loss, piping risk, uplift damage).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> *Since this is a concept-only topic and physical drawings of dam cross-sections are difficult to render in pure Mermaid, the diagrams below present **functional architecture flows** that map the engineering logic ŌĆö these are the same flow-charts examiners expect in answer scripts.*

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Grouting Operation Sequence in a Dam Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Project Foundation Preparation] --> B[Geological Survey and Lugeon Tests]
    B --> C{Is Rock Permeability Acceptable?}
    C -- No --> D[Design Grout Curtain Layout and Mixes]
    C -- Yes --> Z1[Skip Grouting - Proceed to Construction]
    D --> E[Stage 1 Consolidation Grouting Shallow Holes 5 to 15 m]
    E --> F[Concrete Dam Body Placement]
    F --> G[Contact Grouting at Concrete Rock Interface]
    G --> H[Stage 2 Curtain Grouting in Grouting Gallery Deep Holes 30 to 100 m]
    H --> I[Drill and Grout in Descending Stages for Deep Curtains]
    I --> J[Closure Grouting in Tertiary and Quaternary Holes]
    J --> K[Final Lugeon Test to Verify Effectiveness]
    K --> L{Acceptable Lu less than 3?}
    L -- No --> M[Re-grout or Adjust Mix]
    M --> H
    L -- Yes --> N[Install Instrumentation Piezometers Strain Gages Weirs]
    N --> O[First Reservoir Filling with Continuous Monitoring]
    O --> P[Long Term Dam Safety Surveillance]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Instrumentation Architecture in a Concrete Gravity Dam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DamBody
        SG[Strain Gauges in Concrete]
        TH[Thermistors Temperature]
        JM[Joint Meters Between Monoliths]
    end
    subgraph Foundation
        PZ[Piezometers Uplift Pressure]
        DP[Downstream Drainage Gallery]
    end
    subgraph Abutments
        IN[Inclinometers Slope Movement]
        PL[Plumb Lines Vertical Alignment]
    end
    subgraph Hydraulics
        SW[Seepage Weir V Notch at Toe]
        WL[Water Level Recorder Reservoir]
    end
    subgraph Seismic
        AC[Accelerograph Strong Motion]
    end
    PZ --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System SCADA]
    SG --> DAQ
    TH --> DAQ
    JM --> DAQ
    IN --> DAQ
    PL --> DAQ
    SW --> DAQ
    WL --> DAQ
    AC --> DAQ
    DAQ --> ENG[Dam Safety Engineer Decision Dashboard]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Grout Mix Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Fissure Width from Borehole Camera and Lugeon Data] --> B{Fissure Width}
    B -- Less than 0.1 mm --> C[Use Chemical Grout Sodium Silicate or Polyurethane]
    B -- 0.1 to 2 mm --> D[Use Thin Cement Grout w/c 1.0 to 1.5]
    B -- 2 to 10 mm --> E[Use Medium Cement Grout w/c 0.7 to 1.0]
    B -- Greater than 10 mm --> F[Use Thick Cement Sand Grout w/c 0.5 to 0.7]
    C --> G[Adjust Pressure 0.5 to 1.5 MPa]
    D --> H[Adjust Pressure 1.0 to 2.5 MPa]
    E --> I[Adjust Pressure 1.5 to 3.0 MPa]
    F --> J[Use Stage Grouting with Packers]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Cause-Effect Chain Linking Grouting, Instrumentation & Dam Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foundation Rock has Natural Fissures] --> B[Seepage Path Opens Under Dam]
    B --> C[Uplift Pressure Rises]
    C --> D[Effective Stress in Foundation Drops]
    D --> E[Risk of Piping and Sliding Failure]
    E --> F[PREVENTIVE MEASURE GROUTING]
    F --> G[Grout Fills Fissures and Reduces Permeability k]
    G --> H[Seepage Discharge Q Drops by Darcy Law]
    H --> I[Uplift u Drops]
    I --> J[Effective Stress Restored]
    J --> K[VERIFICATION BY INSTRUMENTATION]
    K --> L[Piezometer Reads Lower u]
    K --> M[Seepage Weir Reads Lower Q]
    K --> N[Inclinometer Reads Stable Abutment]
    L --> O[Dam Safety Confirmed]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Types of Piezometers (Most-Frequent KTU Diagram Question)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piezometers Devices to Measure Pore Water Pressure] --> B[Open Standpipe Piezometer Casagrande Type]
    A --> C[Hydraulic Piezometer Twin Tube]
    A --> D[Vibrating Wire Piezometer Modern Most Used]
    A --> E[Pneumatic Piezometer]
    B --> F[Simple Low Cost Slow Response]
    C --> G[Fast Response Suited for Earth Dams]
    D --> H[High Accuracy Digital Output Automated]
    E --> I[Used in Difficult Access Locations]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagrammatic Convention for KTU Answer Scripts:**
> - Always **label the dam zones** (upstream heel, drainage gallery, grout curtain, downstream toe).
> - Use **arrowheads** to show seepage flow direction.
> - Mark the **phreatic line** as a dashed curve.
> - Mark **uplift pressure diagram** as a trapezoidal distribution at the dam base.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 1] [CO3, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**

**Q1.** *Define grouting in dams. List any **three** types of grouting and state the objective of each in one line.*

**Model Answer:**
Grouting is the process of injecting a fluid binder (cement, chemical, or bentonite) under pressure into the rock or soil mass of a dam foundation to improve its strength and reduce permeability.

| Type | Objective |
|---|---|
| **Consolidation Grouting** | Improves bearing capacity and seals near-surface voids in foundation rock. |
| **Curtain Grouting** | Forms a deep, vertical, impervious barrier to cut seepage under the dam. |
| **Contact Grouting** | Seals the gap between dam concrete and foundation rock caused by shrinkage/cooling. |

> *(Any 3 valid types accepted; full marks for naming + objective.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 2] [CO3, Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Q2.** *What is a **piezometer**? Mention its location in a concrete gravity dam and the parameter it measures.*

**Model Answer:**
A piezometer is an instrument that measures the **pore-water pressure (uplift pressure)** at a specific point in the foundation or body of a dam.

- **Location in a gravity dam:** Installed in the **foundation gallery** and the **grouting gallery**, with tips embedded in the foundation rock at strategic points along the dam base (heel, mid-base, toe).
- **Parameter measured:** Pore-water pressure $u$ in **kN/m┬▓** (or m of water head).
- It helps verify whether the **grout curtain + drainage system** is performing as designed, by comparing measured uplift with the theoretical uplift pressure diagram.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model]* [CO3, CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with the help of a neat sketch the **sequence of grouting operations** in the construction of a concrete gravity dam. Also state the role of the **grouting gallery**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the various **types of piezometers** used in dams. Compare the **Casagrande (open standpipe)** type with the **vibrating wire piezometer** in terms of principle, response time, and accuracy.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

The grouting of a concrete gravity dam is carried out in a **strict, staged sequence** that aligns with construction progress. The sequence is as follows:

1. **Pre-construction Site Investigation**
   - Geological mapping and exploratory boreholes.
   - **Lugeon tests** to quantify rock permeability.

2. **Stage 1 ŌĆö Consolidation Grouting**
   - Holes drilled **5 m to 15 m deep** on a grid (typically 3 m ├Ś 3 m) over the entire dam base.
   - Grouted with thin cement grout (w/c Ōēł 1.0) under low pressure (0.3 to 0.5 MPa).
   - *Objective:* Improve bearing capacity and seal weathered top rock before placing concrete.

3. **Concrete Placement in Dam Body**
   - The dam is built in **monoliths** (separate blocks) with **vertical contraction joints**.
   - A **grouting gallery** is provided inside the dam, near the upstream face, running along the dam axis.

4. **Stage 2 ŌĆö Contact Grouting**
   - After the concrete has cooled and shrunk (typically 2 to 3 months after placement), grout is injected through short holes drilled from the gallery to the **concreteŌĆōrock contact**.
   - *Objective:* Fill the shrinkage gap.

5. **Stage 3 ŌĆö Curtain Grouting (Main Impervious Barrier)**
   - Deep holes (30 m to 100 m+) drilled from the grouting gallery, along a single line near the upstream heel.
   - Holes are arranged in **primary, secondary, tertiary** sequence (split-spacing) with progressively closer spacing (e.g., 6 m ŌåÆ 3 m ŌåÆ 1.5 m).
   - Grouting is done in **descending stages** with progressively higher pressure (1 to 3 MPa).
   - *Objective:* Form a vertical curtain to cut seepage.

6. **Closure Grouting**
   - Tertiary and quaternary holes are grouted to **close the remaining leakage paths**.

7. **Verification**
   - Repeat Lugeon tests in check holes.
   - Install piezometers downstream of curtain.

**Role of the Grouting Gallery:**
- Provides **access for drilling and grouting** without disturbing dam operation.
- Houses the **grout pipes, packers, and pressure gauges**.
- After grouting is complete, it doubles as a **drainage and inspection gallery** for instrumentation and maintenance.
- It is usually located **2 to 5 m** from the upstream face for efficient curtain placement.

*(Sketch: Standard textbook figure ŌĆö cross-section of gravity dam showing foundation rock, consolidation grout holes, grouting gallery, curtain grout holes, drainage holes, piezometer locations.)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Types of Piezometers Used in Dams:**

1. **Open Standpipe Piezometer (Casagrande Type)**
2. **Hydraulic Piezometer (Twin-Tube / Closed System)**
3. **Vibrating Wire Piezometer**
4. **Pneumatic Piezometer**
5. **Electrical Resistance Piezometer (Strain-Gauged)**

**Comparison: Casagrande vs Vibrating Wire Piezometer**

| Feature | **Casagrande (Open Standpipe)** | **Vibrating Wire Piezometer** |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Water rises/falls in a transparent standpipe; water level read with a dip meter. | Pressure deflects a diaphragm; tension changes the natural frequency of a stretched wire; frequency is measured electronically. |
| **Components** | Porous tip, standpipe (PVC or steel), water column, reading device. | Porous filter, pressure pad, vibrating wire, electromagnet coil, readout unit. |
| **Response Time** | **Slow** (hours to days) ŌĆö water must physically equilibrate. | **Very fast** (seconds) ŌĆö electronic measurement. |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (┬▒ 10 mm head typically). | **High** (┬▒ 0.1% of full scale). |
| **Automation** | Not possible (manual reading). | Fully automatable; integrates with SCADA. |
| **Cost** | Very low. | High. |
| **Suitability** | Earth dams, small projects, slow-changing conditions. | **Concrete gravity dams, arch dams, automated safety monitoring** (preferred by national dam safety authorities). |
| **Maintenance** | Easy; can be flushed. | Sensitive; requires calibration. |
| **Output** | Water level (manual). | Frequency / digital reading in kPa. |

> *Conclusion:* The Casagrande type is **simple and cheap** but **slow and manual**. The vibrating wire type is the **modern industry standard** for high-precision, automated, real-time dam safety monitoring.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Internal Choice Alternative* ŌĆö [CO3, CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Lugeon test**? Explain its procedure and state its significance in deciding the **grout curtain design** of a dam.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **various types of instruments** used for monitoring the **deformation** of a concrete dam. How is the data interpreted for dam safety?

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:**
A Lugeon test (named after Maurice Lugeon, 1932) is a **packer test** performed in a borehole to determine the **in-situ permeability** of rock, expressed in **Lugeon Units (Lu)**.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Drill a borehole** to the required depth in the foundation rock.
2. **Isolate a test section** of the hole (typically 5 m long) using **two inflatable packers** (top and bottom).
3. **Inject water** into the isolated section at a **standard pressure of 1 MPa** (10 bar).
4. **Measure the steady-state flow rate** $Q$ in litres per minute (L/min).
5. **Compute the Lugeon value:**

$$
Lu = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot p}
$$

6. **Repeat at different depths** along the borehole to build a vertical permeability profile.

**Significance in Grout Curtain Design:**

| Lugeon Value | Rock Condition | Curtain Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 Lu | Very tight | Curtain may be omitted. |
| 1 ŌĆō 3 Lu | Tight | Light curtain, low pressure. |
| 3 ŌĆō 10 Lu | Moderately fractured | **Standard curtain, primary + secondary holes.** |
| 10 ŌĆō 50 Lu | Highly fractured | **Deep curtain with stage grouting and high pressure.** |
| > 50 Lu | Very open | **Chemical grout or composite grout; stage grouting mandatory.** |

- Determines the **depth of the curtain** (deeper for higher Lu).
- Determines the **grout mix** (thinner for lower Lu, thicker for higher Lu).
- Determines the **injection pressure** (higher Lu tolerates higher pressure).
- Provides the **baseline** for post-grouting verification (Lu should drop to < 3 after grouting).

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Instruments for Monitoring Deformation of a Concrete Dam:**

| S.No. | Instrument | Parameter Measured | Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plumb Line** | Horizontal displacement at various elevations | A wire suspended from top of dam; weight hangs in oil dashpot; coordinates measured by optical or coordinate-reading plumb. |
| 2 | **Geodetic Survey Pillars** | Absolute horizontal & vertical displacement of dam crest and abutments | Theodolite / Total Station / GPS surveys at scheduled intervals. |
| 3 | **Inclinometer** | Lateral deformation in foundation or abutment | A probe lowered into a casing; measures tilt at each depth. |
| 4 | **Extensometer (Rod / Tape)** | Change in distance between two anchor points (e.g., across a joint or crack) | Mechanical or electronic measurement of $\Delta L$ between anchors. |
| 5 | **Settlement Gauge / Pendulum** | Vertical settlement within dam body | Vertical reference points read periodically. |
| 6 | **Joint Meter** | Opening/closing of construction joints | Embedded displacement transducer between monoliths. |
| 7 | **Strain Gauge (Vibrating Wire)** | Internal strain in concrete | Embedded in concrete; reads micro-strain. |
| 8 | **GPS Sensors** | Real-time horizontal/vertical displacement of crest | Satellite-based, continuous. |

**Data Interpretation for Dam Safety:**

1. **Baseline Establishment:** During the **first reservoir filling** and the first year of operation, all instrument readings establish the *baseline* (expected behaviour).
2. **Trend Analysis:** Subsequent readings are plotted as **time-history graphs**. Gradual trends are *expected* (creep, thermal effects); **sudden jumps** or **accelerating trends** are *red flags*.
3. **Comparison with Design Tolerances:** Each instrument has a *threshold* (warning level, alarm level). E.g., a plumb line showing > 5 mm/year horizontal drift in an arch dam may trigger an inspection.
4. **Correlation with Hydraulic Events:** Movements are correlated with **reservoir level, temperature, and seismic events**. A movement that correlates with reservoir level is *normal* (elastic response); a movement that **lags** or **fails to recover** during drawdown indicates *plastic/yielding* behaviour.
5. **Safety Audit:** Data are analyzed annually as part of the **Dam Safety Review Panel (DSRP)** report (mandatory under the Dam Safety Act, 2021).
6. **Predictive Modelling:** Modern dams feed data into **finite-element back-analysis models** to update safety parameters in real time.

> *A well-instrumented dam is essentially a "structural patient on life support" ŌĆö the data is its heartbeat, and the dam safety engineer is its cardiologist.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "grout curtain depth" with "dam height."** Curtain depth is typically 0.5 H to 1.0 H, *not equal* to dam height.
> 2. **Do NOT write "piezometer measures water level."** The correct phrase is "piezometer measures **pore-water pressure (uplift pressure)**." Water-level recorders (staff gauges) are different instruments.
> 3. **Do NOT omit the Lugeon units** when stating a permeability value. Always write "$Lu = 5$" ŌĆö never just "5".
> 4. **Do NOT say "grouting strengthens the dam."** The technically correct phrasing is "grouting reduces permeability and improves the *foundation's* bearing and shear strength" ŌĆö the dam *body* is not grouted (except for crack repair grouting, which is a maintenance activity).
> 5. **Do NOT list "thermometer" as a dam instrument** ŌĆö the correct term is "thermistor" or "thermocouple."
> 6. **Viva trap:** If asked "Why is a drainage curtain provided *downstream* of the grout curtain?", the answer is: *to relieve the residual uplift pressure* that bypasses the grout curtain. Students often forget this pairing.
> 7. **Numerical trap:** When using Darcy's law in KTU numericals, ensure you use **consistent units** (k in m/s, A in m┬▓, i dimensionless) ŌĆö marks are lost on unit conversion errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Reading)**

### A. Grouting ŌĆö Key Take-aways
- **Grouting = injecting fluid binder into rock/soil** to reduce permeability & improve strength.
- **Six major types:** Consolidation, Curtain, Contact, Blanket, Backfill, Stage.
- **Grout materials:** Cement (most common), Bentonite, Chemical (for fine fissures), Composite.
- **Grouting sequence:** Site investigation ŌåÆ Lugeon test ŌåÆ Consolidation grouting ŌåÆ Concrete placement ŌåÆ Contact grouting ŌåÆ Curtain grouting ŌåÆ Closure grouting ŌåÆ Verification (Lugeon + piezometer).
- **Mix progression:** Start thin (w/c Ōēł 1.5) ŌåÆ end thick (w/c Ōēł 0.5).
- **Pressure range:** 0.5 to 3.0 MPa; higher for deeper holes.
- **Hole pattern:** Split-spacing (Primary ŌåÆ Secondary ŌåÆ Tertiary ŌåÆ Quaternary).
- **Curtain depth rule of thumb:** $D_c = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$ where $H$ = hydraulic head.
- **Stage grouting:** Done for deep curtains; descending stage (top down) is preferred.
- **Lugeon Unit (Lu):** 1 Lu = 1 L/min per metre at 1 MPa. $< 1$ Lu = tight; $> 50$ Lu = very open.
- **Grout take metric:** kg of cement per m┬│ of rock ŌĆö measures effectiveness.

### B. Instrumentation ŌĆö Key Take-aways
- **Purpose:** Verify design assumptions, monitor long-term behaviour, comply with Dam Safety Act 2021.
- **Five categories:** Hydraulic, Deformation, Strain/Stress, Seismic, Thermal.
- **Key instruments:**
  - Piezometer ŌåÆ pore-water pressure (u)
  - Plumb line ŌåÆ horizontal displacement
  - Inclinometer ŌåÆ lateral deformation in rock/soil
  - Settlement gauge ŌåÆ vertical settlement
  - Seepage weir (V-notch) ŌåÆ seepage discharge Q
  - Accelerograph ŌåÆ seismic acceleration
  - Strain gauge ŌåÆ internal strain
  - Joint meter ŌåÆ monolith joint movement
  - Thermistor ŌåÆ concrete temperature
- **Casagrande piezometer:** Simple, cheap, slow, manual.
- **Vibrating wire piezometer:** Modern, accurate, fast, automatable ŌĆö *industry standard*.
- **Locations in a dam:** Foundation gallery, grouting gallery, dam body, abutments, reservoir.
- **Verification logic:** Compare measured values with design values; plot time-history; correlate with reservoir level, temperature, and seismic events.

### C. Equations to Memorize (For KTU Numerical/Analytical)
- Darcy's Law: $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$
- Hydraulic gradient: $i = h/L$
- Lugeon formula: $Lu = Q/(L \cdot p)$
- Uplift pressure: $u = \gamma_w \cdot h$
- Effective stress: $\sigma' = \sigma - u$
- Phreatic line (Dupuit): $y^2 = h_0^2 + (2qL/k)(L-x)$
- Grout curtain depth: $D_c = 0.5H$ to $1.0H$
- Strain: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$
- Pseudo-static coefficient: $k_h = a_h / g$

### D. Exam-Day Mnemonics
- **"C┬│ B┬▓ S┬▓"** for the 6 grouting types: **C**onsolidation, **C**urtain, **C**ontact, **B**lanket, **B**ackfill, **S**tage.
- **"P-S-I-T-J-S-A"** for the 7 key instruments: **P**iezometer, plumb line / **S**ettlement gauge, **I**nclinometer, **T**hermistor, **J**oint meter, **S**train gauge, **A**ccelerograph.
- **"Thin to Thick"** for grout mix progression.
- **"Lugeon before, Lugeon after"** ŌĆö always test before *and* after grouting.

> **End of Module 3 ŌĆö Grouting and Instrumentation in Dams (Concept Only).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth Dams: Types, Causes of Failure, and Design Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Earth Dams: Types, Causes of Failure, and Design Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Earth Dam** (also called an *embankment dam*) is a dam constructed from naturally available earth materials (soil, rock, sand, clay, gravel) compacted in successive layers with mechanical equipment, in which the principal structural element is the earth-fill itself rather than concrete or masonry. The dam relies on the **weight** of the fill material and the **imperviousness** of its core/shell to resist the stored water and control seepage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *"An earth dam is a trapezoidal-shaped embankment structure in which more than 50% of the total volume consists of compacted earth materials, and stability is derived from shear strength of the fill and its self-weight, NOT from a rigid monolithic structure."*

**Key Components (KTU High-Yield Terminology):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Impervious Core** | Central barrier of low-permeability soil (clay) that resists seepage |
| **Shell / Casing** | Outer pervious/semi-pervious zones providing structural strength |
| **Casing Filter / Transition** | Graded granular layers preventing piping of fines from core |
| **Rock Toe / Drainage Blanket** | Toe drainage controlling the phreatic line and pore pressure |
| **Cut-off Trench / Sheet Pile** | Foundation barrier reducing seepage under the dam |
| **Freeboard** | Vertical distance between crest and maximum reservoir water level |
| **Phreatic Line (Saturation Line)** | Upper boundary of the saturated zone within the dam body |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Permeability Benchmark (IS 1498ŌĆō1970, KTU reference):** Compacted clay core: $k \leq 1 \times 10^{-7}$ cm/s. Pervious shell: $k \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Layered Cake Analogy:**
Imagine baking a giant layered cake where the central **chocolate layer (clay core)** is the barrier that water cannot pass through, and the surrounding **vanilla and biscuit layers (sandy/gravelly shells)** give the cake its strength and shape. If the chocolate layer is cracked, honey (water) leaks through. If the outer layers are too steep, the cake collapses. The bottom of the cake is placed on a tray (foundation), and if that tray has holes (permeable foundation), the cake slowly empties.

**Simpler Analogy ŌĆö The Bucket of Compacted Soil:**
Take a bucket, fill it with alternating layers of sand and clay, and put water on one side. The water cannot pass straight through ŌĆö it must travel around the clay. The shape of the water surface inside the bucket (the *phreatic line*) is parabolic, and the bucket only stays intact if the wet side doesn't slide off. This is precisely what an earth dam does ŌĆö at a scale of **millions of cubic meters**.

**The "Why" behind Earth Dams (versus concrete dams):**

- Concrete dams need **strong rock foundations** (bedrock).
- Earth dams can be built on **any foundation** (alluvial, soft soils) provided seepage and settlement are controlled.
- They use **local materials** ŌåÆ economical for sites far from cement/steel.
- They are **flexible** ŌåÆ better suited to seismic zones.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why earth dams are preferred over gravity dams in certain sites?"* ŌĆö always anchor your answer to: (1) availability of local soil, (2) adaptability to weak foundations, (3) seismic resilience, and (4) economy in remote locations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Earth Dams (KTU Module 4 Core Topic)

Earth dams are classified based on **structural action** and **material zoning**:

### A. Based on Method of Construction

**1. Rolled Fill Earth Dam (Most Common, KTU-relevant)**
- Construction: Soil placed in thin layers (150ŌĆō300 mm), moistened, and compacted with sheepsfoot / pneumatic rollers.
- Modern practice: ~95% of all large earth dams.

**2. Hydraulic Fill Earth Dam**
- Construction: Soil is dredged, mixed with water (1:5 to 1:10 ratio), and pumped via pipelines; coarse particles settle near outer zones; fines settle near center forming a natural core.
- **Disadvantage:** Loose saturated shell prone to liquefaction ŌåÆ rarely used today.

**3. Semi-Hydraulic Fill**
- Compromise: Hydraulic placement + light rolling.

### B. Based on Material Zoning (KTU Most Frequently Asked)

**Type 1: Homogeneous Earth Dam**
- *Single material* of low permeability (silty clay) used for the entire section.
- A small **rock toe / horizontal drainage blanket** controls the phreatic line.
- Used when only one type of impervious soil is locally available.
- Limitation: Larger cross-section needed; phreatic line emerges on downstream slope ŌåÆ must be flattened or drained.

**Type 2: Zoned Earth Dam (Most Common Large Dam)**
- **Central impervious core** (clay) supported by **pervious shells** (sand, gravel, rock) on both upstream and downstream sides.
- **Transition filters** (graded sand-gravel) prevent piping of core fines into the shell.
- Casing materials provide stability; core provides water-tightness.

**Type 3: Diaphragm Type Earth Dam**
- Thin **vertical impervious diaphragm** (concrete, steel sheet pile, asphaltic concrete, or geomembrane) embedded into the foundation.
- Surrounded by pervious earth fill.
- Common for repair/raising of existing dams and where borrow soil is granular.

**Type 4: Rockfill Dam**
- Built primarily from **compacted rock fragments** with a thin upstream impervious membrane (concrete, asphalt, geomembrane).
- Modern technique using HDPE/geomembranes is growing rapidly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** For a 14-mark question, you must draw *labeled* cross-sections of at least **three** types of earth dams. Always show: (i) core/shell zoning, (ii) phreatic line, (iii) foundation cut-off, (iv) drainage toe.

## 2.2 Causes of Failure of Earth Dams (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Failures are categorized by **mechanism of failure**:

| Sl. No. | Cause of Failure | Mechanism | Typical Location |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overtopping** | Insufficient freeboard; spillway inadequate; wave action overtopping crest | Crest / downstream face |
| 2 | **Piping / Internal Erosion** | Seepage water carries fines ŌåÆ forms a pipe ŌåÆ progressive void ŌåÆ collapse | Core-shell interface, foundation contact |
| 3 | **Slope Failure (Upstream/Downstream)** | Pore-water pressure reduces effective stress ŌåÆ shear strength Ōåō ŌåÆ mass slides | Upstream during rapid drawdown; downstream during steady seepage |
| 4 | **Foundation Failure** | Weak pervious foundation ŌåÆ piping; weak clay foundation ŌåÆ shear failure | Foundation contact |
| 5 | **Settlement / Cracking** | Differential settlement of fill or foundation ŌåÆ transverse/hydraulic fracture | Crest, core |
| 6 | **Spillway Erosion** | Inadequate spillway capacity ŌåÆ erosion of abutment and breach | Abutments |
| 7 | **Seismic Liquefaction** | Loss of shear strength due to cyclic loading; rapid pore-pressure build-up | Saturated loose shell |
| 8 | **Burrowing Animals / Tree Roots** | Biological piping paths | Core / shell interface |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** *Overtopping* and *Piping together account for >70% of historical earth dam failures.* If asked to rank causes, lead with these two.

## 2.3 Design Criteria for Earth Dams (KTU Module 4 Outcome)

Earth dams must satisfy **five fundamental design criteria**:

### Criterion 1: Safety Against Overtopping
- **Freeboard** = vertical distance between crest and **maximum water level (MWL)**.
- **Total Freeboard** = Normal Freeboard + Wind Set-up + Wave Run-up.
  $$F_{total} = F_n + (h_w + h_r) + S$$
  where $F_n$ = normal freeboard, $h_w$ = wind set-up, $h_r$ = wave run-up height, $S$ = settlement allowance.
- **IS 1498 / USBR Recommendation:** $F_n \geq 1.5$ m for dams < 30 m height; $F_n \geq 2.0$ŌĆō$3.0$ m for taller dams.

### Criterion 2: Seepage Control
- Phreatic line must remain **entirely within the downstream shell** and exit into a **rock toe or drainage blanket** (never on the downstream slope).
- Seepage quantity (Schaffernak's formula):
  $$q = k \cdot s \cdot \sin \alpha$$
  with $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{1}{m}$ where $m$ = downstream slope (H : V).

### Criterion 3: Slope Stability
- **Factor of Safety against shear failure:**
  $$FS = \dfrac{\Sigma (c \cdot \Delta L + W \cos \theta \tan \phi)}{\Sigma W \sin \theta}$$
  where $c$ = cohesion, $\phi$ = angle of internal friction, $\theta$ = angle of slip surface with horizontal at element, $W$ = weight of slice.
- **Minimum allowable FS (KTU standard / USBR):**

| Loading Condition | Minimum FS |
|---|---|
| Steady seepage (downstream) | 1.5 |
| End of construction (upstream) | 1.3 |
| Sudden drawdown (upstream) | 1.2 ŌĆō 1.3 |
| Seismic condition | 1.0 ŌĆō 1.1 |

### Criterion 4: Foundation Cut-off and Filters
- **Cut-off trench depth:** $\geq 0.5 \times$ depth of pervious foundation, or extend to impervious stratum.
- **Filter criteria (Terzaghi):**
  $$\dfrac{D_{15,\text{filter}}}{D_{85,\text{base}}} \leq 4 \text{ to } 5 \qquad \dfrac{D_{15,\text{filter}}}{D_{15,\text{base}}} \geq 4 \text{ to } 5$$

### Criterion 5: Crest Width and Side Slopes
- **Crest width:** $b = \dfrac{H}{5} + 3$ (m)  (empirical; minimum 4ŌĆō6 m for maintenance access).
- **Side slopes** depend on material:

| Material | Upstream Slope | Downstream Slope |
|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous silty clay | 3 : 1 to 4 : 1 | 2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 |
| Zoned with rock shell | 2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 | 2 : 1 to 2.5 : 1 |
| Rockfill | 1.3 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 | 1.3 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 |

## 2.4 The "Why & How" of Phreatic Line Control

The **phreatic line** (top saturation line) inside an earth dam is the most critical hydraulic boundary. **Why?**

- Above the phreatic line ŌåÆ soil is **unsaturated**, higher shear strength.
- Below the phreatic line ŌåÆ soil is **saturated**, pore pressure reduces effective stress.

**How it is controlled:**

1. **Impervious core** ŌåÆ forces line downward.
2. **Internal chimney drain** ŌåÆ intercepts the line.
3. **Rock toe** ŌåÆ safely discharges seepage.
4. **Horizontal drainage blanket** ŌåÆ lowers line at downstream toe.

> [!TIP]
> **Casagrande's Method for Phreatic Line (Basic Parabola):** The phreatic line is approximated as a parabola with its focus at the entry point $A$ and directrix passing through the downstream starting point $B$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a = \sqrt{s^2 + d^2} - s$ | Casagrande's focal distance | m |
| 2 | $y = \sqrt{2ax + a^2}$ | Basic parabola equation (x from focus) | m |
| 3 | $x = a + \dfrac{y^2}{2a}$ | Alternative form | m |
| 4 | $q = k \cdot s$ | Seepage quantity (Casagrande) | m┬│/s per m length |
| 5 | $q = k \cdot s \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Seepage quantity (Schaffernak) | m┬│/s per m length |
| 6 | $\tan\alpha = 1/m$ | Exit angle (m = DS slope) | ŌĆö |
| 7 | $F_{total} = F_n + h_w + h_r + S$ | Total freeboard | m |
| 8 | $h_r = 0.014 \cdot V^{1.5} \cdot F^{0.5}$ | Wave run-up (Stevenson) | m |
| 9 | $FS = \dfrac{c \cdot L + \Sigma N \tan \phi}{\Sigma T}$ | Slope stability (Swedish circle) | ŌĆö |
| 10 | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma H F_s}$ | Taylor's stability number | ŌĆö |
| 11 | $b = H/5 + 3$ | Minimum crest width | m |
| 12 | $\dfrac{D_{15,f}}{D_{85,b}} \leq 5$ | Filter piping criterion | ŌĆö |
| 13 | $\dfrac{D_{15,f}}{D_{15,b}} \geq 4$ | Filter permeability criterion | ŌĆö |

> **Legend:** $s$ = horizontal projection of wetted portion, $d$ = vertical projection, $k$ = permeability, $m$ = DS slope (H:V), $H$ = dam height, $F_s$ = factor of safety, $V$ = wind velocity (km/h), $F$ = fetch length (km).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Casagrande's Basic Parabola for the Phreatic Line

**Physical Setup (KTU standard):**
A homogeneous earth dam of height $H$ retains water to depth $H_w$ on the upstream face (slope $m_1$ H : 1 V). The phreatic line enters the dam at $A$ (at upstream water level) and emerges at downstream toe $B$. Let $s$ = horizontal projection of wetted portion $AB$ and $d$ = vertical projection.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume a parabola:**
Let the phreatic line be a parabola with focus at $A(x_A, 0)$ and directrix as a vertical line $x = -x_0$. By the geometric definition of a parabola, every point $(x,y)$ on the phreatic line satisfies:

$$\text{Distance from focus} = \text{Distance from directrix}$$

$$(x - x_A)^2 + y^2 = (x + x_0)^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand and simplify:**

$$x^2 - 2x \cdot x_A + x_A^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2x \cdot x_0 + x_0^2$$

$$y^2 = 2x(x_0 + x_A) + (x_0^2 - x_A^2)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define focal parameter:**
Let $2a = x_0 + x_A$ and $a^2 = x_0 \cdot x_A$. Substituting:

$$y^2 = 4a \cdot x - 4 a \cdot x_A + 2a^2$$

Replacing $x_A = a - \dfrac{a^2}{x_0}$ (from $x_0 x_A = a^2$):

$$y^2 = 4ax - 4a^2 + 2a^2 + 4a \cdot \dfrac{a^2}{x_0} = 4ax - 2a^2 + 4 \cdot \dfrac{a^3}{x_0}$$

For $x_0 \to \infty$ (vertical directrix at infinity ŌåÆ parabola with horizontal axis tilted), simplify to:

$$y^2 = 4a(x - a/2)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Standard form (focus at origin):**
Shift origin to the focus $A$ so that $X = x - x_A$. The equation becomes:

$$Y^2 = 2a \cdot X \quad \text{or} \quad X = \dfrac{Y^2}{2a}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Boundary condition at point B:**
At point $B$, we have $X = s$ and $Y = -d$ (B is below the focus by $d$ and to the right by $s$):

$$s = \dfrac{d^2}{2a}$$

Solving for $a$:

$$a = \dfrac{d^2}{2s}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Casagrande equation:**

$$\boxed{\,a = \dfrac{d^2}{2s} = \sqrt{s^2 + d^2} - s\,}$$

The phreatic line equation (in shifted coordinates from focus $A$):

$$\boxed{\,x = a + \dfrac{y^2}{2a} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y = \sqrt{2a(x - a)}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Seepage Quantity by Schaffernak's Method

**Setup:** Consider unit length of dam. The basic parabola has the equation $y^2 = 2a \cdot x$ (focus at $A$). The velocity at any point on the parabola by Darcy's law:

$$v = k \cdot i = k \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$$

From $y^2 = 2ax$:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{a}{y}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Velocity at point on parabola:**

$$v = \dfrac{k \cdot a}{y}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total discharge per unit length (q) is the velocity at the exit point B multiplied by the area through which flow occurs.** At $B$ (downstream exit), the local flow thickness is approximated as $y_B = s \cdot \tan\alpha$:

$$q = v_B \cdot y_B = k \cdot a \cdot \tan\alpha$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Schaffernak's geometric approximation:**
At $B$, the exit angle $\alpha$ satisfies $\tan\alpha = 1/m$ where $m$ is the downstream slope (H : V). Also, $a \approx s$ for practical engineering cases (because the parabola is shallow):

$$q = k \cdot s \cdot \tan\alpha = k \cdot s \cdot \dfrac{1}{m}$$

**Final Schaffernak's Formula:**

$$\boxed{\,q = k \cdot s \cdot \sin\alpha = \dfrac{k \cdot s}{m}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Swedish Slip Circle Method (Fellenius / Ordinary Method)

**Setup:** A trial circular slip surface of radius $R$ is assumed. The sliding mass is divided into $n$ vertical slices of width $b$. For slice $i$ with weight $W_i$ acting at base inclined at angle $\theta_i$ to horizontal:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Driving (shear) force on slice base:**

$$T_i = W_i \cdot \sin\theta_i$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Normal force on slice base:**

$$N_i = W_i \cdot \cos\theta_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Resisting (shear strength) force from Mohr-Coulomb:**

$$S_i = c \cdot \Delta L_i + N_i \tan\phi = c \cdot \dfrac{b}{\cos\theta_i} + W_i \cos\theta_i \tan\phi$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum driving and resisting forces over all slices:**

$$\Sigma T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \sin\theta_i \qquad \Sigma S = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(c \cdot \dfrac{b}{\cos\theta_i} + W_i \cos\theta_i \tan\phi\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Factor of Safety:**

$$\boxed{\,FS = \dfrac{\Sigma S}{\Sigma T} = \dfrac{\sum \left(\dfrac{c \cdot b}{\cos\theta_i} + W_i \cos\theta_i \tan\phi\right)}{\sum W_i \sin\theta_i}\,}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Critical circle:**
The critical slip circle is found by **trial** ŌĆö varying the center $(x_c, y_c)$ and radius $R$ until the **minimum** $FS$ is found. For earth dams, the minimum is usually with the circle passing through the **toe** (toe circle) or **deep-seated**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem (adapted for KTU 2024 scheme):**
A homogeneous earth dam has the following data:
- Height $H = 25$ m, upstream water depth $H_w = 23$ m
- Upstream slope = 3 : 1 (H : V), Downstream slope = 2.5 : 1
- Coefficient of permeability of shell, $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ cm/s
- $c = 20$ kN/m┬▓, $\phi = 18┬░$, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m┬│

**Find:**
(a) Position of the phreatic line using Casagrande's method.
(b) Seepage quantity per meter length of dam using Schaffernak's formula.
(c) Factor of safety of the downstream slope under steady seepage.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Calculate $s$ and $d$ geometrically. The entry point $A$ is at the upstream water surface. The wetted portion projects horizontally:
$$s = (\text{top width}) + 0.3 H_w + (\text{DS slope}) \times H_w$$
(Using Casagrande's empirical correction $0.3H$ for upstream entry correction.)

**Part (b):** Apply $q = k \cdot s / m$ with $m = 2.5$.

**Part (c):** Try a toe circle, divide the sliding mass into slices, apply Swedish method. Detailed slice calculations are tabulated in the model answer of SECTION 5.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Homogeneous | Zoned | Diaphragm | Rockfill |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phreatic control | Difficult (rock toe needed) | Easy (core + filters) | Excellent (full barrier) | Excellent (membrane) |
| Material availability | Single soil | Multiple soils | Granular fill | Rock fragments |
| Seepage quantity | High | Low | Very low | Very low |
| Cost (for same H) | High (large section) | Moderate | Moderate-High | High (if imported rock) |
| Foundation suitability | Wide range | Wide range | Wide range | Needs competent foundation |
| Construction speed | Moderate | Moderate | Fast | Fast |
| Adaptability to raise | Easy | Moderate | Difficult | Moderate |
| KTU Board Question Frequency | **Very High** | **Very High** | Moderate | Low |
