# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Module 4]**
*"Differentiate between a homogeneous earth dam and a zoned earth dam with neat sketches."*

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

| Aspect | Homogeneous Earth Dam | Zoned Earth Dam |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Single soil type (low permeability) | Multiple materials in zones |
| Cross-section | Larger volume needed | Compact section |
| Seepage control | Difficult ŌĆö needs rock toe | Easy ŌĆö central core + filters |
| Stability | Lower (homogeneous mass) | Higher (rock shell support) |
| Cost | Higher (large volume) | Moderate |
| Sketch | Whole dam from one soil with toe drain | Central core, two pervious shells, transition filters |

> **Mark split (KTU):** 1 mark for each of: definition + 2 distinguishing points, 1 mark for sketch.

### Question A2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Module 4]**
*"List any six causes of failure of earth dams."*

**Model Answer (any six, ┬Į mark each):**
1. Overtopping of the crest
2. Piping and internal erosion
3. Slope failure (upstream and downstream)
4. Foundation shear failure
5. Settlement cracks
6. Seismic liquefaction
7. Inadequate spillway capacity
8. Burrowing animals causing biological piping

> **KTU Board Tip:** Always present the *cause* AND the *mechanism* (e.g., "Piping: seepage water erodes fines forming a pipe-like channel"). ┬Į mark for the cause, ┬Į mark for the mechanism = 1 mark each.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ­¤ōī Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 4, Modified]**
*(a)* Describe with sketches the different **types of earth dams** based on material zoning. Highlight the merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A homogeneous earth dam has the following data:
- Height of dam $H = 20$ m, water depth $H_w = 18$ m
- Upstream slope = 3 : 1, Downstream slope = 2.5 : 1 (H : V)
- Crest width = 6 m
- Coefficient of permeability $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ cm/s

Determine:
1. The position of the **phreatic line** using Casagrande's method.
2. The **seepage quantity** per metre length using Schaffernak's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Types of Earth Dams (7 marks):**

**(1) Homogeneous Earth Dam (2 marks)**

- *Definition:* Embankment constructed from a single type of impervious or semi-pervious soil.
- *Features:* Uniform material from upstream to downstream face; a small rock toe or horizontal drainage blanket is provided at the downstream side to control the phreatic line.
- *Merits:* Simple design; uses one borrow material; suitable for sites with limited soil variety.
- *Demerits:* Phreatic line emerges on downstream face ŌĆö must be flattened to 4:1 or 5:1; large cross-section; prone to slip failure on downstream side.

**(2) Zoned Earth Dam (2 marks)**

- *Definition:* Embankment with a central impervious core (clay) flanked by pervious shells (sand, gravel, rock) on both sides. Transition filters (graded granular layers) separate the core from the shells.
- *Merits:* Compact section; phreatic line easily controlled; high stability; accommodates different available materials.
- *Demerits:* Complex construction; requires careful filter design; multiple materials to manage.

**(3) Diaphragm Type Earth Dam (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Thin vertical impervious diaphragm (concrete, sheet pile, geomembrane) embedded in a pervious earth fill.
- *Merits:* Excellent seepage cut-off; quick construction; suited for raising existing dams.
- *Demerits:* Diaphragm susceptible to cracking from differential settlement; difficult to repair.

**(4) Rockfill Dam (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Dam made of compacted rock fragments with an upstream impervious membrane (concrete, asphalt, HDPE).
- *Merits:* Can be built in high rainfall areas; uses locally available rock; high strength.
- *Demerits:* Requires competent foundation; high cost if rock is not locally available; membrane prone to UV degradation if not protected.

> **Sketch allocation (KTU):** 1 mark for each correctly labeled cross-section. Always include: zoning, phreatic line, drainage, foundation cut-off.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $H = 20$ m, $H_w = 18$ m, $m_1 = 3$, $m_2 = 2.5$, $b = 6$ m, $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ cm/s $= 2 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry of the basic parabola (3 marks):**

The wetted portion $AB$ has horizontal projection $s$ and vertical projection $d = H_w = 18$ m.

By Casagrande, the entry point is shifted from the upstream water edge by $0.3 \times H_w = 0.3 \times 18 = 5.4$ m.

The horizontal distance $s$ from the corrected focus $A$ to the downstream starting point $B$ is:

$$s = b + (H - H_w) \times m_1 + 0.3 H_w + H \times m_2$$

$$s = 6 + (20 - 18)(3) + 5.4 + 20(2.5) = 6 + 6 + 5.4 + 50 = 67.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate focal distance (1 mark):**

$$a = \sqrt{s^2 + d^2} - s = \sqrt{(67.4)^2 + (18)^2} - 67.4$$

$$a = \sqrt{4542.76 + 324} - 67.4 = \sqrt{4866.76} - 67.4$$

$$a = 69.76 - 67.4 = 2.36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Phreatic line coordinates (2 marks):**

Using $y^2 = 2ax$ with $a = 2.36$ m, the phreatic line coordinates (with $x$ measured from focus $A$):

| $y$ (m) | $x = y^2 / (2a)$ (m) | Cumulative x from upstream water edge |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 (focus $A$) |
| 5 | 5.30 | 5.30 |
| 10 | 21.19 | 21.19 |
| 15 | 47.69 | 47.69 |
| 18 | 68.64 | 68.64 (point $B$) |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Seepage quantity (1 mark):**

Schaffernak's formula with $\tan\alpha = 1/m_2 = 1/2.5 = 0.4$:

$$q = k \cdot s \cdot \tan\alpha = (2 \times 10^{-7}) \times 67.4 \times 0.4$$

$$q = 5.39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m length}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final answers (box them ŌĆö KTU 2024 requirement):**

$$\boxed{a = 2.36 \text{ m}, \quad q = 5.39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m}}$$

---

### ­¤ōī Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 4]**
*(a)* Explain the **causes of failure of earth dams** in detail. Discuss in particular the phenomenon of **piping** and the design measures to prevent it. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A zoned earth dam has the following slope stability data under **steady seepage condition**:
- Height $H = 30$ m, downstream slope = 2.5 : 1
- $c = 25$ kN/m┬▓, $\phi = 20┬░$, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m┬│
- Critical slip circle: radius $R = 45$ m, divided into 6 slices, with the following slice data:

| Slice | Weight $W$ (kN/m) | Base angle $\theta$ (┬░) | Base length $\Delta L$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 320 | 28 | 9.4 |
| 2 | 540 | 22 | 8.1 |
| 3 | 680 | 14 | 7.7 |
| 4 | 720 | 6 | 7.5 |
| 5 | 640 | -4 | 7.7 |
| 6 | 460 | -16 | 8.3 |

Compute the **factor of safety** of the downstream slope using the **Swedish Slip Circle (Fellenius) method**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Causes of Failure and Piping (7 marks):**

**Major Causes (4 marks):**

**1. Overtopping (1 mark):** When the reservoir level exceeds the dam crest due to inadequate spillway capacity, insufficient freeboard, or wave action. Water flowing over the crest erodes the downstream face, rapidly deepening a breach. *Design measure:* Provide adequate freeboard $F_{total} = F_n + h_w + h_r + S$ and a properly designed spillway.

**2. Piping / Internal Erosion (1.5 marks):** Progressive erosion of soil particles by seepage water, starting at the downstream toe. Fine particles are transported through voids, creating a pipe-like channel that grows upstream (backward erosion). Eventually a continuous pipe forms from upstream to downstream ŌåÆ sudden collapse. *Design measures:* (i) Provide a **rock toe** with **filter** at downstream end; (ii) **Filter criteria** to retain base soil: $D_{15,f}/D_{85,b} \leq 5$ and $D_{15,f}/D_{15,b} \geq 4$; (iii) Adequate **foundation cut-off** to reduce hydraulic gradient.

**3. Slope Failure (1 mark):** Loss of stability of upstream or downstream slope due to excess pore pressure, steep slope angles, or weak shear strength. *Design measure:* Limit factor of safety to 1.5 (steady seepage), 1.3 (end of construction), 1.2 (sudden drawdown) per USBR.

**4. Foundation Failure (0.5 mark):** Weak pervious foundation causing piping; weak clay foundation causing shear failure.

> **Piping phenomenon (1.5 marks):**
> - **Initiation:** Seepage exits at the downstream toe; if exit gradient $i_{exit} > i_{critical}$, soil particles mobilize.
> - **Progression:** Particles carried by seepage; voids enlarge ŌåÆ flow concentrates ŌåÆ higher velocity ŌåÆ more erosion.
> - **Failure:** Pipe grows upstream to the reservoir; dam collapses.
> - **Preventive design:** Provide a **graded filter** of coarse sand ŌåÆ fine gravel ŌåÆ rock, with $D_{15,f}$ small enough to retain base soil but large enough to be 4ŌĆō5 times more permeable than the base.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Swedish Slip Circle Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $c = 25$ kN/m┬▓, $\phi = 20┬░$, $\tan\phi = 0.364$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Tabulate the slice data with trigonometric values (3 marks):**

| Slice | $W$ (kN/m) | $\sin\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $\Delta L$ (m) | $T = W\sin\theta$ | $N = W\cos\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 320 | 0.469 | 0.883 | 9.4 | 150.1 | 282.6 |
| 2 | 540 | 0.375 | 0.927 | 8.1 | 202.4 | 500.7 |
| 3 | 680 | 0.242 | 0.970 | 7.7 | 164.4 | 659.8 |
| 4 | 720 | 0.105 | 0.995 | 7.5 | 75.3 | 716.1 |
| 5 | 640 | -0.070 | 0.998 | 7.7 | -44.7 | 638.4 |
| 6 | 460 | -0.276 | 0.961 | 8.3 | -126.8 | 442.1 |
| **Sum** | | | | | **420.7** | **3239.7** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention:** Slices where the slip surface tilts toward the direction of slide give $T$ as positive (driving). Slices with $\theta < 0$ (opposite tilt) give negative $T$ (resisting).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum the driving forces (1 mark):**

$$\Sigma T = 150.1 + 202.4 + 164.4 + 75.3 - 44.7 - 126.8 = 420.7 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$\Sigma N = 282.6 + 500.7 + 659.8 + 716.1 + 638.4 + 442.1 = 3239.7 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cohesive resistance (1 mark):**

$$c \cdot \Sigma \Delta L = 25 \times (9.4 + 8.1 + 7.7 + 7.5 + 7.7 + 8.3) = 25 \times 48.7 = 1217.5 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Frictional resistance (1 mark):**

$$\Sigma N \tan\phi = 3239.7 \times 0.364 = 1179.3 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Factor of Safety (1 mark):**

$$FS = \dfrac{c \cdot \Sigma \Delta L + \Sigma N \tan\phi}{\Sigma T} = \dfrac{1217.5 + 1179.3}{420.7}$$

$$FS = \dfrac{2396.8}{420.7} = 5.70$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Check against allowable FS and conclude:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Fellenius Method):**
> 1. **Sign of $T$:** For slices with $\theta < 0$ (i.e., slip surface tilts *away* from the direction of slide), the term $W \sin\theta$ is **resisting**, not driving. Setting it as positive (driving) inflates the denominator and gives a falsely low FS ŌĆö a common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Use of base length $\Delta L$ vs width $b$:** Cohesion is multiplied by the *arc length* $\Delta L = b/\cos\theta$, NOT the slice width $b$.
> 3. **Minimum FS by trial:** A single trial circle does not give the *critical* $FS$. You must check 3ŌĆō5 circles to find the minimum. The 14-mark question usually gives you the critical circle's data.
> 4. **Submergence vs. submerged unit weight:** Below the phreatic line, use $\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19$ kN/m┬│. Using $\gamma_{sat}$ throughout overestimates driving forces and underestimates FS.

> **Conclusion for the numerical problem:** $FS = 5.70$ is well above the minimum allowable of $1.5$ for steady seepage ŌĆö the slope is **safe**. However, in practice, such a high FS suggests the trial circle may not be the critical one; additional trials should be performed to confirm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 1 day before the KTU ESE exam.**

- Ō£ģ **Earth dam** = trapezoidal embankment of compacted soil; stability from self-weight + shear strength.
- Ō£ģ **Four main types** = Homogeneous, Zoned, Diaphragm, Rockfill.
- Ō£ģ **Zoned dam** is the most common large dam; has central impervious clay core + pervious shells + transition filters.
- Ō£ģ **Top 3 failure causes** = Overtopping (35%), Piping (30%), Slope failure (15%).
- Ō£ģ **Piping prevention** = Filter + Rock toe + Foundation cut-off.
- Ō£ģ **Phreatic line** = upper boundary of saturation; must exit into a filter/drainage, NOT on the downstream slope.
- Ō£ģ **Casagrande's focal distance** $a = \sqrt{s^2 + d^2} - s$.
- Ō£ģ **Schaffernak's seepage** $q = k \cdot s \cdot \tan\alpha = k \cdot s / m$.
- Ō£ģ **Slope stability FS** (Swedish circle): $FS = \dfrac{c \cdot \Sigma \Delta L + \Sigma N \tan\phi}{\Sigma T}$.
- Ō£ģ **Allowable FS**: 1.5 (steady seepage), 1.3 (end of construction), 1.2 (sudden drawdown), 1.0ŌĆō1.1 (seismic).
- Ō£ģ **Filter criteria (Terzaghi):** $D_{15,f}/D_{85,b} \leq 5$ and $D_{15,f}/D_{15,b} \geq 4$.
- Ō£ģ **Crest width** $b = H/5 + 3$ (m); minimum 4 m for maintenance.
- Ō£ģ **Freeboard** = Normal freeboard + Wind set-up + Wave run-up + Settlement allowance.
- Ō£ģ **Use submerged unit weight** $\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - 9.81$ for soil below phreatic line.
- Ō£ģ **Sign convention in Fellenius:** Slices with $\theta < 0$ are resisting, not driving.
- Ō£ģ **Critical slip circle** is found by trial (not single calculation).
- Ō£ģ **Foundation cut-off** depth Ōēź 0.5 ├Ś pervious foundation depth, or to impervious stratum.
- Ō£ģ **Rolled fill** is the standard construction method; hydraulic fill is obsolete due to liquefaction risk.

> **High-Yield One-Liners for KTU Viva/Exam:**
> 1. *"Earth dams are gravity structures in soil form."*
> 2. *"Piping eats the dam from toe to crest ŌĆö design filters to swallow the water, not the soil."*
> 3. *"Phreatic line is the dam's lifeline ŌĆö keep it inside the shell."*
> 4. *"Factor of safety is the dam's safety margin ŌĆö never less than 1.5 under steady seepage."*
> 5. *"Zoned dam = clay heart, rock body, filter lungs."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arch Dams: Thin Cylinder Theory, Types, and Methods for Design (List Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET755 - DESIGN OF HYDRAULIC STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arch Dams: Thin Cylinder Theory, Types, and Methods for Design (List Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Arch Dam ŌĆö Formal Definition

An **Arch Dam** is a curved masonry or concrete dam that derives its stability primarily from the **horizontal arch action** of the curved structure transmitting the reservoir water pressure laterally into the canyon walls (abutments) by compressive thrust, rather than relying solely on its own weight for stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET755 Module 4):**
> An arch dam is a structure curved in plan, designed so that a major portion of the water load is transferred horizontally to the abutments by arch action. Vertical cantilever action also exists, but the curved geometry carries most of the load in compression.

**General Nomenclature:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $H$ | Height of dam | m |
| $L$ | Chord length (crest) | m |
| $r$ | Mean radius of arch | m |
| $t$ | Thickness of arch at elevation | m |
| $\phi$ | Central angle of arch | degrees (┬░) |
| $p$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | kN/m┬▓ |
| $\gamma_w$ | Unit weight of water | **9.81 kN/m┬│** |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Curved Bridge Concept"

Imagine a thin curved sheet of plywood held at both ends between two rigid walls. If you push against its convex side, the load is transmitted sideways into the walls as **compressive thrust** along the curve. The plywood does not need to be heavy; it is *shaped* correctly.

**That is exactly what an arch dam does:**
- The **reservoir water** pushes against the **upstream convex face**.
- The **curved plan** of the dam converts this horizontal push into **in-plane compression**.
- The **canyon walls (abutments)** act like the rigid wooden supports in the analogy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not a straight gravity dam?** In a narrow V-shaped or U-shaped canyon, a straight gravity dam would require enormous self-weight (massive concrete) to resist overturning. An arch dam uses the geometry of the valley itself as a structural asset, drastically reducing concrete volume (often **40ŌĆō60% less** than an equivalent gravity dam).

---

## 1.3 The Thin Cylinder Theory ŌĆö Conceptual Foundation

The **Thin Cylinder Theory** (also called the **Elementary Cylinder Theory** or **Thin Arch Theory**) is the *simplest analytical model* used to estimate stresses in an arch dam. It treats the dam as a series of **independent horizontal arches**, each behaving like a thin-walled pressure pipe ring loaded by uniform radial water pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Assumption:** At any horizontal elevation, the dam behaves as an isolated thin cylindrical ring, **independent of the arches above and below it**. Vertical cantilever action is completely ignored in this elementary approach.

**Conditions where Thin Cylinder Theory is reasonably valid:**
- Dams in **narrow, V-shaped valleys** where arch action dominates.
- Dams where the **thickness $t$ is small** compared to the radius $r$ (i.e., $t/r < 0.1$).
- **Upper portions** of any arch dam (where cantilever stiffness is low and arch stiffness dominates).
- **Preliminary sizing** before more rigorous methods are applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Thin Cylinder Theory

The elementary thin cylinder theory is built on the following classical assumptions:

1. **Horizontal slice idealization:** The dam is conceptually cut into a stack of horizontal arches. Each arch is analyzed separately.
2. **Independent ring action:** Each horizontal arch acts as an isolated thin ring; vertical interaction between rings is neglected.
3. **Uniform radial pressure:** Hydrostatic water pressure $p$ at the elevation of the arch is assumed to act **uniformly** around the full extrados (upstream face) and **radially inward**.
4. **Thin-wall condition:** The thickness $t$ is small compared to the radius $r$ (typically $t/r \le 0.1$).
5. **Linear elastic, homogeneous, isotropic material:** Concrete is treated as a uniform elastic medium with constant Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$.
6. **Plain stress condition in the ring plane:** Stresses vary only in the circumferential and radial-thickness directions; out-of-plane shear is ignored.
7. **Fixed (built-in) abutments:** The arch ends are assumed fully restrained against rotation and translation (worst-case simplification).

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

Consider a thin horizontal arch of mean radius $r$ and uniform thickness $t$ subjected to uniform radial pressure $p$ per unit area.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cut the ring:**
Imagine cutting the ring at the crown and "unrolling" the two halves. The internal compressive thrust $H$ per unit height of dam acts tangentially at the cut section.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Equilibrium of a half-ring:**
The sum of horizontal forces on the half-ring (the radial pressure times the projected area) must equal the internal thrust at the crown.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Projected area of half-ring:**
The radial pressure $p$ acting on the half-ring's curved surface, when projected onto a vertical diameter, gives a resultant force:
$$F = p \cdot 2r$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Internal compressive thrust:**
For equilibrium, the thrust at the crown section (per unit height) is:
$$H = p \cdot r$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to hoop (compressive) stress:**
The thrust $H$ is distributed over the arch thickness $t$:
$$\sigma = \frac{H}{t} = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sign convention:**
Since the arch is in compression under water load, the **hoop stress is compressive** (negative in tension-positive convention, positive in compression-positive convention).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p = \gamma_w \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ below reservoir surface | kN/m┬▓ |
| 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ | **Hoop (circumferential) compressive stress in thin arch** | kN/m┬▓ |
| 3 | $H = p \cdot r$ | Total horizontal thrust per unit height at crown | kN/m |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Required thickness for given allowable stress | m |
| 5 | $\dfrac{t}{r}$ | Thinness ratio (should be $\le 0.1$ for validity) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\phi$ | Central angle of arch (typically $133.6┬░$ to $154┬░$ for economy) | degrees |
| 7 | $T = 2r \sin(\phi/2)$ | Chord length (span) of arch at elevation | m |
| 8 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{p \cdot r^{2}}{E \cdot t}$ | Temperature-equivalent radial deflection concept (for temp analysis) | m |
| 9 | $\sigma_{allow}$ | Allowable compressive stress in concrete (typically **7ŌĆō10 MPa** for M20ŌĆōM25) | MPa |
| 10 | $V_{cantilever}$ | Shear in vertical cantilever (zero in pure cylinder theory) | kN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize Formula #2 ŌĆö it is the single most-tested equation in the KTU Module 4 Arch Dam section.** It is the textbook definition of "Thin Cylinder Theory" for arch dams.

---

## 2.4 Limitations of Thin Cylinder Theory

| Limitation | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Ignores vertical cantilever action** | In reality, lower portions of the dam (near base) carry significant load by cantilever bending into the foundation. |
| **No provision for foundation flexibility** | Real abutments deform; the theory assumes infinite abutment stiffness. |
| **No temperature or shrinkage effects** | Concrete arches are highly sensitive to seasonal temperature drops, which induce tensile stresses. |
| **Uniform pressure assumption** | Real reservoir has silt pressure, ice pressure, and seismic hydrodynamic pressure. |
| **No vertical curvature (dome effect)** | Modern arch dams often curve vertically too (double curvature), increasing efficiency. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thin cylinder theory, despite its simplicity, is **still used today** for:

- **Preliminary sizing** of arch dams in feasibility reports.
- **Quick sanity checks** during detailed design (e.g., to verify FEA hoop stresses are in the right order of magnitude).
- **Educational illustration** of arch action.
- **Upper-zone design** where arch action truly dominates over cantilever action.
- **Small diversion weirs and check dams** in narrow gorges where a thin arch is structurally efficient and economical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Hoop Stress Equation

We will derive the canonical thin cylinder formula $\sigma = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ rigorously from first principles, step by step, so that no logical transition is skipped.

**Given:**
- A horizontal arch at depth $h$ below the reservoir water surface.
- Mean radius of the arch: $r$ (m).
- Uniform thickness of the arch: $t$ (m).
- Hydrostatic pressure: $p = \gamma_w \cdot h$ (kN/m┬▓).
- Material: linear elastic concrete, with the arch ends built into rigid abutments.

**To find:** Maximum circumferential (hoop) compressive stress in the arch.

---

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretize the curved arch as a thin ring.**

We conceptually slice the dam horizontally at the elevation of interest. The resulting ring is modeled as a thin cylindrical pressure vessel of radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$, closed (or treated as a full circular ring for a complete arch).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Free-body diagram: cut the ring into two halves.**

Imagine a vertical diametrical cut through the crown of the arch. The two halves are kept in equilibrium by the internal compressive thrust $H$ acting at the cut section.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate the resultant of radial pressure on the half-ring.**

Consider an elemental arc length $ds = r\,d\theta$ on the upstream face. The elemental force on this arc (per unit vertical height of dam) is:

$$dF = p \cdot ds = p \cdot r \, d\theta$$

The horizontal component of this elemental force (per unit height) is:

$$dF_x = dF \cdot \cos\theta = p \cdot r \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate over the half-circumference.**

The total horizontal force from water pressure on the half-ring (per unit height of dam) is:

$$F_x = \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} p \cdot r \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

$$F_x = p \cdot r \left[ \sin\theta \right]_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} = p \cdot r \left[ 1 - (-1) \right] = 2 p r$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply horizontal equilibrium.**

For the half-ring, sum of horizontal forces = 0. The internal thrust at the crown, $H$, balances the resultant water pressure:

$$H = F_x = 2 p r$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Note: This is for a FULL semi-circle.**

The full half-circumference corresponds to a central angle $\phi = 180┬░$ (a full semicircle). For a real arch dam, the central angle is typically much smaller (around $133┬░$ to $154┬░$). However, by symmetry and standard pressure-vessel theory, the hoop stress in a closed thin ring under uniform external pressure is independent of the central angle. We can verify this with the standard pressure-vessel formula.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Standard pressure-vessel hoop stress formula.**

For a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel (closed ring) subjected to uniform internal pressure $p$, radius $r$, and wall thickness $t$, the classical Lam├®-derived hoop stress is:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute the value of $p$.**

Since $p = \gamma_w \cdot h$, the hoop stress at any depth $h$ becomes:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{\gamma_w \cdot h \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Determine the thickness required for a target allowable stress.**

Rearranging for $t$:

$$t = \frac{p \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{\gamma_w \cdot h \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Verify units.**

- Numerator: $(\text{kN/m}^2)(\text{m}) = \text{kN/m}$
- Denominator: $\text{kN/m}^2$
- Result: $(\text{kN/m}) / (\text{kN/m}^2) = \text{m}$ Ō£ō

This confirms dimensional consistency.

---

## 3.2 Illustrative Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A thin arch dam is to be built in a narrow V-shaped valley. At an elevation **40 m below the reservoir surface**, the arch has a **mean radius of 75 m**. The allowable compressive stress in concrete is **8.0 MPa**. Using thin cylinder theory, determine:

(a) The required thickness of the arch at this elevation.
(b) The maximum hoop (compressive) stress if the arch thickness provided is **1.5 m**.
(c) Comment on the validity of the thin cylinder theory assumption.

**Given Data:**

$$\gamma_w = 9.81 \, \text{kN/m}^3, \quad h = 40 \, \text{m}, \quad r = 75 \, \text{m}, \quad \sigma_{allow} = 8.0 \, \text{MPa} = 8000 \, \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate hydrostatic pressure at the elevation.**

$$p = \gamma_w \cdot h = 9.81 \times 40 = 392.4 \, \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Part (a): Required thickness.**

$$t = \frac{p \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{392.4 \times 75}{8000}$$

$$t = \frac{29430}{8000} = 3.679 \, \text{m} \approx 3.68 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Part (b): Hoop stress at $t = 1.5$ m.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{392.4 \times 75}{1.5}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{29430}{1.5} = 19620 \, \text{kN/m}^2 = 19.62 \, \text{MPa}$$

This **far exceeds** the allowable 8.0 MPa, so a 1.5 m thickness is **unsafe**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Part (c): Validity check.**

$$\frac{t}{r} = \frac{3.68}{75} = 0.049 < 0.1 \quad Ō£ō$$

The thin-wall condition is satisfied, so the thin cylinder theory is **valid at this elevation**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Design Theories

| Parameter | Thin Cylinder Theory | Thick Cylinder Theory | Trial Load Method | Finite Element Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arch action considered? | Yes (only) | Yes (only) | Yes | Yes |
| Cantilever action considered? | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Foundation flexibility? | No | No | Yes (assumed) | Yes (modeled) |
| Temperature stresses? | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Complexity | Very low | Low | Very high | High |
| Computational effort | Minutes | Minutes | Weeks (manual) | Hours (computer) |
| Modern use | Preliminary | Preliminary | Legacy standard | Current standard |
| KTU exam relevance | **High** | Moderate | Mention only | Mention only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Classification of Arch Dams (Types)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ARCH DAMS] --> B[Classification by Curvature Geometry]
    A --> C[Classification by Plan Shape]

    B --> B1[Single Curvature - Arch in plan only]
    B --> B2[Double Curvature - Arch in plan AND vertical curve - Dome]

    B1 --> B11[Constant Radius Type]
    B1 --> B12[Variable Radius Type]

    C --> C1[Constant Center Arch Dam]
    C --> C2[Variable Center Arch Dam]
    C --> C3[Cupola or Dome Dam]

    B11 --> D1[Extrados and Intrados are concentric circles]
    B12 --> D2[Radius reduces with depth]

    C1 --> E1[Same center for all horizontal arches]
    C2 --> E2[Center shifts downstream as depth increases]
    C3 --> E3[Curved both horizontally and vertically]

    D1 --> F1[Suitable for U-shaped valleys]
    D2 --> F2[Suitable for V-shaped valleys]
    E2 --> F3[More efficient material usage]
    E3 --> F4[Highest structural efficiency]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Thin Cylinder Theory Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[RESERVOIR AND DAM SECTION]
        A[Hydrostatic Pressure p] -->|acts radially| B[Upstream Face of Arch]
    end

    subgraph A2[THIN ARCH RING]
        B --> C[Horizontal Arch Slice]
        C --> D[Thin Cylinder Model - radius r, thickness t]
    end

    subgraph A3[LOAD TRANSFER PATH]
        D -->|Hoop Compression| E[Crown Section]
        D -->|Thrust to Abutments| F[Canyon Walls - Abutments]
    end

    subgraph A4[OUTPUT STRESS]
        E --> G[Hoop Stress sigma = p x r divided by t]
        F --> H[Compressive Reaction at Abutments]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Methods for Design of Arch Dams (List Only)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[DESIGN METHODS FOR ARCH DAMS] --> N[Analytical Methods]
    M --> O[Numerical Methods]
    M --> P[Experimental Methods]
    M --> Q[Hybrid Methods]

    N --> N1[1 Thin Cylinder Theory - elementary, arch only]
    N --> N2[2 Thick Cylinder Theory - radial stress gradient]
    N --> N3[3 Cantilever Method - vertical slices only]
    N --> N4[4 Elastic Theory Methods - arch and cantilever split]

    O --> O1[5 Finite Element Method FEA - current industry standard]
    O --> O2[6 Finite Difference Method FDM]
    O --> O3[7 Boundary Element Method BEM]

    P --> P1[8 Structural Model Analysis - photoelasticity]
    P --> P2[9 Small-scale physical model testing in labs]
    P --> P3[10 In-situ stress and deformation monitoring]

    Q --> Q1[11 Trial Load Method - iterative arch plus cantilever]
    Q --> Q2[12 Membrane Theory of Shells]
    Q --> Q3[13 Limit State and Reliability Based Methods]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic ŌĆö Forces on a Thin Arch Ring


**Key features shown in the schematic:**

- Water pressure $p$ pushes inward radially.
- The arch is supported on both ends by **abutments** (canyon walls).
- The **crown** (top center) is the thinnest, most stressed section.
- The **abutments** receive the entire horizontal thrust from the arch.
